topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Understanding that written Japanese represents spoken language
topics.japaneseName: 書き言葉と話し言葉
topics.description: Understand that marks on a page can stand for spoken words and that reading means recovering language and meaning from print.
evidence: Point to print when asked where the words are on a page
Recognise that the same written word can be read the same way again
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show {{name}} a familiar label or short sentence and ask what part can be read aloud.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Hiragana sound-symbol mapping
topics.japaneseName: ひらがなの音と字形
topics.description: Recognise common hiragana characters and connect each character with its usual mora sound in familiar words.
evidence: Read basic hiragana characters aloud in isolation
Read simple familiar words written in hiragana
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to read a small set of basic hiragana and then read a few familiar hiragana words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing hiragana with correct stroke direction
topics.japaneseName: ひらがなの筆順
topics.description: Form hiragana legibly with attention to stroke order, direction, balance, and placement on the writing line.
evidence: Write basic hiragana in a recognisable form
Start and move strokes in the conventional direction for taught characters
topics.assessmentPrompt: Dictate several simple mora and observe whether {{name}} writes the hiragana legibly and in the taught direction.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Dakuten and handakuten in kana words
topics.japaneseName: 濁点と半濁点
topics.description: Recognise that dakuten and handakuten alter kana sounds and use them when reading and writing familiar words.
evidence: Distinguish pairs such as か/が and は/ぱ in reading
Add the correct sound mark when writing familiar dictated words
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to read and write familiar word pairs that differ by dakuten or handakuten.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Small tsu and geminate consonants
topics.japaneseName: 促音の小さい「っ」
topics.description: Read and write small っ/ッ as a cue that the following consonant is held or doubled in pronunciation.
evidence: Read familiar words containing small っ with a clear held consonant
Choose small っ when spelling familiar words that contain gemination
topics.assessmentPrompt: Dictate familiar words with and without gemination and ask {{name}} to write and read them back.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Long vowels in kana spelling
topics.japaneseName: 長音の表し方
topics.description: Recognise and spell long vowels in age-appropriate hiragana and katakana words, including the long-vowel mark in katakana.
evidence: Read words with long vowels without shortening them
Spell familiar long-vowel words using the taught kana convention
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to sort familiar words by short and long vowel sounds and write selected examples.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Katakana for loanwords, names, and sound words
topics.japaneseName: カタカナの使い道
topics.description: Understand that katakana is commonly used for loanwords, some names, sound words, emphasis, and technical labels.
evidence: Read basic katakana words in familiar contexts
Explain why a familiar word is written in katakana rather than hiragana
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show {{name}} a short set of hiragana and katakana words and ask which ones are names, loanwords, or sound words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading vertical and horizontal Japanese text
topics.japaneseName: 縦書きと横書き
topics.description: Follow the direction of Japanese text in both horizontal and vertical layouts, including page order and line movement.
evidence: Track print in the correct direction for horizontal text
Track print in the correct column order for vertical text
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} one horizontal and one vertical short text and ask them to point to where reading starts and continues.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using Japanese punctuation marks
topics.japaneseName: 句読点とかぎかっこ
topics.description: Use basic Japanese punctuation to mark sentence endings, pauses, quoted speech, and simple lists in age-appropriate writing.
evidence: Place 。 at the end of a complete sentence
Use 「」 around simple quoted speech when prompted
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to copy or compose two short sentences and add missing punctuation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing on genko yoshi manuscript paper
topics.japaneseName: 原稿用紙の使い方
topics.description: Place characters, punctuation, title, name, indentation, and line breaks appropriately on manuscript paper for school writing.
evidence: Write one character per square in ordinary manuscript layout
Place punctuation and paragraph starts using taught conventions
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to rewrite a short paragraph on genko yoshi and check placement of title, name, punctuation, and indentation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading grade 1 kanji in familiar words
topics.japaneseName: 一年生の漢字を読む
topics.description: Read grade 1 kanji when they appear in familiar words and short sentences, using context to choose likely readings.
evidence: Read a taught grade 1 kanji word in context
Use surrounding kana or sentence meaning to support a likely kanji reading
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to read short familiar sentences containing taught grade 1 kanji.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing grade 1 kanji in simple sentences
topics.japaneseName: 一年生の漢字を書く
topics.description: Write taught grade 1 kanji legibly in simple words and sentences, attending to approximate stroke order and balance.
evidence: Write taught grade 1 kanji from dictation or memory
Use taught kanji appropriately in short self-composed sentences
topics.assessmentPrompt: Dictate familiar words containing taught grade 1 kanji and ask {{name}} to use two of them in a sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using okurigana to read inflected words
topics.japaneseName: 送り仮名を手がかりに読む
topics.description: Use kana following a kanji stem as a clue to word form, inflection, and reading in familiar verbs and adjectives.
evidence: Notice that okurigana changes with verb or adjective form
Use okurigana to support the reading of a familiar inflected word
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show {{name}} familiar words with shared kanji stems and different okurigana and ask how the readings or forms differ.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Building vocabulary through kanji compounds
topics.japaneseName: 熟語で語彙を広げる
topics.description: Recognise that kanji can combine into words and that known characters can help infer or remember meanings of new compounds.
evidence: Identify a known kanji inside a compound word
Make a plausible meaning connection between a compound and its component kanji
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} several familiar compounds and ask which kanji they know inside each word and what clue it gives.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Looking up unknown words in a dictionary
topics.japaneseName: 辞書で言葉を調べる
topics.description: Use kana order, headwords, and entry information to find and understand unfamiliar words in age-appropriate dictionaries or digital tools.
evidence: Find a word using kana order or search input
Use the entry to choose a meaning that fits the sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to look up an unfamiliar word from a short text and explain which entry meaning fits.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Identifying who, where, when, and what happened in a story
topics.japaneseName: 物語の基本情報を捉える
topics.description: Identify the main participants, setting, time, and key events in an age-appropriate story read aloud or independently.
evidence: Name the main character or speaker in a story
Tell what happened first or most importantly in the story
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a short story, ask {{name}} who was in it, where it happened, and what happened.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Retelling a story in sequence
topics.japaneseName: 順序に沿って物語を話す
topics.description: Retell a story by selecting important events and presenting them in an order that preserves the plot.
evidence: Retell at least three key events in order
Use sequencing words or time cues to connect events
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to retell a familiar story to someone who has not just read it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Describing a character's feelings from actions and dialogue
topics.japaneseName: 行動や会話から気持ちを考える
topics.description: Infer a character's feelings from what the character says, does, notices, or changes across the story.
evidence: State a plausible feeling for a character
Point to an action or line of dialogue that supports the inference
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} what a character is feeling at a key moment and what in the text made them think so.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading poems aloud with rhythm
topics.japaneseName: 詩をリズムよく音読する
topics.description: Read short poems aloud with attention to mora rhythm, line breaks, repetition, and sound patterns.
evidence: Read a short poem aloud with clear line breaks
Notice repeated sounds, words, or rhythm patterns
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to prepare and read a short poem aloud, then identify one repeated sound or phrase.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Finding the main idea in an explanatory text
topics.japaneseName: 説明文の中心を捉える
topics.description: Identify what an explanatory text is mainly about and distinguish central information from supporting details.
evidence: State the topic or main idea of a short explanatory text
Choose details that directly support the main idea
topics.assessmentPrompt: After reading a short explanatory text, ask {{name}} to write the main idea in one sentence and mark two supporting details.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using headings, captions, diagrams, and tables
topics.japaneseName: 見出し・図表・キャプションを読む
topics.description: Use informational text features to predict structure, locate information, and connect prose with diagrams or tables.
evidence: Use a heading to predict what a section covers
Explain how a diagram, caption, or table supports the written explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} an informational page and ask them to use headings and a diagram to find an answer.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing complete sentences with appropriate punctuation
topics.japaneseName: 文として書く
topics.description: Write a complete sentence that expresses one thought and mark its boundary with appropriate Japanese punctuation.
evidence: Write a sentence that can be read as a complete thought
Use sentence-ending punctuation consistently in short writing
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write three sentences about a familiar experience and check whether each one is complete and punctuated.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sequencing sentences into a short paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 文を順序よく段落にする
topics.description: Arrange related sentences so that a short paragraph has a clear order, focus, and connection between ideas.
evidence: Group related sentences together
Use time, place, or reason cues to make sentence order clear
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a set of related sentences and ask them to arrange and revise them into a clear paragraph.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing an opinion with a reason
topics.japaneseName: 理由を付けて考えを書く
topics.description: State an opinion or preference and support it with at least one relevant reason or example.
evidence: State a clear opinion in writing
Give a reason that explains or supports the opinion
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write which of two choices they prefer and explain one reason.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for important details
topics.japaneseName: 大事なことを聞き取る
topics.description: Listen to a short explanation, story, or peer comment and identify the information most important for the purpose.
evidence: Answer literal questions after listening to a short talk
Retell one or two important details without repeating everything
topics.assessmentPrompt: Read or play a short explanation and ask {{name}} to tell the two most important things they heard.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Asking relevant questions after listening
topics.japaneseName: 聞いて質問する
topics.description: Ask questions that seek missing information, clarify meaning, or extend a peer's or teacher's explanation.
evidence: Ask a question connected to what was just said
Use the answer to improve understanding or continue the discussion
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a short peer presentation, ask {{name}} to write or say one question that would help them understand more.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Taking turns in discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いで順番に話す
topics.description: Participate in a small-group discussion by listening, taking turns, responding to others, and staying on the shared topic.
evidence: Wait for or negotiate a turn before speaking
Respond to a previous speaker rather than starting an unrelated topic
topics.assessmentPrompt: Observe {{name}} in a short group discussion and note whether their turns connect to the shared topic.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presenting a short report clearly
topics.japaneseName: 短い発表を分かりやすく行う
topics.description: Organise a short oral report with a clear topic, selected details, and audible delivery for classmates.
evidence: State the topic of a short presentation clearly
Include selected details in an order listeners can follow
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to give a one-minute report and check whether listeners can identify the topic and two details.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Monitoring whether a text makes sense while reading
topics.japaneseName: 読みながら分かったか確かめる
topics.description: Notice confusion while reading and use rereading, context, questions, or reference tools to repair understanding.
evidence: Pause and identify a word, sentence, or idea that is unclear
Choose a repair strategy such as rereading, asking, or looking up a word
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a slightly challenging paragraph and ask them to mark any confusing part and explain what they would do next.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using kanji components and radicals as clues
topics.japaneseName: 部首や構成要素を手がかりにする
topics.description: Notice recurring kanji components and radicals and use them as clues for meaning, lookup, grouping, or memory.
evidence: Identify a familiar component or radical in a taught kanji
Use the component as a clue for grouping, remembering, or looking up the character
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show {{name}} several taught kanji with a shared component and ask what the shared part might help them notice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Distinguishing on-yomi and kun-yomi patterns
topics.japaneseName: 音読みと訓読みの違い
topics.description: Recognise that a kanji may have different readings and that compounds and native Japanese words often cue different reading patterns.
evidence: Notice that the same kanji can be read differently in two familiar words
Use word context to choose a plausible reading for a taught kanji
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} familiar words sharing one kanji and ask how the reading changes between the words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Looking up kanji by reading, component, or shape
topics.japaneseName: 漢字を辞典で調べる
topics.description: Use available readings, components, radicals, stroke count, or digital handwriting input to look up an unknown kanji.
evidence: Choose a practical lookup route for an unknown kanji
Use the lookup result to confirm reading or meaning in context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to look up one unfamiliar kanji from a text and explain how they found the entry.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading prefecture-name kanji in social studies and maps
topics.japaneseName: 都道府県名の漢字を読む
topics.description: Read and recognise kanji used in prefecture names when they appear in maps, addresses, reports, and informational texts.
evidence: Read taught prefecture-name kanji in a map or list
Use geographic or word context to support recognition of a prefecture name
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show {{name}} a simple map or list of prefecture names and ask them to read selected names aloud.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using synonyms, antonyms, and related words
topics.japaneseName: 類義語・対義語・関連語
topics.description: Recognise relationships among words and use related words to choose more precise vocabulary in reading, discussion, and writing.
evidence: Group familiar words by similar or opposite meanings
Replace a general word with a more precise related word when prompted
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to sort word cards into similar, opposite, and related groups and use one replacement word in a sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recognising proverbs, idioms, and set expressions
topics.japaneseName: ことわざ・慣用句・決まった言い方
topics.description: Understand that some expressions carry meanings that cannot be fully predicted from each word and connect them to situations where they are used.
evidence: Explain the meaning of a familiar proverb or idiom in everyday language
Choose a situation where a familiar set expression would fit
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a familiar proverb or idiom and ask what it means and when someone might use it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Distinguishing polite and casual classroom language
topics.japaneseName: 丁寧な言葉とくだけた言葉
topics.description: Recognise that language choices shift with audience, setting, and purpose, especially between peer talk and classroom presentation or teacher interaction.
evidence: Identify whether an utterance is suitable for a friend, teacher, or presentation
Revise a casual phrase into a more classroom-appropriate form when prompted
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} short utterances and ask where each would be appropriate and how to make one more polite.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Noticing dialect and standard language variation
topics.japaneseName: 方言と共通語
topics.description: Notice that Japanese varies by region and situation and that standard language and dialect can each be appropriate in different contexts.
evidence: Identify a dialect word or expression as regionally marked when context is provided
Explain why a speaker might choose dialect or standard language in a situation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show {{name}} a short dialect example with context and ask what differs from standard language and why it might be used.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using connective words for sequence, contrast, and cause
topics.japaneseName: つなぎ言葉を使う
topics.description: Use connectives to show order, contrast, cause, and result so that sentences and paragraphs relate clearly.
evidence: Choose a connective that fits a relationship between two sentences
Revise a short paragraph so the connection between ideas is clearer
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} two related sentences and ask which connective best shows their relationship.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Subject and predicate awareness in Japanese sentences
topics.japaneseName: 主語と述語の関係
topics.description: Identify the main thing being talked about and what is said about it in simple Japanese sentences, including omitted subjects when context is clear.
evidence: Identify the subject or topic and predicate in a simple sentence
Explain when the subject is omitted but recoverable from context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to mark what a sentence is about and what is said about it in several short examples.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Adding modifiers to make sentences more specific
topics.japaneseName: 修飾語で詳しくする
topics.description: Use modifying words and phrases to add detail about people, things, actions, time, place, and manner in age-appropriate sentences.
evidence: Identify a word or phrase that modifies another part of a sentence
Add a modifier to make a plain sentence more precise
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a short plain sentence and ask them to add when, where, how, or what kind of detail.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using quotation in narrative writing
topics.japaneseName: 会話文を物語に入れる
topics.description: Use quoted speech to show what a character says and punctuate it so readers can distinguish narration from dialogue.
evidence: Use Japanese quotation marks around simple dialogue
Place a dialogue sentence where it helps show action or feeling
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to add one line of dialogue to a short story scene and punctuate it clearly.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing a diary entry about an experience
topics.japaneseName: 経験を日記に書く
topics.description: Write a short account of a personal experience with basic time order, concrete details, and a feeling or thought.
evidence: Describe a real experience in a few connected sentences
Include when or what happened and one feeling or thought
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write about something that happened today or yesterday and include what they thought or felt.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing descriptions with sensory details
topics.japaneseName: 様子が伝わるように書く
topics.description: Use observed details, sensory language, and modifiers to help a reader imagine a person, place, object, or event.
evidence: Include visible, audible, tactile, or emotional details in a description
Choose words that make the scene or object more specific
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to describe a familiar object or place so another person can imagine it clearly.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing an explanatory report from observations
topics.japaneseName: 観察したことを報告文に書く
topics.description: Organise observations or gathered facts into a short explanatory report with a clear topic and supporting details.
evidence: Select facts or observations that fit the report topic
Organise the facts into a readable order with a clear beginning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write a short report from notes about an observed plant, object, event, or survey result.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Summarising a text in writing
topics.japaneseName: 文章を要約する
topics.description: Write a concise summary that preserves the main idea and important supporting information while leaving out minor details.
evidence: State the main idea of the source text in the summary
Omit details that are interesting but not necessary
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to read a short informational text and write a three-sentence summary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising writing for clarity and audience
topics.japaneseName: 相手に伝わるように推敲する
topics.description: Reread a draft from the reader's perspective and revise wording, order, detail, or sentence boundaries to make meaning clearer.
evidence: Identify one place where a reader may be confused
Make a revision that clarifies wording, order, detail, or punctuation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} their own short draft and ask them to revise one part for a specific reader.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing two texts on the same topic
topics.japaneseName: 同じ話題の文章を比べる
topics.description: Compare how two informational texts treat the same topic, including differences in focus, detail, structure, or viewpoint.
evidence: State one similarity and one difference between two texts on the same topic
Use specific parts of each text to support the comparison
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} two short texts on the same topic and ask what each one focuses on.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Identifying an author's purpose and supporting evidence
topics.japaneseName: 筆者の目的と根拠を捉える
topics.description: Infer what an informational author wants readers to understand or consider and identify evidence used to support that purpose.
evidence: State the author's likely purpose in a short informational text
Point to facts, examples, or wording that support that purpose
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} what the author seems to want readers to understand and what evidence supports that answer.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Identifying themes and messages in stories
topics.japaneseName: 物語の主題を考える
topics.description: Infer a broader theme or message from characters, events, changes, and repeated ideas across a literary text.
evidence: State a plausible theme or message of a story
Use character change or key events as support for the theme
topics.assessmentPrompt: After reading a story, ask {{name}} what idea the story leaves them thinking about and what in the story supports that.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recognising narrator and point of view
topics.japaneseName: 語り手と視点
topics.description: Recognise who is telling a story or passage and how that viewpoint shapes what the reader knows, feels, or questions.
evidence: Identify the narrator or viewpoint in a short passage
Explain how the viewpoint affects what information is shown or hidden
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} who is telling a passage and what the reader knows because of that viewpoint.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing characters across stories
topics.japaneseName: 物語をまたいで人物を比べる
topics.description: Compare characters from different stories by actions, feelings, goals, relationships, or changes across events.
evidence: Name a meaningful similarity or difference between two characters
Support the comparison with events or dialogue from each story
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to compare two characters from different stories and cite one moment for each character.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Taking notes from sources
topics.japaneseName: 資料からメモを取る
topics.description: Record brief notes from a text, talk, diagram, or source without copying everything, keeping the notes tied to a question or purpose.
evidence: Select information relevant to a question or purpose
Write notes in short phrases or organised bullets rather than copying full passages
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a short source and a research question, then ask them to take notes for that question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Considering source purpose and reliability
topics.japaneseName: 資料の目的と確かさを考える
topics.description: Consider who made a source, why it was made, and whether it is suitable for answering a question or supporting a claim.
evidence: Identify a source creator, audience, or purpose when available
Explain why a source is or is not useful for a specific question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show {{name}} two sources on a topic and ask which is more useful for a given question and why.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Giving reasons and evidence in discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いで理由や根拠を述べる
topics.description: Support a spoken opinion or proposal with reasons, examples, or evidence that other participants can respond to.
evidence: State a reason after giving an opinion in discussion
Refer to a text, experience, observation, or source as support
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a small discussion, ask {{name}} to give an opinion and one reason others can evaluate.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Building agreement or disagreement in structured discussion
topics.japaneseName: 立場を比べて話し合う
topics.description: Compare positions in a structured discussion, respond to reasons, and work toward a decision, agreement, or clarified disagreement.
evidence: Restate another participant’s reason before agreeing or disagreeing
Help the group identify a shared decision or remaining point of disagreement
topics.assessmentPrompt: Observe {{name}} in a short structured discussion and note whether they respond to reasons rather than only repeating their position.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presenting with visual aids
topics.japaneseName: 資料を使って発表する
topics.description: Use a diagram, chart, note card, slide, or object to make an oral presentation clearer without reading everything aloud.
evidence: Choose a visual aid that supports the presentation topic
Refer to the visual aid while explaining rather than simply displaying it
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to give a short report using one visual aid and explain what the aid helps listeners understand.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing viewpoints while listening
topics.japaneseName: 聞きながら考えを比べる
topics.description: Listen to multiple speakers and compare their viewpoints, reasons, or evidence before responding.
evidence: State how two speakers’ views are similar or different
Use the comparison to ask a question or make a response
topics.assessmentPrompt: After two short peer comments, ask {{name}} how the ideas differ and what question they would ask next.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading aloud in meaningful phrase groups
topics.japaneseName: 意味のまとまりで音読する
topics.description: Read age-appropriate text aloud by grouping words and phrases so listeners can hear sentence meaning, pauses, and emphasis.
evidence: Pause at natural phrase or punctuation boundaries
Reread a sentence with improved phrasing after discussing its meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to read a short passage aloud twice, once before and once after marking natural phrase groups.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Navigating media texts and digital pages
topics.japaneseName: メディアやデジタル資料を読む
topics.description: Use menus, headings, links, captions, search boxes, and page structure to locate information in digital or media texts.
evidence: Use page features to locate information without reading every item linearly
Explain which feature helped them find the answer
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a simple digital or media page and ask them to find a specific answer using visible page features.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Analysing structure in explanatory texts
topics.japaneseName: 説明的文章の構成を分析する
topics.description: Analyse how an explanatory text is organised, including introduction, examples, comparison, cause-effect, and conclusion.
evidence: Describe how sections of an explanatory text are organised
Explain how the organisation helps the author develop the explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to map the sections of an explanatory text and explain why the author may have used that order.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evaluating argument and evidence
topics.japaneseName: 主張と根拠を評価する
topics.description: Evaluate whether an argument connects its claim, reasons, evidence, assumptions, and counterpoints clearly enough to be convincing.
evidence: Identify the main claim and supporting evidence in an argumentative text
Explain one strength or weakness in the connection between claim and evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a short argumentative passage and ask whether the evidence is sufficient for the claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Facilitating a discussion toward a shared purpose
topics.japaneseName: 目的に沿って話し合いを進行する
topics.description: Help a group discussion stay oriented to its purpose by summarising positions, inviting evidence, and clarifying next steps.
evidence: Summarise two positions in a discussion without distorting them
Ask a question or propose a next step that advances the shared purpose
topics.assessmentPrompt: Observe {{name}} in a short group discussion and ask them to summarise the options and next step.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Entering classical Japanese through familiar forms and rhythm
topics.japaneseName: 古典の言葉とリズムに親しむ
topics.description: Recognise classical Japanese as historically related to modern Japanese and use sound, rhythm, annotation, and familiar words to enter short classical passages.
evidence: Read a short classical passage aloud with support
Identify a familiar word, phrase, or cultural reference that connects the passage to modern Japanese
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a short annotated classical passage and ask what sounds, words, or ideas feel familiar or different.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing an argumentative essay with counterpoints
topics.japaneseName: 反論を踏まえて意見文を書く
topics.description: Write an argument that states a claim, supports it with evidence, acknowledges another view, and explains why the claim remains reasonable.
evidence: State a clear claim and support it with relevant evidence
Address a counterpoint or limitation without losing the essay focus
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write a short argument on a school issue, including one counterpoint and a response.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading public texts and media critically
topics.japaneseName: 公共的な文章とメディアを批判的に読む
topics.description: Read public-facing texts and media by examining audience, purpose, evidence, framing, and the relationship between verbal and visual information.
evidence: Identify target audience and purpose in a public text or media item
Explain how wording, evidence, layout, or visuals shape reader interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a public notice, article, or media page and ask how it frames the issue for its intended audience.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Connecting language culture across eras
topics.japaneseName: 時代をこえた言語文化のつながり
topics.description: Connect modern Japanese expression with older literary, rhetorical, or cultural forms, noticing continuity and change in language culture.
evidence: Identify one continuity or contrast between modern and older Japanese expression
Explain how cultural context changes interpretation of a phrase or passage
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to compare a short modern passage with an older expression or literary excerpt and explain one connection.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Analysing logic and rhetoric in argumentative prose
topics.japaneseName: 論理と修辞を分析する
topics.description: Analyse how argumentative prose uses definitions, assumptions, evidence, examples, concessions, and rhetorical choices to persuade readers.
evidence: Map a text’s claim, reasons, evidence, and concessions
Explain how a rhetorical choice affects the force or fairness of the argument
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} an argumentative essay and ask them to map its logic and identify one rhetorical move.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Interpreting symbolism and narrative technique
topics.japaneseName: 象徴と表現技法を解釈する
topics.description: Interpret how imagery, symbolism, narrative distance, silence, repetition, and structure contribute to literary meaning.
evidence: Identify a literary technique and connect it to interpretation
Support an interpretation with multiple textual details rather than plot summary alone
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to explain how one repeated image, silence, or structural choice changes the meaning of a literary passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical grammar and kanbun reading foundations
topics.japaneseName: 古典文法と漢文訓読の基礎
topics.description: Use basic classical grammar, annotation, and kanbun reading marks to understand short classical or Sino-Japanese passages with support.
evidence: Use annotation or reading marks to parse a short classical or kanbun sentence
Explain a basic difference between the classical form and modern Japanese equivalent
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} an annotated classical or kanbun sentence and ask them to explain its rough meaning and one form clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing a research synthesis argument
topics.japaneseName: 資料を統合して論じる
topics.description: Synthesize multiple sources into an argument that distinguishes source positions, evaluates evidence, and develops an original claim.
evidence: Integrate information from multiple sources without flattening their differences
Use source evidence to support an original claim and acknowledge limitations
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write a short synthesis argument using two or three sources on a shared issue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparative literary and cultural interpretation
topics.japaneseName: 文学と文化を比較して解釈する
topics.description: Compare literary works, eras, genres, or cultural contexts to develop an interpretation that accounts for both textual detail and historical perspective.
evidence: Compare two works or contexts using specific textual details
Explain how historical or cultural context changes the interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to compare two literary passages or eras and explain how context changes what the text means.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing source claims on the same topic
topics.japaneseName: 同じ話題の資料の主張を比べる
topics.description: Compare two or more informational sources on the same topic by identifying each source’s claim, evidence, purpose, and useful limits.
evidence: State how two sources make different claims or emphasize different evidence
Explain which source is more useful for a particular question and why
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} two short sources on the same topic and ask which source better answers a research question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Asking evidence-focused questions in discussion
topics.japaneseName: 根拠に着目して質問する
topics.description: Ask questions that clarify a speaker’s evidence, assumptions, examples, or missing information during a structured discussion.
evidence: Ask a question that requests evidence or clarification rather than a new topic
Use the answer to refine the group’s understanding of the issue
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a short discussion, ask {{name}} to pose one question that tests the evidence for a classmate’s claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Interpreting abstract kanji compounds in school texts
topics.japaneseName: 学習語彙の抽象的な漢語を読む
topics.description: Use kanji meanings, components, context, and subject-area knowledge to infer abstract Sino-Japanese compounds in upper-elementary texts.
evidence: Infer the approximate meaning of an unfamiliar compound from known kanji and context
Explain how one kanji contributes to the word’s meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Select several abstract compounds from a school text and ask {{name}} to infer meaning from components and context.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Summarising with selected textual evidence
topics.japaneseName: 根拠を選んで要約する
topics.description: Write a concise summary that preserves the main idea and includes selected evidence without copying unnecessary details.
evidence: Include the central point and omit minor details in a written summary
Select one or two textual details that support the summary
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to summarise an explanatory passage in several sentences and underline the details they chose as support.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Planning inquiry with multiple sources
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料を使う調査計画を立てる
topics.description: Plan an inquiry by forming a focused question, deciding what information is needed, selecting source types, and tracking notes by source.
evidence: Turn a broad topic into a focused inquiry question
Choose at least two source types and explain what each may contribute
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to plan a small research task with a question, source list, and note-taking categories.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Structuring an opinion with counterclaim awareness
topics.japaneseName: 反対意見を意識して意見文を構成する
topics.description: Organise an opinion text so that the claim, reasons, evidence, possible counterclaim, and conclusion are clear to the reader.
evidence: Use paragraph order to separate claim, reasons, evidence, and conclusion
Mention another possible view and explain why the writer’s view still holds
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to revise an opinion paragraph into a short composition with claim, evidence, counterclaim, and conclusion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presenting inquiry findings with source support
topics.japaneseName: 資料にもとづいて調査結果を発表する
topics.description: Present inquiry findings by stating the question, explaining source-based evidence, using visual support, and answering clarifying questions.
evidence: Present a research question and source-supported finding clearly
Use a visual aid or note card to help listeners follow the evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to give a brief inquiry presentation and answer one question about a source used.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracing related themes across literary works
topics.japaneseName: 複数の文学作品に共通する主題を読む
topics.description: Compare literary works by tracing related themes, character choices, settings, and authorial messages across texts.
evidence: Identify a theme that appears in more than one literary work
Use details from each work to explain a similarity or difference in treatment
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to compare two stories or poems and explain how each treats a related theme.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising for cohesion, register, and audience
topics.japaneseName: つながり・文体・相手を意識して推敲する
topics.description: Revise writing by improving sentence connections, word choice, register, paragraph flow, and suitability for audience and purpose.
evidence: Revise connectives or paragraph order to make the text easier to follow
Adjust word choice or register for audience and purpose
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a draft and ask for two revisions: one for cohesion and one for audience or register.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Matching register to audience and purpose
topics.japaneseName: 相手と目的に応じて文体を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose language formality, word choice, and expression style according to audience, medium, relationship, and communicative purpose.
evidence: Explain why one expression fits an audience or medium better than another
Revise a casual expression into a more suitable school or public expression
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a casual note and ask them to revise it for a teacher, classmate, or public notice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Analysing sentence constituents and modifiers
topics.japaneseName: 文の成分と修飾関係を分析する
topics.description: Analyse how phrases and modifiers relate inside a Japanese sentence, including subject-predicate relations, modification, quotation, and clause boundaries.
evidence: Mark which phrase modifies another phrase in a sentence
Explain how changing a modifier changes the meaning or emphasis of the sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to annotate a sentence from a reading passage and explain one modifier relationship.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Analysing narrative structure and viewpoint
topics.japaneseName: 物語の構成と視点を分析する
topics.description: Analyse how narrative order, viewpoint, setting, dialogue, and omitted information shape the reader’s interpretation of a story.
evidence: Explain how viewpoint changes what the reader knows or feels
Describe how narrative order affects interpretation of a character or event
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to explain how a story’s viewpoint or order affects the reader’s view of one event.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Structuring explanatory writing from evidence
topics.japaneseName: 根拠にもとづいて説明文を構成する
topics.description: Write an explanatory text that introduces a topic, orders evidence logically, uses examples or comparisons, and concludes with the main insight.
evidence: Organise evidence into a clear explanatory order
Use examples, comparison, or cause-effect language to support explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write a short explanation from notes, then label the role of each paragraph.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using abstract vocabulary in academic reading and discussion
topics.japaneseName: 抽象語彙を読解と話し合いで使う
topics.description: Understand and use abstract vocabulary for concepts, relationships, judgment, comparison, and reasoning in secondary-school texts.
evidence: Explain an abstract word from context and kanji components
Use an abstract word accurately in discussion or written explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to define several abstract words from a passage and use one in an explanation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Synthesising viewpoints during discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いで複数の意見を整理・統合する
topics.description: Synthesize multiple viewpoints in discussion by naming agreements, disagreements, evidence gaps, and possible shared conclusions.
evidence: Summarise more than one viewpoint and identify a point of agreement or disagreement
Propose a shared conclusion or next question based on the discussion
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a group discussion, ask {{name}} to pause and synthesise the positions before the group decides a next step.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing literary interpretations with evidence
topics.japaneseName: 根拠にもとづいて解釈を比べる
topics.description: Compare different interpretations of a literary work by checking each interpretation against textual evidence and plausible inference.
evidence: State two possible interpretations of a passage or character choice
Use textual evidence to explain which interpretation is better supported
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} two interpretations of a story passage and ask which is better supported by the text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Identifying framing and bias in media texts
topics.japaneseName: メディアの構成と偏りを読む
topics.description: Identify how media texts frame events through selection, ordering, wording, visuals, source choice, omission, and assumed audience.
evidence: Identify one framing choice in a media text
Explain how source selection, wording, or visuals may influence reader interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} two media treatments of the same issue and ask how each frames the issue differently.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recognising classical allusion in modern texts
topics.japaneseName: 現代文に生きる古典的表現を読む
topics.description: Recognise when modern texts draw on classical phrases, proverbs, idioms, kanbun-derived expressions, or older cultural references.
evidence: Identify a classical or older expression used in a modern text
Explain how the allusion or idiom adds meaning beyond literal wording
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to find an older expression or allusion in a modern passage and explain its effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Formulating inquiry questions for contemporary Japanese
topics.japaneseName: 現代の課題に向けた探究課題を立てる
topics.description: Formulate inquiry questions about contemporary language, society, literature, or media that can be investigated through texts and evidence.
evidence: Turn a broad issue into a researchable inquiry question
Explain what kinds of texts or evidence would be needed to investigate the question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to refine a broad contemporary issue into an inquiry question and evidence plan.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Genre and rhetorical inheritance in language culture
topics.japaneseName: ジャンルと修辞の継承を読む
topics.description: Notice how genres and rhetorical habits carry across periods, including narrative openings, poetic compression, essay form, allusion, and public style.
evidence: Identify a genre convention or rhetorical pattern in a text
Explain a continuity or transformation across modern and older expression
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to compare a modern passage with an older or genre-linked expression and identify a rhetorical continuity.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence-based presentation and discussion of contemporary issues
topics.japaneseName: 根拠にもとづいて現代の課題を発表・討議する
topics.description: Present and discuss a contemporary issue using evidence, clear framing, response to questions, and attention to audience understanding.
evidence: Present a contemporary issue with a claim, evidence, and audience-conscious explanation
Respond to a question by referring to evidence or clarifying the claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to present a short evidence-based position on a contemporary issue and handle one challenge question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracking definitions and conceptual distinctions in logical prose
topics.japaneseName: 論理的文章の定義と概念区分を追う
topics.description: Track how logical prose defines terms, divides concepts, qualifies claims, and distinguishes similar ideas.
evidence: Identify a key term and explain how the text defines or narrows it
Explain why a conceptual distinction matters to the argument
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a logical prose passage and ask them to map one definition and one conceptual distinction.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing a short literary criticism essay
topics.japaneseName: 短い文学批評を書く
topics.description: Write a literary interpretation that states an interpretive claim, analyses textual evidence, and explains how technique contributes to meaning.
evidence: State an interpretive claim about a literary passage
Analyse textual evidence rather than only retelling plot
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write a short critical paragraph about a literary technique and its meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing comparative commentary on classical and modern expression
topics.japaneseName: 古典と現代の表現を比較して論じる
topics.description: Write commentary that compares classical and modern expression, explaining linguistic, cultural, rhetorical, or interpretive continuity and difference.
evidence: Compare a classical expression with a modern equivalent or echo
Explain how linguistic or cultural context changes interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to write a brief commentary comparing a classical phrase with a modern text that echoes it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presenting a public-issue research argument
topics.japaneseName: 公共的な課題について調査にもとづき発表する
topics.description: Present a public-issue argument that synthesizes sources, anticipates questions, addresses limits, and uses spoken and visual structure effectively.
evidence: Present a source-based argument with claim, evidence, qualification, and conclusion
Answer a question by clarifying evidence, limits, or implications
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to present a source-based argument on a public issue and respond to one evidence-focused question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for story sequence and speaker cues
topics.japaneseName: 話の順序と話し手の合図を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen to a short story, classroom talk, or explanation and notice order words, repeated ideas, speaker emphasis, and what happened first or next.
evidence: Retell two or three events in the order heard
Point out a word, pause, or repeated idea that helped them follow the talk
topics.assessmentPrompt: Read a short story aloud and ask {{name}} what happened first, what happened next, and what listening clue helped.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Oral recount with one clarifying question
topics.japaneseName: 聞いたことを話し直し質問する
topics.description: Recount a short heard text or classmate comment in order and ask one clarifying question about missing or uncertain information.
evidence: Recount the main events or points in a short oral text
Ask one question that targets unclear information
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have {{name}} listen to a classmate report, restate it briefly, and ask one clarifying question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for the main point in an explanation
topics.japaneseName: 説明の中心を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen to a short explanation and identify its main point, supporting detail, and what the listener is expected to understand or do.
evidence: State the main point of a short oral explanation
Separate one important detail from a less important detail
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a short oral explanation and ask for the central point and one supporting detail.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Taking listening notes with key words
topics.japaneseName: 大事な言葉を聞き取ってメモする
topics.description: Take simple notes while listening by recording key words, names, numbers, reasons, and questions rather than full sentences.
evidence: Record key words from a short talk without trying to copy every word
Use the notes to answer a question about the talk
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have {{name}} listen to a short report, take key-word notes, and answer questions from those notes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for claim, reasons, and evidence
topics.japaneseName: 主張・理由・根拠を聞き分ける
topics.description: Listen to an opinion or argument and distinguish the speaker claim, reasons, evidence, and any point that needs clarification.
evidence: Identify the speaker claim and at least one reason or evidence item
Ask or note one question about the strength or clarity of the evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play or read a short opinion statement and ask {{name}} to identify the claim, reasons, evidence, and one question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Summarising discussion decisions and open issues
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いの決定と課題をまとめる
topics.description: Listen to a group discussion and summarise what was decided, what remains unresolved, and who will do what next.
evidence: Summarise the decision and one unresolved issue after a discussion
Identify the next action or responsible person when stated
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a short group discussion, ask {{name}} to summarise the decision, unresolved issue, and next action.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Inferring speaker intent from wording and context
topics.japaneseName: 言い方と文脈から意図を推測する
topics.description: Infer a speaker intent, stance, or unstated request by using wording, relationship, situation, and what has already been said.
evidence: Explain what a speaker likely wants the listener to understand or do
Cite a wording or context clue that supports the inference
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a short dialogue and ask what the speaker implies and which clue supports that reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evaluating how a discussion develops
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いの展開を評価する
topics.description: Listen to a discussion and evaluate whether the exchange stayed on purpose, used evidence, responded to prior points, and moved toward a next step.
evidence: Identify a turn that advanced or diverted the discussion
Explain how evidence, questions, or summaries affected the discussion flow
topics.assessmentPrompt: After observing a short discussion, ask {{name}} which turn helped the group most and why.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing argument stance across speakers
topics.japaneseName: 複数の話し手の立場を比べて聞く
topics.description: Listen to two or more speakers and compare their claims, stance, evidence, assumptions, and response to opposing views.
evidence: Compare two speaker stances and the evidence each uses
Identify one assumption or counterpoint in the spoken exchange
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a short debate or panel exchange and ask how two speakers differ in stance and evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening to public speech and media for framing
topics.japaneseName: 公共的な発話とメディアの構成を聞く
topics.description: Listen to public speech or media commentary and identify topic, audience, purpose, evidence, framing, and likely intended effect.
evidence: State the audience, purpose, and main framing of a public spoken text
Explain how wording, ordering, or evidence shapes listener interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to listen to a short public speech or media commentary and explain its framing and intended audience.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Critiquing logic in an oral presentation
topics.japaneseName: 発表の論理を批評して聞く
topics.description: Listen to an oral presentation and critique definition, claim, evidence, assumption, concession, and rhetorical fairness.
evidence: Map a speaker claim, support, and assumption from an oral presentation
Explain one strength or weakness in the logic or rhetoric
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a short presentation, ask {{name}} to map its argument and identify one logical or rhetorical issue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Synthesising a panel or source-based oral exchange
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料・発言を統合して聞く
topics.description: Listen to a panel, seminar, or source-based exchange and synthesize positions, evidence, unresolved questions, and implications for an argument.
evidence: Synthesize multiple spoken positions into a structured summary
Connect spoken evidence to a written or research-based argument
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a panel-style exchange and ask for a synthesis of positions, evidence, unresolved issues, and implications.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Asking for help when listening is unclear
topics.japaneseName: 聞き取れないときに助けを求める
topics.description: Notice when classroom speech is unclear and use a simple question, repetition request, or gesture to get help.
evidence: Signal when a direction or story detail is unclear
Ask for repetition or help using a classroom-appropriate expression
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a classroom instruction task, observe whether {{name}} notices confusion and asks for help.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rereading or retelling to check understanding
topics.japaneseName: 読み直し・話し直しで理解を確かめる
topics.description: Use rereading, retelling, or looking back at pictures and sentences to check whether a story or explanation makes sense.
evidence: Reread or retell when a story event or sentence does not make sense
Correct a misunderstanding after checking the text or talk again
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to retell a story, identify one uncertain part, and check it by rereading or listening again.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing a strategy for an unknown word
topics.japaneseName: 分からない言葉への対応を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose among rereading, context clues, kanji clues, asking someone, or using a dictionary when an unknown word blocks understanding.
evidence: Name a strategy used to approach an unknown word
Explain whether the strategy helped and what meaning was inferred
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a passage with an unfamiliar word and ask them to choose and explain a strategy.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Keeping a question and note log while learning
topics.japaneseName: 疑問とメモを学習に生かす
topics.description: Track questions, useful notes, and follow-up points while reading, listening, or researching so they can be used in discussion or writing.
evidence: Record questions and notes in a way that can be used later
Return to a note or question during discussion or writing
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to keep a short note log during a reading or listening task and use one note in a follow-up answer.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Setting purpose and success criteria for a language task
topics.japaneseName: 目的と達成の目安を決める
topics.description: Before reading, listening, writing, or discussion, set a purpose and identify what successful understanding or expression should include.
evidence: State the purpose of a language task before starting
Name one criterion that will show whether the task was successful
topics.assessmentPrompt: Before a discussion or reading task, ask {{name}} to state the purpose and one success criterion, then revisit it afterward.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on discussion and presentation contribution
topics.japaneseName: 話し合い・発表への参加を振り返る
topics.description: Reflect on how a contribution, question, evidence choice, or presentation choice helped or limited audience understanding and group progress.
evidence: Identify one contribution that helped a discussion or presentation
Name one change that would improve clarity, evidence, or audience response next time
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a presentation or group discussion, ask {{name}} to identify one strength and one next improvement.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using criteria to revise and reflect on writing
topics.japaneseName: 観点を使って推敲し振り返る
topics.description: Use explicit criteria such as purpose, evidence, order, wording, and audience to revise a text and explain the effect of the revision.
evidence: Select a revision based on a stated criterion
Explain how the revision improves purpose, evidence, order, wording, or audience fit
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a draft and criteria list; ask them to revise one part and explain the reason.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Maintaining an inquiry evidence log
topics.japaneseName: 探究の根拠ログを作る
topics.description: Maintain an evidence log that records source, claim, evidence, question, reliability concern, and how the evidence may be used.
evidence: Record source, evidence, and question information in a reusable form
Explain why one piece of evidence is useful or limited
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to build a short evidence log from two sources and identify one useful and one limited item.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Self-reviewing argument strength and counterpoints
topics.japaneseName: 主張の強さと反論対応を自己評価する
topics.description: Review an argument by checking claim clarity, evidence relevance, counterpoint handling, wording, and reader or listener response.
evidence: Identify a strength and weakness in the learner argument
Revise or plan a revision that improves evidence or counterpoint response
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to self-review an argument using claim, evidence, counterpoint, and wording criteria.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on source bias and inquiry framing
topics.japaneseName: 資料の偏りと探究の立て方を振り返る
topics.description: Reflect on how source selection, media framing, search terms, and assumptions shape an inquiry question and possible conclusions.
evidence: Explain how a source or search choice may bias inquiry direction
Revise an inquiry question or source plan to reduce a limitation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to review an inquiry plan and identify one framing bias and one correction.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on alternative interpretations
topics.japaneseName: 別の解釈を比較して振り返る
topics.description: Reflect on literary or logical interpretation by comparing alternatives, evidence, assumptions, and what would change the interpretation.
evidence: State an alternative interpretation and the evidence that could support it
Explain why one interpretation is stronger or what evidence would change the judgment
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to compare two interpretations and explain which is better supported and why.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Portfolio reflection on language growth
topics.japaneseName: 言語活動の成長をポートフォリオで振り返る
topics.description: Use a portfolio of writing, reading notes, presentations, discussions, and source work to explain growth, remaining challenges, and next language goals.
evidence: Select evidence from multiple language activities to show growth
Set a next goal based on patterns across the portfolio
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to choose portfolio evidence and write a reflection on growth, remaining challenge, and next goal.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading grade 2 kanji in word families
topics.japaneseName: 二年生漢字を語のまとまりで読む
topics.description: Read newly taught grade 2 kanji in familiar word families and notice how the same character contributes meaning across related words.
evidence: Read grade 2 kanji in familiar words using kana and sentence context
Group words that share a kanji and explain the shared meaning clue
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} several familiar grade 2 kanji words and ask them to read and group related words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing grade 2 kanji in word context
topics.japaneseName: 語の中で二年生漢字を書く
topics.description: Write taught grade 2 kanji in meaningful words and short sentences while attending to character shape, stroke order, and word meaning.
evidence: Write taught grade 2 kanji accurately within familiar words
Choose the correct kanji for a word from meaning and context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to complete short sentences with grade 2 kanji words and explain one choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Grouping words by meaning field
topics.japaneseName: 意味のまとまりで言葉を集める
topics.description: Group words by meaning field, school topic, character relationship, or use situation to expand vocabulary beyond isolated memorisation.
evidence: Group words by meaning or use situation
Add a new word to a group and explain why it belongs
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to sort words from a story or explanation into meaning groups and add one related word.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Grouping kanji by component and meaning
topics.japaneseName: 部品と意味で漢字をまとめる
topics.description: Group grade 3 kanji by recurring components, radicals, shape patterns, and meaning clues to support reading and memory.
evidence: Identify a recurring component in several kanji
Explain how a component, radical, or shape feature may support meaning or memory
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} several grade 3 kanji and ask them to group them by component or meaning clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using on-yomi and kun-yomi word families
topics.japaneseName: 音訓の語群を手がかりにする
topics.description: Use repeated on-yomi and kun-yomi patterns across word families to read and understand newly encountered kanji words.
evidence: Identify whether a familiar kanji reading is likely on-yomi or kun-yomi in context
Use a known word family to read or infer a new compound
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to compare words sharing one kanji and explain how the reading changes by word type.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing dictionary headwords for compound words
topics.japaneseName: 熟語の見出し語を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose useful dictionary headwords for compound words, inflected words, and words with okurigana when checking meaning or reading.
evidence: Decide whether to look up the whole word, kanji compound, or base form
Use a dictionary result to confirm reading or meaning in context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} unknown words from a passage and ask which dictionary entry they would search and why.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing among multiple kanji readings from context
topics.japaneseName: 文脈から複数の読みを選ぶ
topics.description: Use word type, okurigana, compound structure, sentence context, and prior word knowledge to choose among multiple readings of a kanji.
evidence: Explain why one reading fits a word better than another
Use okurigana or compound structure as a clue to reading
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} kanji words with multiple possible readings and ask which reading fits each sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading kanji-dense words in informational texts
topics.japaneseName: 説明文の漢字語を読む
topics.description: Read kanji-dense words in explanations, diagrams, tables, and captions by using context, known compounds, and source-domain vocabulary.
evidence: Identify kanji words that carry key information in an explanatory text
Use diagram, caption, or paragraph context to infer a kanji word meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to mark key kanji words in an explanatory text and explain how they support the main idea.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing precise words for description and explanation
topics.japaneseName: 説明や描写に合う言葉を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose more precise nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs for description or explanation based on nuance, context, and reader understanding.
evidence: Replace a vague word with a more precise word and explain the effect
Choose wording that fits the subject, reader, and sentence context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a draft sentence with vague wording and ask for a more precise revision and explanation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recognising jukugo formation patterns
topics.japaneseName: 熟語の成り立ちを読む
topics.description: Recognise common compound formation patterns such as modifier-head, synonym pair, contrast pair, verb-object, and subject-predicate compounds.
evidence: Classify a familiar compound by its relation between kanji
Use compound structure to infer approximate meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to sort several kanji compounds by relationship type and infer one unfamiliar compound.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using context to resolve multiple meanings
topics.japaneseName: 文脈で多義語の意味を選ぶ
topics.description: Use sentence, paragraph, genre, and domain context to decide which meaning of a polysemous word or kanji compound fits.
evidence: Explain which meaning of a word fits a context and why
Use surrounding words or topic knowledge to rule out another meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} sentences using the same word in different meanings and ask how context changes interpretation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Interpreting subject-area Sino-Japanese words
topics.japaneseName: 教科で使う漢語を読む
topics.description: Interpret high-utility Sino-Japanese words in school subjects by using kanji meaning, compound structure, and explanatory context.
evidence: Explain a subject-area word using kanji and context clues
Use a learned academic word accurately in a short explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to explain several subject-area words from a science, social studies, or Japanese text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using Joyo and Kyoiku kanji resources for independent reading
topics.japaneseName: 常用漢字・配当漢字を手がかりに読む
topics.description: Use grade allocation, Joyo kanji references, dictionary lookup, and context to read unfamiliar kanji words beyond a single taught list.
evidence: Use a kanji or grade-allocation reference to check reading or meaning
Explain how context and reference lookup together support independent reading
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} an unfamiliar kanji word in context and ask them to use a reference and explain the result.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Building domain-specific vocabulary during inquiry
topics.japaneseName: 調査で分野の語彙を増やす
topics.description: Build a vocabulary set for an inquiry topic by recording terms, readings, meanings, source contexts, and relationships among terms.
evidence: Record topic-specific terms with reading, meaning, and source context
Use two or more recorded terms accurately in an inquiry explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to create a short vocabulary log for an inquiry topic and use several terms in a summary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing words for public audience and register
topics.japaneseName: 相手と場に合う語彙を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose words that match public audience, relationship, medium, and purpose, distinguishing casual, classroom, public, and formal wording.
evidence: Revise wording to fit a public or classroom audience
Explain why one word choice fits the medium or relationship better
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to revise a casual explanation into a notice, presentation, or teacher-facing text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Analysing kanji as morphemes in secondary texts
topics.japaneseName: 中学校教材で漢字を形態素として分析する
topics.description: Analyse kanji as meaning-bearing morphemes in academic words, compounds, nominalisations, and argument vocabulary in lower-secondary texts.
evidence: Break an academic compound into meaningful kanji or morpheme parts
Explain how morpheme analysis helps infer meaning in context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to break down several academic compounds from a secondary text and explain the meaning clues.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using abstract nouns in explanation
topics.japaneseName: 説明で抽象名詞を使う
topics.description: Understand and use abstract nouns that name concepts, categories, relations, causes, changes, and viewpoints in explanatory texts.
evidence: Explain an abstract noun from context in an explanatory passage
Use an abstract noun accurately in an oral or written explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to define and use several abstract nouns from an explanatory text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recognising kanji across fonts, handwriting, and variants
topics.japaneseName: 字体・書体・手書きの違いを認識する
topics.description: Recognise the same kanji across print fonts, handwriting styles, common shape variation, and display contexts without confusing it with another character.
evidence: Match the same kanji across print and handwritten styles
Explain which shape features are essential and which vary by style
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show {{name}} the same kanji in several styles and ask what stays stable across the forms.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recognising Sino-Japanese and classical-derived vocabulary
topics.japaneseName: 漢語・古典由来語を読む
topics.description: Recognise how Sino-Japanese compounds, classical-derived expressions, and idioms carry historical or formal meaning in secondary texts.
evidence: Identify a Sino-Japanese or classical-derived expression in a text
Explain the formal, historical, or idiomatic effect of the expression
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to find a formal or classical-derived expression and explain how it affects tone or meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading classical kanji-kana orthography with support
topics.japaneseName: 古典の漢字仮名交じり表記を読む
topics.description: Use annotation, familiar kanji, kana conventions, and historical context to read short classical or older kanji-kana passages with support.
evidence: Identify familiar kanji or expressions in a short classical passage
Use annotation or context to explain the rough meaning of an older expression
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} an annotated classical passage and ask which written clues support a rough reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using argument and stance vocabulary
topics.japaneseName: 主張・立場を表す語彙を使う
topics.description: Understand and use vocabulary for claim, basis, counterargument, concession, stance, assumption, and conclusion in argument reading and writing.
evidence: Identify argument vocabulary and explain its function in a text
Use stance or counterargument vocabulary accurately in a written argument
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to revise an argumentative paragraph using precise claim, evidence, and counterargument terms.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Interpreting Joyo kanji in public and media texts
topics.japaneseName: 公共的文章・メディアの常用漢字を読む
topics.description: Interpret Joyo kanji and kanji compounds in public notices, articles, essays, media pages, and policy-like texts with attention to register and audience.
evidence: Explain how kanji compounds contribute to formal or public register
Use context and reference tools to interpret a public-text kanji word
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a public-facing text and ask how key kanji compounds shape tone and meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Public-discourse and rhetorical vocabulary
topics.japaneseName: 公共的な文章の語彙と修辞語
topics.description: Understand and use vocabulary for public issues, rhetoric, framing, audience, evidence, and viewpoint in contemporary Japanese texts.
evidence: Explain how a rhetorical or public-discourse word frames an issue
Use public-discourse vocabulary in an inquiry question, summary, or presentation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to identify framing words in a public text and reuse several in a short inquiry summary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Critical terminology for logical and literary interpretation
topics.japaneseName: 論理的・文学的解釈の批評語彙
topics.description: Use critical terms for definition, premise, implication, symbolism, viewpoint, technique, and interpretation in logical and literary analysis.
evidence: Use critical terminology to explain a logical or literary interpretation
Distinguish everyday wording from analytical terminology in an essay or discussion
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to revise an interpretation paragraph by adding precise critical terminology.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using kanbun marks and kanji order to support reading
topics.japaneseName: 返り点と漢字の配列を手がかりに漢文を読む
topics.description: Use kanbun reading marks, kanji order, annotation, and familiar compounds to support the reading of short kanbun passages.
evidence: Use a reading mark or annotation to explain reading order
Connect a kanbun expression to a modern Japanese meaning or idiom
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give {{name}} a short annotated kanbun passage and ask them to explain a reading mark and one meaning clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Synthesising disciplinary lexicons across sources
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料の専門語彙を統合する
topics.description: Synthesize disciplinary vocabulary across sources by tracking how terms are defined, contrasted, translated, or used differently by source and genre.
evidence: Compare how two sources define or use a key term differently
Use a disciplinary term consistently in a synthesis argument
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to build a term map from multiple sources and use it in a research synthesis paragraph.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Advanced kanji style and reference control
topics.japaneseName: 高度な漢字表記と参照の管理
topics.description: Control kanji, kana, reference lookup, style, and register choices in advanced writing, quotation, classical comparison, and public argument.
evidence: Justify a kanji/kana or reference choice in an advanced text
Revise notation or word form to fit quotation, register, or interpretive purpose
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask {{name}} to revise an advanced paragraph for kanji/kana notation, quotation accuracy, and register fit.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sentence boundaries and early particle awareness
topics.japaneseName: 文のまとまりと助詞への気付き
topics.description: Notice that written sentences have boundaries, sentence-final punctuation, and small function words that connect words in spoken and written Japanese.
evidence: Mark where a short sentence ends and where the next sentence begins
Point to a small function word that helps show how words are connected
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a short familiar passage and show where sentences end and which small words connect ideas.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Handwriting posture, spacing, and character placement
topics.japaneseName: 姿勢・字間・文字の位置に気を付けて書く
topics.description: Write short hiragana strings while controlling posture, pencil movement, character placement, spacing, and readable size.
evidence: Write a short line with stable character size and spacing
Adjust posture, paper position, or pencil grip when readability breaks down
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child copy a short sentence and identify one place where size, spacing, or placement could improve.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Special kana spellings for particles wa, e, and o
topics.japaneseName: 助詞「は」「へ」「を」の表記
topics.description: Read and write the particles は, へ, and を in short sentences while distinguishing their particle readings from ordinary kana readings.
evidence: Read particle は, へ, and を correctly in sentence context
Choose the correct particle spelling in a familiar sentence frame
