topics.assessmentPrompt: Give sentence frames with missing particles and ask the child to choose and read は, へ, or を in context.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Paragraph indentation and line breaks in manuscript writing
topics.japaneseName: 段落の字下げと行の使い方
topics.description: Use indentation, line breaks, punctuation placement, and manuscript-paper conventions to make short written pieces easier to read.
evidence: Start a new paragraph with appropriate indentation or layout convention
Place punctuation and line breaks so the text remains readable
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to copy or revise a short paragraph on manuscript paper, paying attention to indentation and line breaks.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Word order and basic particle roles in simple sentences
topics.japaneseName: 語順と基本的な助詞の働き
topics.description: Notice how topic, destination, object, and predicate parts of a simple sentence are arranged and supported by particles.
evidence: Reorder word cards into a natural short sentence
Explain what a familiar particle helps show in the sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give word and particle cards from a familiar sentence and ask the child to build and read a natural sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Quotation marks for direct speech in stories
topics.japaneseName: 会話文のかぎかっこの使い方
topics.description: Use Japanese quotation marks to show direct speech and distinguish narration from a character or speaker voice.
evidence: Identify the speaker of quoted speech in a short story
Add quotation marks to a simple direct-speech sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a short story sentence and ask the child to mark the spoken words and name the speaker.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Word classes: nouns, verbs, adjectives, and modifiers
topics.japaneseName: 名詞・動詞・形容詞・修飾語の気付き
topics.description: Classify familiar words by broad function and notice how verbs, adjectives, nouns, and modifiers contribute to sentence meaning.
evidence: Group familiar words by broad grammatical function
Explain how changing a modifier changes sentence meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to sort words from a familiar passage and explain how one modifier changes the sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Romaji as a support for names, input, and reference
topics.japaneseName: ローマ字の読み書きと使い道
topics.description: Read and write simple romaji for names, labels, keyboard input, and reference while keeping kana as the primary Japanese writing system.
evidence: Write a familiar name or word in romaji from kana
Explain one practical situation where romaji helps and one where kana or kanji is expected
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to convert several familiar kana words to romaji and say when romaji would be useful.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using grammar awareness to look up inflected words
topics.japaneseName: 活用した語を辞書で調べる
topics.description: Use awareness of word class, okurigana, and base forms to choose a likely dictionary headword for an inflected or modified word.
evidence: Identify a likely base form for a familiar inflected word
Use a dictionary or glossary to check the word meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child simple inflected words from a text and ask which headword they would look up and why.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Modifier scope and particle choice in expanded sentences
topics.japaneseName: 修飾の範囲と助詞の選択
topics.description: Understand which word or phrase a modifier describes and how particle choice shapes meaning in expanded sentences.
evidence: Draw or explain what a modifier describes in a sentence
Compare two particle choices and explain the meaning difference in context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to revise an expanded sentence by moving a modifier or changing a particle, then explain the changed meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Commas for readability and clause boundaries
topics.japaneseName: 読点で文の区切りを分かりやすくする
topics.description: Use Japanese commas to clarify clause boundaries, listed elements, and sentence rhythm without over-punctuating.
evidence: Add commas where they help separate clauses or listed elements
Explain why one comma improves readability and another is unnecessary
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a long sentence and ask them to add commas that make the sentence easier to read.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Polite endings and audience match in classroom writing
topics.japaneseName: 相手に合わせた文末表現
topics.description: Choose sentence endings and polite forms that fit classroom reports, letters, presentations, and casual peer writing.
evidence: Identify whether a sentence ending fits the audience and purpose
Revise one ending to make the style more suitable
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to compare two versions of the same message and choose which better fits a teacher, classmate, or public audience.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Clause relations with conjunctions and connective expressions
topics.japaneseName: 接続語で文と文の関係を表す
topics.description: Use conjunctions and connective expressions to show cause, contrast, condition, addition, sequence, and conclusion across sentences or clauses.
evidence: Label the relationship shown by a connector in a short passage
Choose a connector that makes the relation between two ideas explicit
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give sentence pairs and ask the child to connect them with a phrase that shows cause, contrast, or sequence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Active and passive perspective in sentences
topics.japaneseName: 能動・受身と視点の違い
topics.description: Notice how active and passive sentence forms shift attention among actor, receiver, affected person, and event result.
evidence: Identify who acted and who was affected in active and passive sentences
Explain how passive wording changes the viewpoint of a short sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to compare active and passive versions of a sentence and explain what becomes more prominent.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Proofreading okurigana, kana, and kanji choices
topics.japaneseName: 送り仮名・仮名・漢字を見直す
topics.description: Proofread short writing for okurigana, kana spelling, kanji choice, punctuation, and readability using dictionaries or classroom references.
evidence: Find and correct a spelling, okurigana, or kanji-choice issue in a short text
Use a dictionary, chart, or reference to justify one correction
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a short draft with a few seeded notation issues and ask them to proofread with a reference tool.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Register and sentence-ending consistency across a text
topics.japaneseName: 文章全体の文体をそろえる
topics.description: Maintain consistent register, sentence endings, and audience stance across a report, opinion, letter, or presentation script.
evidence: Identify a sentence whose ending or register does not fit the surrounding text
Revise a paragraph so the audience stance is consistent
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to revise a mixed-style paragraph for consistent sentence endings and audience relationship.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sentence combining and complexity control
topics.japaneseName: 文をつなぎ複雑さを調整する
topics.description: Combine short sentences into clearer complex sentences while controlling modifier placement, clause order, connective choice, and readability.
evidence: Combine two short sentences without losing the intended relation
Split or revise an overlong sentence to improve readability
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child short related sentences and an overlong sentence; ask them to combine one pair and simplify the other.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Editing for kana-kanji balance and readability
topics.japaneseName: 仮名と漢字の使い分けを見直す
topics.description: Revise short writing so kana, kanji, punctuation, paragraph layout, and sentence length support readability for the intended audience.
evidence: Identify a word or phrase where kana-kanji choice affects readability or tone
Revise a short passage for notation, layout, and sentence readability
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to revise a short report for kana-kanji balance, punctuation, and paragraph readability.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Part-of-speech and inflection foundations
topics.japaneseName: 品詞と活用の基礎
topics.description: Analyze common Japanese word classes, inflectional forms, and how predicates change for tense, polarity, connection, and sentence ending.
evidence: Classify familiar words by part of speech and whether they inflect
Identify how a predicate form changes tense, polarity, or sentence connection
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student sentences from a grade-level text and ask them to identify word classes and one inflectional change.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Auxiliary words and particles as meaning signals
topics.japaneseName: 助詞・助動詞が表す意味
topics.description: Interpret how particles and auxiliary words signal relation, stance, modality, connection, negation, and speaker attitude in grade-level texts.
evidence: Explain what relation or stance a particle or auxiliary word contributes
Compare two sentence endings and describe the change in meaning or tone
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate particles and auxiliary words in a short passage and explain two meaning contributions.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Proofreading sentence connections in explanatory writing
topics.japaneseName: 説明文の文と文のつながりを推敲する
topics.description: Revise explanatory writing for sentence connection, connective choice, pronoun/reference clarity, paragraph order, and unnecessary repetition.
evidence: Find a weak sentence connection or unclear reference in a draft
Revise the draft so the explanation flows more logically
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a short explanatory draft and ask them to revise two sentence connections or references.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Complex sentence embedding and clause packaging
topics.japaneseName: 複文と節の組み込み
topics.description: Analyze and revise complex sentences that embed clauses as modifiers, reasons, conditions, quoted content, or noun-like units.
evidence: Identify an embedded clause and explain its role in a sentence
Revise a complex sentence to make clause relations clearer
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to mark embedded clauses in a passage and revise one sentence for clarity.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Respectful, humble, and polite forms as a system
topics.japaneseName: 尊敬語・謙譲語・丁寧語の体系
topics.description: Use respectful, humble, and polite language according to speaker role, listener role, in-group/out-group relation, and communicative purpose.
evidence: Distinguish respectful, humble, and polite forms in familiar contexts
Revise a short exchange so keigo choices match roles and relationship
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student classroom, interview, and workplace-like situations and ask them to choose or revise keigo forms.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Differences between spoken and written styles
topics.japaneseName: 話し言葉と書き言葉の違い
topics.description: Compare spoken and written styles by noticing ellipsis, fillers, sentence endings, connectors, density, and expected explicitness.
evidence: Identify features that make an excerpt sound spoken or written
Rewrite a spoken-style sentence into a more suitable written style
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare a transcript-like excerpt and a written paragraph, then revise one sentence for the opposite mode.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical inflection and particle entry points
topics.japaneseName: 古典文法の活用と助詞への入口
topics.description: Use supported classical-language examples to notice historical inflection, particles, sentence endings, and differences from modern Japanese.
evidence: Identify a classical form or particle that differs from modern usage
Use notes or glosses to explain the rough modern meaning of a short phrase
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a supported classical phrase and ask them to identify one form and explain its modern-language clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Argument modality and stance expressions
topics.japaneseName: 論述のモダリティと立場表現
topics.description: Analyze how argument sentences express certainty, possibility, obligation, evaluation, concession, and author stance.
evidence: Identify wording that marks certainty, possibility, obligation, or evaluation
Explain how a stance expression affects the strength of an argument
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate stance expressions in an argument passage and revise one overstrong or unclear claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Editing argument drafts for logical connectives
topics.japaneseName: 論理の接続を見直して推敲する
topics.description: Revise an argument draft so logical connectives, concession, counterclaim, evidence, and conclusion accurately show the reasoning path.
evidence: Find a connective that misstates the relation between claims
Revise a paragraph so counterclaim, evidence, and conclusion are clearer
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student an argument paragraph with weak connectives and ask them to revise the logic and explain the changes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Register and modality in public contemporary texts
topics.japaneseName: 現代の公共的文章の文体とモダリティ
topics.description: Analyze how public texts use register, modality, quotation, hedging, and evaluative wording to frame contemporary issues.
evidence: Identify register and modality choices that shape reader interpretation
Explain how hedging or evaluative wording changes the force of a claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a public text for register, modality, quotation, and evaluative stance.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rhetorical devices in contemporary informational prose
topics.japaneseName: 現代文の修辞と情報提示
topics.description: Identify analogy, contrast, enumeration, repetition, quotation, and framing devices in contemporary informational or argumentative prose.
evidence: Identify a rhetorical device and its local effect in a passage
Explain how a device helps frame evidence, comparison, or reader response
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a short contemporary prose passage and ask them to identify one rhetorical device and its effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Quotation and citation style in inquiry writing
topics.japaneseName: 探究での引用と出典の示し方
topics.description: Use quotation, paraphrase, source labels, and citation conventions to distinguish the writer voice from source information in inquiry writing.
evidence: Mark which statements come from a source and which are the writer interpretation
Revise a paragraph to integrate quotation or paraphrase with a clear source label
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an inquiry paragraph so quoted, paraphrased, and original statements are clearly distinguished.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rhetorical stance, hedging, and argumentative precision
topics.japaneseName: 修辞的立場・婉曲・論述の精度
topics.description: Analyze and revise logical prose for hedging, qualification, premise control, stance consistency, and degree of certainty.
evidence: Identify a hedge, qualification, premise, or overclaim in logical prose
Revise a claim to better match the available evidence and intended stance
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a logical paragraph and ask them to revise one claim for precision, qualification, and stance consistency.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical grammar and inflection analysis
topics.japaneseName: 古典文法と活用の分析
topics.description: Analyze supported classical Japanese sentences for inflection, particles, auxiliary verbs, ellipsis, and relation to modern interpretation.
evidence: Identify a classical inflectional form, particle, or auxiliary in a supported text
Explain how the grammar clue affects modern-language interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a supported classical sentence and explain one grammar feature that changes interpretation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanbun readback order and paraphrase control
topics.japaneseName: 漢文訓読の語順と現代語訳の調整
topics.description: Use kanbun marks and teacher-provided supports to control reading order, clause relation, and modern-Japanese paraphrase.
evidence: Follow a supported kanbun reading order using marks or annotations
Produce a rough modern-Japanese paraphrase that preserves the relation among clauses
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a supported kanbun sentence and ask them to trace reading order and paraphrase it in modern Japanese.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Academic register and style-sheet control
topics.japaneseName: 学術的な文体と表記ルールの管理
topics.description: Apply a consistent style sheet for academic or public writing, including register, terms, notation, quotation, headings, and reference conventions.
evidence: Create or follow a style sheet for terms, notation, headings, and citation form
Revise a longer draft for register and notation consistency
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create a brief style sheet for a research essay and revise a paragraph against it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Synthesis, paraphrase, and quotation control
topics.japaneseName: 統合・要約・引用の使い分け
topics.description: Integrate multiple sources by distinguishing direct quotation, paraphrase, summary, synthesis, citation, and original claim.
evidence: Label source use as quotation, paraphrase, summary, or synthesis
Revise a synthesis paragraph so source voices and original claims are clear
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student source notes and a draft paragraph, then ask them to clarify quotation, paraphrase, synthesis, and their own claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Advanced proofreading for public-facing text
topics.japaneseName: 公開を意識した高度な校正
topics.description: Proofread public-facing or submission-ready writing for notation, kanji-kana balance, quotation, style consistency, accessibility, and reader trust.
evidence: Find notation, reference, style, or accessibility issues in a near-final text
Revise a public-facing draft so it is more consistent, readable, and trustworthy
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to perform a final proofread of a public-facing essay or presentation handout and justify three corrections.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Matching pictures, words, and events in a story
topics.japaneseName: 絵・言葉・出来事を結び付けて物語を読む
topics.description: Use pictures, repeated words, and short written sentences to connect characters, places, actions, and event order in an early story.
evidence: Point to the picture or sentence that shows who did what
Tell what changed from one story page or scene to the next
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a short picture-supported story and show which words and pictures tell the main event.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing event captions and two-sentence recounts
topics.japaneseName: 出来事を短い文で書く
topics.description: Write one or two readable sentences that name an event, action, feeling, or observation from the child’s own experience or a picture.
evidence: Write a caption or two-sentence recount with a clear event or action
Read the sentence aloud and confirm that another reader can tell what happened
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child draw or choose a picture and write one or two sentences telling what happened.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing story events and character reactions
topics.japaneseName: 出来事と人物の反応を比べる
topics.description: Compare two events or character reactions in a story and explain how words, actions, or pictures show a change.
evidence: Describe how a character reacts differently across two events
Use one word, action, or picture detail as evidence for the comparison
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to choose two story scenes and explain how the character or situation changed.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Beginning-middle-end structure in experience writing
topics.japaneseName: はじめ・中・終わりで経験を書く
topics.description: Organize a short experience text with a beginning, middle event, and ending or reflection so the reader can follow the sequence.
evidence: Arrange three or more sentences into beginning, middle, and ending order
Add one sentence that shows a feeling, result, or reflection
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to write a short experience piece and mark the beginning, middle, and ending.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Matching informational questions to text evidence
topics.japaneseName: 問いと本文の答えを結び付ける
topics.description: Read a short informational text by matching questions to the sentence, heading, caption, or diagram that answers them.
evidence: Find where the text answers a who, what, why, how, or how-many question
Explain whether the answer came from a sentence, heading, caption, or diagram
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a short informational text and ask them to match each question to the evidence location.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Simple note cards from one source
topics.japaneseName: 一つの資料からメモカードを作る
topics.description: Record short note cards from one book, article, or digital page by separating source title, useful facts, and the child’s own question.
evidence: Write a note card with source title and one useful fact
Add one own question or comment separate from copied source information
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to make two note cards from one short source and label source fact versus own question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Topic and supporting sentences in a paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 中心文とくわしい文で段落を書く
topics.description: Write a short paragraph with a central sentence and supporting detail sentences that stay on the same topic.
evidence: Identify the sentence that states the paragraph topic
Add or remove a detail sentence based on whether it supports the topic
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to write a paragraph about an observation and underline the topic sentence and supporting details.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Inferring scene meaning from dialogue and narration
topics.japaneseName: 会話と地の文から場面を読む
topics.description: Infer what a literary scene means by connecting dialogue, narration, setting, action, and character feelings.
evidence: Explain a character feeling using both dialogue and narration
Identify a scene detail that changes how the reader understands the event
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a short story scene and ask what the character may be feeling and which words support that interpretation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Cause and effect in explanatory texts
topics.japaneseName: 説明文の原因と結果を読む
topics.description: Identify cause, effect, reason, and result relationships in explanatory texts, including when the relation is shown by examples or diagrams.
evidence: Mark a cause and effect relation in a short explanatory text
Use a diagram, example, or connective to explain how the relation is shown
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a short explanatory text and fill a cause-effect chart from the passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising paragraph order for the reader
topics.japaneseName: 読み手に分かりやすい段落順に直す
topics.description: Revise a short report or explanation so paragraph order follows time, topic grouping, cause-effect, or question-answer logic.
evidence: Explain why one paragraph order is easier for the reader to follow
Move or revise a paragraph to make the order match the text purpose
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a scrambled short report and ask them to order paragraphs for the reader and explain the choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Integrating diagrams and prose in explanation
topics.japaneseName: 図表と本文を結び付けて説明を読む
topics.description: Read explanatory texts by linking prose with diagrams, tables, photographs, captions, and labels to build a full understanding.
evidence: Explain what information comes from the diagram and what comes from the prose
Use both prose and visual information to answer a comprehension question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to answer questions that require combining a diagram, caption, and paragraph from one text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Repeated details and motifs in literary reading
topics.japaneseName: 繰り返される表現や細部に注目する
topics.description: Notice repeated details, images, words, actions, or motifs and explain how they support theme, mood, or character change.
evidence: Track a repeated detail or expression across a story or poem
Explain how repetition contributes to theme, mood, or character interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to mark a repeated detail in a literary text and explain why the author may have repeated it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Section headings in informational reports
topics.japaneseName: 報告文を見出しで構成する
topics.description: Organize an informational report with section headings, grouped facts, examples, and a conclusion that matches the inquiry question.
evidence: Create headings that match grouped information in a report
Revise one section so facts and examples fit the heading
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to organize research notes into a short report with headings and a conclusion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing author viewpoints across sources
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料の書き手の立場を比べる
topics.description: Compare how two sources on the same topic frame facts, examples, word choice, and author viewpoint.
evidence: Identify a similarity and difference in how two sources treat the same topic
Use wording, example choice, or organization as evidence for viewpoint
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child two short sources on one topic and ask them to compare author viewpoint with evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Basic quotation and paraphrase from sources
topics.japaneseName: 資料から引用と自分の言葉での言い換えをする
topics.description: Distinguish exact quotation from paraphrase and use source labels to show where information came from in an inquiry text.
evidence: Mark whether a sentence is copied exactly, paraphrased, or the writer own idea
Add a source label to a quoted or paraphrased fact
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to turn source notes into two sentences: one quotation and one paraphrase, both with source labels.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising narrative perspective and detail
topics.japaneseName: 視点と細部を意識して物語を書く
topics.description: Revise narrative writing so viewpoint, setting, dialogue, action, and sensory detail work together to shape reader interpretation.
evidence: Identify whether a narrative passage stays in a consistent viewpoint
Revise a scene by adding or adjusting action, dialogue, or detail
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to revise a short narrative scene for viewpoint consistency and meaningful detail.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source-based explanatory summary
topics.japaneseName: 資料に基づいて説明を要約する
topics.description: Summarize an explanatory source by preserving main idea, evidence, and important terms while excluding unsupported personal opinion.
evidence: Write a concise summary that preserves main idea and key evidence
Separate source information from personal response or evaluation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to summarize a short explanatory source and highlight source facts versus their own response.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Conflict and turning point in narrative structure
topics.japaneseName: 物語の対立と転換点を読む
topics.description: Analyze how conflict, turning point, sequence, and viewpoint shape interpretation of a narrative or literary excerpt.
evidence: Identify the conflict and turning point in a narrative excerpt
Explain how the turning point changes character or theme interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a narrative excerpt and ask them to mark the conflict, turning point, and interpretive effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence-commentary paragraph writing
topics.japaneseName: 根拠と考察をつなぐ段落を書く
topics.description: Write a paragraph that introduces evidence, explains its relevance, and connects it back to the central explanation or interpretation.
evidence: Introduce evidence and explain how it supports the paragraph point
Revise a paragraph where evidence is present but commentary is missing
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write or revise a paragraph with one claim, one evidence item, and commentary explaining the link.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Claim, reason, evidence, and warrant in argument
topics.japaneseName: 主張・理由・根拠・論拠を読む
topics.description: Analyze an argument by separating claim, reason, evidence, warrant, counterpoint, and conclusion.
evidence: Label the claim, reasons, evidence, and warrant in a short argument
Explain whether the evidence actually supports the claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a short argument and ask them to diagram claim, reason, evidence, warrant, and conclusion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparative literary response with evidence
topics.japaneseName: 根拠を用いて文学作品を比べて書く
topics.description: Write a comparative response that contrasts interpretations of two scenes, characters, works, or reader responses with textual evidence.
evidence: State a comparison point between two literary passages or interpretations
Use evidence from both sides of the comparison
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a short comparative response using evidence from two literary excerpts or interpretations.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Turning discussion notes into a summary
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いのメモを要約にする
topics.description: Transform seminar or group-discussion notes into a concise summary of positions, evidence, unresolved questions, and next inquiry steps.
evidence: Group discussion notes by position, evidence, and question
Write a summary that preserves multiple viewpoints without collapsing them
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a discussion, ask the student to organize notes into a summary with positions, evidence, and open questions.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing classical and modern expression
topics.japaneseName: 古典と現代の表現を比べて読む
topics.description: Compare classical and modern expressions by noticing word choice, sentence rhythm, imagery, cultural assumptions, and supported paraphrase.
evidence: Identify one expression whose classical wording differs from modern Japanese
Explain how a modern paraphrase preserves or changes the effect
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a supported classical phrase and modern paraphrase, then ask the student to compare wording and effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Counterargument and rebuttal revision
topics.japaneseName: 反論と再反論を入れて論述を直す
topics.description: Revise an argumentative text by adding a fair counterargument, response, evidence, and conclusion that strengthen the reasoning.
evidence: Add a counterargument that represents an opposing view fairly
Revise the rebuttal so it responds with evidence rather than restating the claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an argument paragraph by adding a counterargument and evidence-based rebuttal.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Triangulating media and source claims
topics.japaneseName: 複数メディアの情報を照合する
topics.description: Compare media and source claims by checking author, date, evidence, missing context, framing, and agreement or disagreement across sources.
evidence: Compare two or more media sources for claim, evidence, and framing
Identify what additional source would help verify or complicate the claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student two media excerpts on the same issue and ask them to triangulate claims, evidence, and gaps.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing interpretive lenses on contemporary issues
topics.japaneseName: 現代的課題を複数の観点から読む
topics.description: Read contemporary issue texts by comparing social, ethical, scientific, economic, cultural, or historical lenses and how each lens frames the problem.
evidence: Identify two lenses used to frame a contemporary issue
Explain how each lens changes what counts as important evidence or action
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate two short issue texts for lens, evidence, and implied problem framing.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Annotated bibliography notes for inquiry
topics.japaneseName: 探究のための注釈付き資料リスト
topics.description: Create annotated bibliography notes that record source identity, usefulness, key claim, evidence type, limitation, and next question.
evidence: Write a source annotation with claim, evidence type, usefulness, and limitation
Use annotations to choose which source is most relevant to an inquiry question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate three sources for a contemporary issue inquiry and select the most useful source with a reason.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rhetorical analysis paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 修辞の効果を分析する段落を書く
topics.description: Write an analysis paragraph that identifies a rhetorical choice, quotes or paraphrases evidence, and explains its effect on reader interpretation.
evidence: Name a rhetorical choice and provide textual evidence
Explain how the choice affects framing, emphasis, credibility, or reader response
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a paragraph analyzing one rhetorical choice in a contemporary prose passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Assumptions and warrants in logical prose
topics.japaneseName: 論理的文章の前提と論拠を読む
topics.description: Analyze logical prose by identifying assumptions, warrants, definitions, scope limits, counterexamples, and evidence quality.
evidence: Identify an unstated assumption or warrant needed for an argument
Explain how a definition or scope limit affects the argument
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a logical prose excerpt and ask them to identify a warrant, assumption, and scope limitation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Applying literary-critical concepts to interpretation
topics.japaneseName: 文学批評の観点を用いて解釈する
topics.description: Apply a critical concept such as narrator reliability, symbolism, imagery, structure, gender, class, memory, or place to support a literary interpretation.
evidence: Choose a critical concept that fits the literary passage
Use textual evidence to show how the concept changes interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to interpret a literary excerpt using one critical concept and two pieces of textual evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Research literature-review paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 先行研究や資料を整理する段落を書く
topics.description: Write a literature-review style paragraph that groups sources by position, method, evidence, or limitation rather than listing them one by one.
evidence: Group two or more sources by relationship rather than source order
Write a paragraph that explains agreement, disagreement, gap, or limitation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to turn annotated source notes into a paragraph grouping sources by position and evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Critiquing a secondary interpretation of literature
topics.japaneseName: 他者の文学解釈を批判的に読む
topics.description: Read another interpretation of a literary work and evaluate its evidence, assumptions, blind spots, and possible alternative reading.
evidence: Identify evidence and assumptions in a secondary interpretation
Offer an alternative interpretation supported by textual evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a short critical comment on a literary text and ask them to evaluate evidence and propose an alternative reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparative source synthesis matrix
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料を比較して統合表にまとめる
topics.description: Build a synthesis matrix that compares multiple sources by claim, evidence, method, scope, reliability, and usefulness for a research argument.
evidence: Complete a matrix comparing sources by claim, evidence, scope, and reliability
Use the matrix to identify a pattern, conflict, gap, or research decision
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create a synthesis matrix from several sources and explain one pattern and one gap.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Extended argument architecture
topics.japaneseName: 長い論述の構成を設計する
topics.description: Design and revise a longer argument with problem framing, thesis, definitions, source synthesis, counterargument, implication, and conclusion.
evidence: Create an outline that shows thesis, sections, evidence, counterargument, and conclusion
Revise the argument architecture when evidence or scope changes
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to outline a long research argument and justify the order of sections and evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Literary-cultural synthesis essay
topics.japaneseName: 文学と文化を統合して論じる文章
topics.description: Write an essay that synthesizes literary evidence, cultural context, classical or modern comparison, and the writer’s interpretation.
evidence: Use literary evidence and cultural context to support a synthesis claim
Explain how comparison across works, eras, or genres changes interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to draft a literary-cultural synthesis paragraph with evidence from at least two works or contexts.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Adapting advanced writing for public audiences
topics.japaneseName: 高度な文章を公開する相手に合わせて直す
topics.description: Revise advanced writing for a specific public audience by adjusting framing, terms, evidence density, quotation, register, and call to action.
evidence: Identify what a public audience needs clarified, reduced, or expanded
Revise a paragraph so framing, terms, evidence, and register match the audience
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to adapt a research or literary argument paragraph for a public audience and explain the revision choices.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Pair talk: listen and respond to a classmate
topics.japaneseName: 友だちの話を聞いて返す
topics.description: Listen to a classmate in a short pair exchange, repeat the important part, and respond with a related comment or question.
evidence: Repeat one important part of a classmate’s short statement
Respond with a related comment, question, or gesture
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child pair-talk about a familiar topic and ask them to repeat and respond to the partner’s main point.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Show-and-tell with simple sequence
topics.japaneseName: 順序をつけて紹介する
topics.description: Present a familiar object, drawing, or experience in two or three ordered parts using simple sequence words and clear voice.
evidence: Tell two or three ordered details about an object or experience
Speak clearly enough for classmates to ask or answer one simple question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to introduce an object or drawing using first/next/finally-style sequencing.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Expressive oral reading and listening for voice cues
topics.japaneseName: 声の調子を聞きながら音読する
topics.description: Read or listen to short familiar text aloud and notice voice volume, pause, rhythm, and feeling cues that support meaning.
evidence: Read a short familiar line with a pause or voice cue that matches meaning
Explain how a classmate’s voice helped show feeling or sequence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child listen to and reread a short line, then identify one voice cue that helped meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classmate interview with one follow-up question
topics.japaneseName: 友だちにインタビューして聞き返す
topics.description: Ask a classmate prepared questions, listen to the answer, and ask one follow-up question that targets a missing detail.
evidence: Ask prepared questions in a clear order
Ask one follow-up question based on the answer actually heard
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child interview a classmate about a familiar experience and ask one follow-up question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Shared retelling and comparison after listening
topics.japaneseName: 聞いた話を二人で確かめる
topics.description: Retell a heard story or explanation with a partner, compare what each listener remembered, and agree on the main event or point.
evidence: Retell a heard text with a partner and compare remembered details
Agree on the main event or point after checking differences
topics.assessmentPrompt: Read a short story aloud and ask pairs to retell, compare details, and decide the main event.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Mini report about an object or experience
topics.japaneseName: ものや体験について短く報告する
topics.description: Give a short oral report that names the topic, gives two details, and closes with a feeling, discovery, or question for listeners.
evidence: Present a topic with two details in a clear order
End with a feeling, discovery, or question that invites listener response
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to give a one-minute report on an object or experience and answer one listener question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Preparing questions before listening
topics.japaneseName: 聞く前に問いを準備する
topics.description: Set a listening purpose by preparing questions before a talk, then check which questions were answered and what remains unclear.
evidence: Prepare two questions before listening to a short report
Mark which questions were answered and which need follow-up
topics.assessmentPrompt: Before a classmate report, ask the child to prepare questions and then check answers after listening.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Small-group discussion with role cards
topics.japaneseName: 役割をもって少人数で話し合う
topics.description: Participate in a small-group discussion with simple roles such as facilitator, recorder, questioner, or summarizer.
evidence: Carry out one assigned discussion role during a small-group task
Use a role phrase to ask, summarize, record, or invite another speaker
topics.assessmentPrompt: Assign role cards in a small-group task and ask the child to use the role to support the discussion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Explaining an observation in order
topics.japaneseName: 観察したことを順序立てて説明する
topics.description: Explain an observation, process, or classroom finding orally by stating what was noticed, in what order, and what question or conclusion follows.
evidence: State observed facts in a clear order
Add one conclusion, question, or next observation based on the facts
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to explain an observation or experiment note orally using order and conclusion language.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Information interview with note-taking
topics.japaneseName: 取材してメモを取る
topics.description: Conduct a short information interview, take keyword notes, and confirm a detail before using the information in a report or discussion.
evidence: Ask prepared and follow-up questions during an interview
Take keyword notes and confirm one important detail with the speaker
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child interview a peer or adult about a classroom topic and produce confirmed keyword notes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Paraphrasing before adding an idea
topics.japaneseName: 相手の意見を言い換えてから発言する
topics.description: Show listening in discussion by paraphrasing a classmate’s idea before adding agreement, disagreement, question, or evidence.
evidence: Paraphrase a classmate’s idea fairly before responding
Add a related reason, question, or evidence after the paraphrase
topics.assessmentPrompt: During group discussion, ask the child to begin a response by restating a classmate’s idea fairly.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presentation with audience questions
topics.japaneseName: 聞き手の質問に答える発表
topics.description: Present a short report with clear topic, order, visual or note support, and planned time for audience questions.
evidence: Present a short report with visible organization and supporting details
Answer an audience question by referring back to the report or source
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to give a short report and answer two audience questions using report details.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracking roles and contributions in discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いの役割と発言を追う
topics.description: Listen to a discussion by tracking who proposed, questioned, supported, disagreed, summarized, or moved the discussion forward.
evidence: Identify two different contribution types in a group discussion
Explain how a contribution moved the discussion forward or left a gap
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a group discussion, ask the child to name contributors, contribution types, and one missing role.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing evidence in spoken reports
topics.japaneseName: 発表の根拠を比べて聞く
topics.description: Listen to two short spoken reports and compare what evidence each speaker used, how specific it was, and whether it answered the question.
evidence: Identify evidence used in two spoken reports
Compare which evidence is more specific or more relevant to the question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child listen to two short reports and compare the evidence used by each speaker.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Persuasive mini-speech with counter-question
topics.japaneseName: 反対の質問を想定した短い説得発表
topics.description: Give a short persuasive speech that states a claim, reason, evidence, and likely question from someone who is not yet convinced.
evidence: Present claim, reason, and evidence in a short persuasive speech
Anticipate and answer one counter-question from the audience
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to give a short persuasive speech and prepare one question an unconvinced listener might ask.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Consensus discussion with agenda and decision criteria
topics.japaneseName: 議題と判断基準を使って合意形成する
topics.description: Use an agenda and simple decision criteria to compare proposals, build consensus, record unresolved points, and decide next actions.
evidence: Use decision criteria to compare proposals in discussion
Summarize consensus, unresolved point, and next action
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the group a classroom decision task and ask them to use criteria, reach consensus, and summarize the next action.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source-supported presentation with Q&A
topics.japaneseName: 資料を根拠にした発表と質疑応答
topics.description: Present inquiry findings with source support, explain why the source is relevant, and answer questions by returning to evidence.
evidence: Cite or refer to a source during an oral presentation
Answer an audience question using evidence rather than only opinion
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to present inquiry findings and answer audience questions with source evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Debate listening with an evidence chart
topics.japaneseName: 討論を聞いて根拠を表に整理する
topics.description: Listen to a structured debate and chart each side’s claim, evidence, counterpoint, and unanswered question.
evidence: Chart claims and evidence for at least two sides of a debate
Identify one unanswered question or weak evidence point
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child listen to a short debate and complete a claim-evidence-counterpoint chart.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Speech outline and audience adaptation
topics.japaneseName: 聞き手に合わせてスピーチを構成する
topics.description: Plan and deliver a speech with opening, main points, evidence, examples, transitions, and audience-aware word choice.
evidence: Create a speech outline with opening, main points, evidence, and closing
Revise one expression or example for the intended audience
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to outline and deliver a short speech, then explain one audience-adaptation choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Discussion evidence tracker
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いの根拠を追跡する
topics.description: Track each speaker’s claim, evidence, source, and relation to the discussion question during a lower-secondary discussion.
evidence: Record speaker, claim, evidence, and source during a discussion
Identify which evidence most directly answers the discussion question
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a discussion, ask the student to complete an evidence tracker and identify the strongest evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for implied stance and tone
topics.japaneseName: 言い方から立場や調子を推測する
topics.description: Infer speaker stance from wording, tone, emphasis, hedging, examples, and what the speaker chooses not to say directly.
evidence: Identify wording or tone that suggests agreement, concern, uncertainty, or emphasis
Explain the inferred stance without overstating what was explicitly said
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play a short statement and ask the student to infer stance from wording, tone, and emphasis.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Seminar facilitation through paraphrase and linking
topics.japaneseName: 言い換えと関連付けで討議を進める
topics.description: Facilitate a seminar by paraphrasing contributions, linking related views, naming disagreement, and returning to the shared question.
evidence: Paraphrase and link two contributions during seminar discussion
Name an agreement, disagreement, or unresolved question without closing discussion prematurely
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to facilitate a short seminar segment using paraphrase, linking, and question-return moves.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing literary interpretations orally
topics.japaneseName: 文学の解釈を口頭で比べる
topics.description: Compare multiple interpretations of a literary text in discussion by citing textual evidence, explaining assumptions, and asking clarifying questions.
evidence: State how two interpretations differ and what evidence each uses
Ask a clarifying question about another reader’s assumption or evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have students discuss two interpretations of a literary passage and ask each student to compare evidence and assumptions.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Oral explanation of data and tables
topics.japaneseName: 資料や表を使って説明する
topics.description: Explain a table, chart, or survey result orally by naming the data source, trend, contrast, and limitation.
evidence: Explain a trend or contrast from a table or chart
State one limitation or caution about the data source
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a small chart and ask them to explain the main trend, contrast, and limitation orally.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Formal debate rebuttal and closing statement
topics.japaneseName: 反論とまとめを含む討論
topics.description: Participate in formal debate by presenting claim, evidence, rebuttal, response to counterargument, and concise closing statement.
evidence: Respond to an opposing argument with evidence-based rebuttal
Give a closing statement that weighs the central point of disagreement
topics.assessmentPrompt: Run a short debate and ask the student to deliver a rebuttal and closing statement based on evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical recitation rhythm and phrasing
topics.japaneseName: 古典の音読のリズムと区切り
topics.description: Read or listen to supported classical passages aloud, noticing rhythm, phrasing, repeated patterns, and how sound supports interpretation.
evidence: Read a supported classical line with meaningful phrase breaks
Explain how rhythm or repeated phrasing supports memory or interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to read a supported classical passage aloud and identify one phrase break or rhythmic pattern.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Analyzing framing in media speech
topics.japaneseName: メディア音声の問題設定を分析する
topics.description: Listen to public or media speech and analyze how topic framing, examples, order, word choice, and omitted context shape listener interpretation.
evidence: Identify how a speaker frames the issue for listeners
Name one example, order choice, or omission that affects interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play a short public or media speech excerpt and ask the student to analyze framing and omitted context.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Contemporary issue roundtable
topics.japaneseName: 現代的課題について円卓で討議する
topics.description: Participate in a roundtable on a contemporary issue by stating position, evidence, uncertainty, and a response to another participant.
evidence: State a position with evidence and one uncertainty or limitation
Respond to another participant by extending, qualifying, or challenging the point
topics.assessmentPrompt: Run a short roundtable on a contemporary issue and ask each student to contribute position, evidence, and response.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rhetorical analysis of public speech
topics.japaneseName: 公共的なスピーチの修辞を聞く
topics.description: Analyze public speech for thesis, audience framing, repetition, contrast, evidence, emotional appeal, and call to action.
evidence: Identify a rhetorical device in a public speech excerpt
Explain how the device shapes audience response or issue framing
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play or read a public speech excerpt and ask the student to annotate thesis, rhetorical device, and audience effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Inquiry pitch with source basis
topics.japaneseName: 資料に基づいて探究テーマを提案する
topics.description: Pitch an inquiry question by explaining problem framing, source basis, expected value, uncertainty, and next research step.
evidence: Present an inquiry question with source basis and expected value
Answer a question about scope, uncertainty, or next research step
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to pitch an inquiry question and defend its source basis and scope in Q&A.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Literary interpretation colloquium
topics.japaneseName: 文学解釈をめぐる討議
topics.description: Discuss a literary interpretation by naming interpretive lens, textual evidence, assumption, alternative reading, and unresolved ambiguity.
evidence: State an interpretation with lens, evidence, and assumption
Respond to an alternative reading without collapsing ambiguity too quickly
topics.assessmentPrompt: Hold a literary interpretation colloquium and ask the student to present and respond to alternative readings.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Oral defense of a logical argument
topics.japaneseName: 論理的主張を口頭で説明し防御する
topics.description: Defend a logical argument orally by explaining definitions, assumptions, evidence quality, counterexample, and limits of the claim.
evidence: Explain definition, assumption, evidence, and limit in response to challenge
Revise or qualify a claim orally when evidence is insufficient
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a short argument presentation, ask the student to answer challenges about definitions, evidence, and counterexamples.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Critiquing evidence in a peer presentation
topics.japaneseName: 発表の根拠を批評的に聞く
topics.description: Critique a peer presentation by evaluating evidence relevance, source quality, reasoning, organization, and audience response.
evidence: Identify a strength and limitation in a peer presentation’s evidence
Ask a critique question that targets reasoning, source quality, or organization
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the student listen to a peer presentation and submit a critique question with evidence-based rationale.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Research symposium moderation
topics.japaneseName: 研究発表会を司会進行する
topics.description: Moderate a research-style session by opening the question, managing turns, connecting presentations, tracking time, and synthesizing unresolved issues.
evidence: Manage turn-taking and time while preserving discussion purpose
Synthesize common themes and unresolved issues across presentations
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to moderate a short symposium segment and provide a closing synthesis of themes and questions.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Panel synthesis response
topics.japaneseName: パネル討議を統合して応答する
topics.description: Listen to a panel or source-based oral exchange and synthesize positions, shared assumptions, conflicts, gaps, and possible next questions.
evidence: Synthesize multiple speaker positions without flattening disagreement
Name one shared assumption, conflict, evidence gap, or next research question
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a panel discussion, ask the student to synthesize positions and propose one follow-up question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Adapting a research argument for a public speech
topics.japaneseName: 研究論述を公開発表向けに調整する
topics.description: Adapt a research or literary-cultural argument for a public audience by adjusting framing, term density, evidence selection, story, and call to action.
evidence: Revise a research argument into an audience-aware speech structure
Explain how term density, evidence, and framing changed for public listeners
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to convert a research paragraph into a public speech segment and explain the adaptation choices.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Getting facts from pictures and captions
topics.japaneseName: 絵と短い説明から事実を読む
topics.description: Use a picture, label, and short caption to identify one factual detail and tell how it is shown.
evidence: Point to the picture or caption that gives a fact
Tell the fact in a complete spoken sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a simple illustrated explanation and ask which picture or caption tells one fact.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using book parts to find information
topics.japaneseName: 本の部分を使って情報を探す
topics.description: Recognize a title, cover, page, picture, label, and page number as parts that help a reader find information.
evidence: Name a title, picture, and page number in an informational book
Use a page or picture cue to return to a fact
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to find a named page or picture in an informational book and explain which part helped.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Words for observable attributes
topics.japaneseName: 物の様子を表す言葉
topics.description: Use words for color, shape, size, number, position, and movement to describe observable facts accurately.
evidence: Choose precise attribute words for an object or picture
Revise a vague description by adding an observable detail
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show the child an object or picture and ask for two precise words that another reader could verify.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Steps and order in informational texts
topics.japaneseName: 説明文の順序を読む
topics.description: Identify the order of steps or events in a short explanatory text and retell the sequence using order words.
evidence: Put three explanatory steps in text order
Use order words to retell how something happens
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a short how-to or nature explanation and arrange three step cards in order.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Collecting information with a class survey
topics.japaneseName: 学級アンケートで情報を集める
topics.description: Ask a simple survey question, tally responses, and state one result without overstating what the data shows.
evidence: Record responses in a tally or simple table
State one result using the collected numbers
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child survey classmates about a familiar topic and report one result from the tally.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Words for quantity and comparison
topics.japaneseName: 数量や比べ方を表す言葉
topics.description: Use words for more, less, same, increase, decrease, and order to compare information in text or simple data.
evidence: Use comparison words to explain a simple table or survey result
Replace a vague comparison with a more precise word
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child two small quantities or survey results and ask them to compare them in words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Predicting answers from headings and questions
topics.japaneseName: 見出しと問いから答えを予想する
topics.description: Use headings, subheadings, and reader questions to predict what information an explanatory section should answer.
evidence: Turn a heading into a likely question
Check whether the section answers the predicted question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to preview headings, write one likely question, then verify it after reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recording title, author, and date from a source
topics.japaneseName: 資料の題名・作者・日付を記録する
topics.description: Record basic source-identification information before taking notes so facts can be traced back to a book, article, or page.
evidence: Copy or record a source title and author or publisher
Add a date when it is available on the source
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child two classroom sources and ask them to make a source card for one of them.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Definition and example vocabulary
topics.japaneseName: 定義と例を示す言葉
topics.description: Use words and phrases that define a term, introduce an example, or distinguish a general idea from a concrete case.
evidence: Identify a sentence that defines a key word
Add an example phrase to clarify a new term
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to find a definition sentence and write one example that fits it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Distinguishing facts, examples, and explanations
topics.japaneseName: 事実・例・説明の役割を分けて読む
topics.description: Tell whether a sentence states a fact, gives an example, or explains why or how something happens.
evidence: Label sentences as fact, example, or explanation
Explain how an example helps the reader understand a fact
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a paragraph and ask them to mark one fact, one example, and one explanatory sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Extracting notes from tables and charts
topics.japaneseName: 表やグラフからメモを取る
topics.description: Read a simple table or chart, extract the important value or pattern, and write it as a note in words.
evidence: Find a requested value or pattern in a table or chart
Write a note that converts the visual information into a sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a table or chart and write two notes about what it shows.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Cause and result connective words
topics.japaneseName: 原因と結果を結ぶ言葉
topics.description: Use connective expressions that show cause, reason, result, and contrast when reading or writing explanations.
evidence: Underline words that signal cause or result
Revise two sentences by adding an appropriate connective
topics.assessmentPrompt: Have the child add cause-result connectives to a short explanatory paragraph.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Problem-solution structure in explanatory texts
topics.japaneseName: 問題解決型の説明構造を読む
topics.description: Identify a problem, proposed solution, evidence, and outcome in an explanatory or public-information text.
evidence: Mark the problem and solution in a text
Explain which evidence supports the proposed solution
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a problem-solution text and complete a problem, solution, evidence, outcome chart.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source-comparison note table
topics.japaneseName: 資料比較メモ表
topics.description: Compare two sources on the same topic by recording claim, evidence, source type, and agreement or disagreement.
evidence: Complete a comparison table for two sources
State one agreement or difference using the notes
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give two short sources and ask the child to compare their claims and evidence in a note table.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Words that label evidence and examples
topics.japaneseName: 根拠や例を示す言葉
topics.description: Recognize and use expressions that introduce evidence, examples, data, and quoted information in reports and opinions.
evidence: Identify phrases that introduce evidence in a paragraph
Add an evidence-introducing phrase to a report sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to revise a report paragraph by making evidence signals explicit.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence chains in explanations
topics.japaneseName: 説明の根拠のつながりを読む
topics.description: Trace how facts, examples, diagrams, and expert statements connect to support an explanatory conclusion.
evidence: Draw a chain from evidence to conclusion
Explain which link in the chain is strongest or weakest
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to map evidence from a multi-paragraph explanation to its conclusion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sorting sources by usefulness and reliability
topics.japaneseName: 資料の有用性と信頼性を分けて考える
topics.description: Sort sources for an inquiry by relevance, currency, author or publisher, evidence quality, and match to the question.
evidence: Rank sources for usefulness to an inquiry question
Give one reliability reason for accepting or rejecting a source
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give several sources for a topic and ask the child to select the two most useful with reasons.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Causal and contrastive academic connectives
topics.japaneseName: 因果・対比を示す学習語彙
topics.description: Use academic connectives for cause, contrast, condition, addition, and conclusion in source-based explanation.
evidence: Group connectives by relationship type
Revise an explanation so connectives match the logic
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to revise a short explanation by selecting connectives that clarify the reasoning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Definition, example, and generalization structure
topics.japaneseName: 定義・例示・一般化の構造を読む
topics.description: Analyze how an explanatory text defines a concept, develops it through examples, and moves toward a broader generalization.
evidence: Annotate definition, example, and generalization moves
Explain how an example supports the general claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a lower-secondary explanatory text for definition, example, and generalization moves.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source credibility criteria
topics.japaneseName: 資料の信頼性を判断する観点
topics.description: Evaluate a source using author, publisher, evidence, date, purpose, and relation to the inquiry question.
evidence: Apply at least three credibility criteria to a source
Explain the difference between relevance and reliability
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student two sources and ask which is more reliable for a specific question, with criteria.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Building meaning for abstract terms from context
topics.japaneseName: 文脈から抽象語の意味を作る
topics.description: Infer the meaning of an abstract academic term from definition, example, contrast, and repeated use across a text.
evidence: Infer an abstract term from context clues
Write a provisional definition and revise it after rereading
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to define an unfamiliar abstract term using only context from the text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading claims supported by data commentary
topics.japaneseName: データ解説が支える主張を読む
topics.description: Analyze how a writer turns numbers, graphs, or survey results into commentary that supports a claim.
evidence: Match a data point to the commentary that interprets it
Judge whether the commentary is justified by the data
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a graph-supported paragraph and ask whether the commentary fairly represents the data.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Mapping media claims to evidence
topics.japaneseName: メディアの主張と根拠を対応させる
topics.description: Map a media text by separating claim, quoted source, image or data evidence, omitted context, and reader effect.
evidence: Create a claim-evidence map for a media text
Identify one point where evidence is weak, missing, or emotional
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map a short article or media post and identify how the evidence supports the claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evaluative and hedging words in argument
topics.japaneseName: 評価や限定を示す言葉
topics.description: Recognize words that evaluate strength, certainty, importance, limitation, and probability in explanatory and argumentative texts.
evidence: Underline words that weaken, strengthen, or limit a claim
Explain how changing a hedge changes the claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise three claims by changing evaluative or hedging words and explaining the effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence hierarchy in policy arguments
topics.japaneseName: 政策的な主張の根拠の階層を読む
topics.description: Evaluate how a policy-oriented argument uses statistics, cases, expert judgment, counterexamples, and assumptions.
evidence: Rank evidence types by strength for a policy claim
Identify an assumption that connects evidence to recommendation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a policy argument and ask them to rank its evidence and identify one assumption.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracing source lineage and context
topics.japaneseName: 情報の出所と文脈をたどる
topics.description: Trace a claim from a secondary source back to an original source, noting context, date, and changes in wording.
evidence: Identify whether a source quotes, summarizes, or links to an earlier source
Explain how context affects the meaning of a quoted claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to trace a claim across two sources and record what changed or stayed the same.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Stance and attribution vocabulary
topics.japaneseName: 立場と出典を示す語彙
topics.description: Use vocabulary that attributes claims to sources, marks stance, and distinguishes fact, interpretation, and evaluation.
evidence: Choose attribution verbs that fit a source stance
Rewrite a claim to separate source statement from student interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a paragraph so every claim is attributed or marked as interpretation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing public-discourse genres
topics.japaneseName: 公共的な文章ジャンルを比較する
topics.description: Compare editorials, public notices, commentary, speeches, and reports by purpose, audience, evidence, and rhetorical stance.
evidence: Compare two public-discourse genres with purpose and audience criteria
Explain how genre changes evidence selection or tone
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two public texts on the same issue and explain how genre shapes the argument.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Refining database and web queries for inquiry
topics.japaneseName: 探究のための検索語を調整する
topics.description: Refine search terms, synonyms, date ranges, source types, and exclusion terms to gather sources for a focused inquiry question.
evidence: Document two query revisions and why they improved results
Select sources that match the revised inquiry scope
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to refine an inquiry search twice and explain how the result set changed.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Policy and public-register vocabulary
topics.japaneseName: 政策・公共的表現の語彙
topics.description: Interpret and use public-register vocabulary for policy, rights, responsibility, evidence, feasibility, and social impact.
evidence: Define public-register terms from context and source use
Use policy vocabulary accurately in a source-based explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a public-issue text for policy terms and use three in an explanation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading method and evidence sections in academic prose
topics.japaneseName: 学術的文章の方法と根拠を読む
topics.description: Analyze how academic prose states a question, defines method, presents evidence, and limits a conclusion.
evidence: Identify question, method, evidence, and limitation in an academic passage
Explain how the method affects the strength of the conclusion
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate an academic passage and explain how the method supports or limits the claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Notes across competing scholarly positions
topics.japaneseName: 複数の学説や立場を整理するメモ
topics.description: Take notes that separate research question, position, evidence, method, and limitation across sources with competing interpretations.
evidence: Create notes that keep two positions and their evidence separate
Write one comparison sentence about how the positions differ
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give two short academic-position excerpts and ask the student to make a comparison note table.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Methodology and limitation vocabulary
topics.japaneseName: 方法と限界を示す語彙
topics.description: Use vocabulary that describes method, scope, limitation, validity, interpretation, and competing explanations.
evidence: Identify words that limit a conclusion or describe method
Use limitation vocabulary to qualify a claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an overgeneralized claim with method and limitation vocabulary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading synthesis arguments across source types
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料を統合する論証を読む
topics.description: Evaluate how a synthesis argument combines scholarly, public, statistical, and experiential sources into one warranted position.
evidence: Map how different source types contribute to a synthesis claim
Identify a gap, tension, or unresolved assumption across sources
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map source roles in a synthesis argument and judge where the argument needs stronger support.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Attribution ethics and source boundaries
topics.japaneseName: 出典明示と資料利用の倫理
topics.description: Maintain clear boundaries among quotation, paraphrase, summary, synthesis, and original claim while giving source attribution.
evidence: Label quoted, paraphrased, summarized, and original material in a draft
Correct an attribution problem without weakening the argument
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a synthesis paragraph and ask them to mark source boundaries and fix one attribution issue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Transferring disciplinary register across sources
topics.japaneseName: 分野ごとの語彙と文体を統合する
topics.description: Move between public, academic, literary, and disciplinary vocabularies while keeping terms accurate and register appropriate.
evidence: Explain how the same issue is worded differently across disciplines
Revise a synthesis paragraph so terms from different fields are used consistently
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to synthesize two discipline-specific passages and explain how they adjusted terminology.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Mora segmentation when copying kana words
topics.japaneseName: かな語をモーラで区切って写す
topics.description: Segment familiar spoken words into mora-sized kana units when reading, copying, or checking early written words.
evidence: Clap or count mora while matching them to kana in a word
Copy a short kana word and check that each mora has a written unit
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to copy familiar kana words and explain how the sounds match the written units.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Katakana stroke shape and readable placement
topics.japaneseName: 片仮名の字形と配置
topics.description: Write common katakana with readable stroke direction, shape, spacing, and placement in simple words and names.
evidence: Write selected katakana with recognizable stroke shape and proportion
Copy a simple katakana word so another reader can identify it
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to copy and read simple katakana words, checking stroke shape and spacing.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading punctuation as pause and sentence boundary
topics.japaneseName: 句読点を休みと文の区切りとして読む
topics.description: Use Japanese punctuation to pause, separate sentences, and copy short sentences with visible boundaries.
evidence: Pause appropriately at maru and ten when reading aloud
Copy a short sentence with punctuation in the correct place
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read and copy two short sentences, marking where each sentence ends.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reviewing kana spelling for long vowels and sound changes
topics.japaneseName: 長音・促音・拗音の表記を見直す
topics.description: Review kana words for long vowels, small tsu, contracted sounds, dakuten, and handakuten in short written work.
evidence: Find and correct a kana spelling error involving length, gemination, or sound mark
Explain what changed in the sound when the spelling changed
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child short kana-rich sentences and ask them to check sound-mark and long-vowel spellings.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Mixed kana-kanji word boundaries
topics.japaneseName: 漢字仮名交じり文の語のまとまり
topics.description: Recognize how kanji, kana, particles, and okurigana help show word boundaries in simple mixed-script sentences.
evidence: Mark word groups in a mixed kana-kanji sentence
Identify where okurigana or a particle helps show the boundary of a word
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a simple mixed-script sentence and mark the word groups that make it easier to read.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Clean copy with margins and visible corrections
topics.japaneseName: 余白と訂正を意識して清書する
topics.description: Produce a clean handwritten copy by controlling margins, line position, spacing, and simple corrections before final writing.
evidence: Recopy a short draft with improved spacing and line placement
Make a visible correction without making the text harder to read
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to turn a rough draft into a clean copy and explain one correction they made.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Keyboard romaji-to-kana input control
topics.japaneseName: ローマ字入力で仮名を正しく出す
topics.description: Use romaji input to produce intended kana, including small kana, long sounds, and corrections during digital writing.
evidence: Type a short kana phrase from romaji input and correct an input error
Explain how a small kana or long sound was entered
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to type a short Japanese phrase and fix one kana input error.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Linking okurigana to dictionary forms
topics.japaneseName: 送り仮名と辞書形を結び付ける
topics.description: Use okurigana and inflection endings to connect an inflected word in text to its dictionary form and basic meaning.
evidence: Find the dictionary form of an inflected word with okurigana
Explain which kana ending changed and which part stayed stable
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child inflected words from a text and ask them to identify the dictionary form for lookup.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Character proportion and component layout
topics.japaneseName: 字形のバランスと部品の配置
topics.description: Write kanji and kana with balanced proportions by noticing component placement, stroke density, and character box alignment.
evidence: Rewrite a character to improve component balance and readability
Explain whether a component sits left-right, top-bottom, or enclosing another part
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to revise several handwritten characters for proportion and component placement.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji notebook for multiple readings and word families
topics.japaneseName: 複数の読みと熟語を記録する漢字ノート
