topics.description: Maintain a kanji notebook that groups readings, word families, okurigana patterns, and example contexts for kanji with multiple readings.
evidence: Record at least two readings or word-family examples for a kanji
Use the notebook to choose a reading in a new sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to add a multiple-reading kanji to a notebook and use it to read a new word.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recording radical and stroke-count lookup results
topics.japaneseName: 部首・画数で調べた結果を記録する
topics.description: Use component, radical, stroke count, or shape to look up an unfamiliar kanji and record the result for later reading or writing.
evidence: Use a radical or stroke-count route to find an unfamiliar kanji
Record reading, meaning clue, and one example word from the lookup
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child an unfamiliar kanji from a text and ask them to look it up and record the result.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Copyediting quotation marks and commas
topics.japaneseName: かぎ括弧と読点を校正する
topics.description: Check direct speech, commas, sentence boundaries, and line placement so quoted and explanatory sentences are readable.
evidence: Correct quotation-mark placement in a short passage
Add or remove a comma to improve readability without changing the meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to copyedit a short dialogue or report passage for quotation marks and commas.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Selecting kanji for homophones from context
topics.japaneseName: 同音異義語の漢字を文脈で選ぶ
topics.description: Use sentence meaning, word family, and subject context to choose the appropriate kanji for common homophones.
evidence: Choose the correct kanji for a homophone in a sentence
Explain which context clue ruled out another kanji
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child homophone choices in short sentences and ask them to justify the kanji selection.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Proofreading okurigana and inflection endings
topics.japaneseName: 送り仮名と活用語尾を校正する
topics.description: Proofread inflected words for okurigana, tense or aspect endings, and kana-kanji consistency in short reports and opinions.
evidence: Find an okurigana or inflection-ending error in a draft
Correct the error while keeping the intended word and sentence meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to proofread a short draft for okurigana and inflection-ending errors.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Proofreading punctuation for paragraph flow
topics.japaneseName: 段落の流れをよくする句読点の校正
topics.description: Review paragraph punctuation, connective placement, and sentence length so readers can follow the flow of ideas.
evidence: Revise punctuation to clarify one long or confusing sentence
Explain how punctuation or connective placement changes readability
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to proofread a paragraph and make one punctuation revision that improves flow.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kana-kanji balance in a typed draft
topics.japaneseName: 入力文の漢字と仮名のバランス
topics.description: Review kana-kanji conversion choices in typed drafts so the text is readable, age-appropriate, and not over-converted.
evidence: Identify an over-converted or under-converted word in a typed draft
Revise conversion choices to improve readability
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to review a typed paragraph and explain two kana-kanji conversion choices.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Scanning public texts for orthographic choices
topics.japaneseName: 公共の文章の表記を観察する
topics.description: Notice how public notices, pamphlets, websites, and classroom documents use kana, kanji, numbers, punctuation, and emphasis.
evidence: Identify at least three orthographic choices in a public text
Explain how a notation choice helps the intended reader
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a public notice or pamphlet and ask them to mark notation choices that affect readability.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Accurate transcription of source titles and names
topics.japaneseName: 資料名・人名を正確に転記する
topics.description: Copy source titles, personal names, place names, dates, and publication details accurately when preparing inquiry notes or reports.
evidence: Transcribe a title or name without changing kanji, kana, punctuation, or order
Check a copied source detail against the original
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to copy bibliographic details from a source and verify them against the original.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Secondary-school notation conventions
topics.japaneseName: 中学校の表記・記号の約束
topics.description: Use notation conventions for grammar labels, examples, emphasis, brackets, ellipses, and cited words in secondary Japanese tasks.
evidence: Explain what a notation mark or bracket is doing in a worksheet or text
Use a convention consistently in notes or a short explanation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a class handout and explain the purpose of several notation conventions.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Peer-editing symbols and revision marks
topics.japaneseName: 相互推敲の記号と修正記号
topics.description: Use simple editing marks, margin comments, insertion marks, and deletion marks to support peer revision without rewriting the whole draft.
evidence: Use revision marks to suggest an insertion, deletion, or movement
Explain a peer-editing mark so the writer can revise independently
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to mark a peer paragraph with limited revision symbols and explain each mark.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji, abbreviations, and symbols in academic texts
topics.japaneseName: 学習文の漢字・略語・記号を読む
topics.description: Interpret kanji compounds, abbreviations, symbols, and notation shortcuts in secondary explanatory and subject-area texts.
evidence: Identify whether a compact written form is a kanji compound, abbreviation, or symbol
Use context to restate the compact form in fuller words
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a subject-area paragraph and ask them to explain compact written forms that carry important meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Font variation and public notation choices
topics.japaneseName: 字体・フォントと公共文書の表記
topics.description: Recognize kanji and kana across print, handwriting, font variation, emphasis styles, and public-document notation choices.
evidence: Match the same word across different fonts or handwriting styles
Explain why a public text might choose a particular notation style
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare the same public phrase in several fonts or notation styles and identify what stays constant.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Digital source transcription accuracy
topics.japaneseName: デジタル資料の正確な転記
topics.description: Transcribe digital source information accurately, preserving names, titles, dates, URLs, punctuation, and specialized notation.
evidence: Transcribe a digital source detail without losing punctuation or order
Check a copied detail against the original screen or page
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copy digital source details into notes and verify them against the original.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Awareness of orthography in classical quotations
topics.japaneseName: 古典引用の表記への気付き
topics.description: Notice that classical quotations, proverbs, and older expressions may use unfamiliar kana-kanji balance, wording, or punctuation conventions.
evidence: Identify an orthographic feature that differs from ordinary contemporary prose
Use notes or glosses to avoid misreading an older written form
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare a classical or proverb quotation with a contemporary explanation and mark notation differences.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using support to read classical kana and word boundaries
topics.japaneseName: 注を使って古典の仮名と語の切れ目を読む
topics.description: Use teacher notes, glosses, punctuation, and spacing support to identify word boundaries and kana forms in introductory classical Japanese.
evidence: Mark likely word boundaries in a supported classical passage
Use a note or gloss to explain an unfamiliar kana or word form
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a short supported classical passage and ask them to mark word boundaries and notation supports.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Introductory kanbun markup as reading support
topics.japaneseName: 漢文訓読の記号を読む手がかりにする
topics.description: Recognize basic kanbun reading marks, return cues, and annotation layout as supports for reading order rather than ordinary punctuation.
evidence: Point out a kanbun mark or annotation and explain its reading-support role
Follow a simple marked reading order with teacher support
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to inspect a short marked kanbun excerpt and explain how the marks support reading order.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Copyediting citation punctuation in argument writing
topics.japaneseName: 論述文の引用符と句読点を校正する
topics.description: Check quotation marks, source labels, punctuation, and sentence boundaries in an argument draft that uses textual evidence.
evidence: Correct citation or quotation punctuation in an argument draft
Explain how the correction separates the writer’s claim from source words
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to proofread an argument paragraph for quotation, citation, and punctuation clarity.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using a style guide for public text
topics.japaneseName: 公共的文章のスタイルガイドを使う
topics.description: Apply a simple style guide to public-facing writing by checking notation, punctuation, headings, numerals, citations, and consistency.
evidence: Apply a style-guide rule to revise a public text draft
Explain why a consistency rule helps the intended reader
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to use a short style checklist to revise a public-information paragraph.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Transcribing speech for media analysis
topics.japaneseName: メディア分析のために発話を書き起こす
topics.description: Transcribe a short spoken excerpt for analysis by representing pauses, emphasis, quoted words, and register without over-cleaning the speech.
evidence: Produce a short transcript that preserves important pauses or emphasis
Mark one place where transcription choices affect interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to transcribe a brief speech excerpt and explain a notation choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Verifying advanced kanji and orthography in public prose
topics.japaneseName: 公共的文章の漢字・表記を確認する
topics.description: Use dictionaries, official references, and context to verify kanji, okurigana, terminology, and notation choices in public prose.
evidence: Verify a kanji or okurigana choice with a reference source
Explain why the verified form fits the register and audience
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to check several advanced written forms in a public text draft using reference sources.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Punctuation and scope in logical prose
topics.japaneseName: 論理的文章の句読点と係り受けの範囲
topics.description: Analyze how punctuation, clause boundaries, and modifier scope affect the interpretation of definitions, claims, and qualifications in logical prose.
evidence: Explain how punctuation changes the scope of a modifier or qualification
Revise punctuation to reduce ambiguity in a logical paragraph
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two punctuated versions of a sentence and explain the difference in scope.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Transcribing classical annotations and reading marks
topics.japaneseName: 古典の注・訓点を正確に写す
topics.description: Accurately transcribe classical-text annotations, reading marks, source titles, and glosses so later interpretation uses the correct evidence.
evidence: Copy a classical annotation or reading mark without changing its position or function
Check the copied annotation against the source text
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to transcribe a short annotated classical excerpt and verify notation details.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Academic quotation and citation format control
topics.japaneseName: 学術的な引用と出典表示の形式管理
topics.description: Control quotation boundaries, citation format, source labels, page or section references, and paraphrase boundaries in academic prose.
evidence: Format a short quotation and citation consistently
Distinguish source words from paraphrase and student commentary
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a source-based paragraph for quotation and citation format.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Publication copyediting for style consistency
topics.japaneseName: 公開文章の表記統一と校正
topics.description: Copyedit an extended public or academic text for consistent notation, terminology, punctuation, headings, citations, and register.
evidence: Create or apply a consistency checklist for an extended text
Resolve a notation or terminology inconsistency with a reason
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copyedit a multi-section draft and document three consistency decisions.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Multiscript notation and accessibility choices
topics.japaneseName: 複数表記とアクセシビリティへの配慮
topics.description: Choose kanji, kana, furigana, numerals, romanization, emphasis, and layout conventions with attention to audience access and register.
evidence: Explain how script or notation choice affects accessibility for an intended audience
Revise a public text to improve accessibility without weakening register
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to adapt a public-facing text for a specified audience and justify notation choices.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reference collation in final proofing
topics.japaneseName: 最終校正で出典を照合する
topics.description: Before publication or submission, collate quotations, paraphrases, citations, names, titles, dates, and terminology against original sources.
evidence: Check a citation or quotation against its source and record the result
Correct a source-detail mismatch without changing the argument’s claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to perform a final source-collation pass on a research paragraph or short paper.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing a favorite story detail with a reason
topics.japaneseName: 好きな場面を理由とともに選ぶ
topics.description: Choose a favorite picture, word, character action, or scene from a story and give a simple reason linked to the text.
evidence: Point to a favorite detail in a story or picture book
Give one reason that refers to the story rather than only personal preference
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to choose a favorite story detail and say why that detail mattered in the story.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Seasonal words in poems and stories
topics.japaneseName: 詩や物語の季節の言葉
topics.description: Notice words that evoke seasons, weather, school events, and everyday customs in poems, songs, and short stories.
evidence: Find a word that shows season, weather, or event context
Tell what feeling or scene the seasonal word helps create
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to find a seasonal or event word in a poem or story and explain the scene it suggests.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on book choice and reading enjoyment
topics.japaneseName: 読んだ本を選び楽しさを振り返る
topics.description: Say what made a text enjoyable, easy, difficult, surprising, or worth rereading after a shared or independent reading.
evidence: Name one reason for choosing or liking a book
Tell whether the text felt easy, hard, funny, surprising, or worth rereading
topics.assessmentPrompt: After reading, ask the child to say what kind of book they chose and what they noticed about their reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Character change and cause in a story
topics.japaneseName: 人物の変化ときっかけを読む
topics.description: Identify how a character changes across a story and connect the change to an event, action, or dialogue clue.
evidence: Describe how a character was different before and after an event
Name the event or words that helped explain the change
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to explain how a character changed and what in the story caused that change.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Greeting and classroom register by situation
topics.japaneseName: 場面に合ったあいさつと言葉遣い
topics.description: Choose greeting, thanks, apology, and classroom expressions that fit listener, time, and situation.
evidence: Match a greeting or classroom expression to a situation
Explain why a more polite or more casual expression fits the listener
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child classroom situations and ask which expression fits each listener and why.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading response with one praise and one question
topics.japaneseName: 読書感想のよいところと問い
topics.description: Respond to a text by naming one part that worked well or felt interesting and one question for rereading or discussion.
evidence: Write or say one interesting part from the text
Ask one question that could guide rereading or peer talk
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to give one star and one question after a short reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Images and sound words in poetry
topics.japaneseName: 詩のイメージと音の言葉
topics.description: Identify words in a poem that create sound, rhythm, image, movement, or feeling and explain their effect when read aloud.
evidence: Choose a word or phrase that creates a strong image or sound
Explain how reading aloud changes the feeling of the phrase
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to select a phrase from a poem and explain its image or sound effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using proverbs and idioms in context
topics.japaneseName: ことわざ・慣用句を場面で使う
topics.description: Understand a common proverb or idiom as a fixed expression and choose a situation where it could be used appropriately.
evidence: Explain the situation meaning of an idiom or proverb in simple terms
Match a fixed expression to a story or classroom situation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a proverb or idiom and ask what situation it fits and what it means there.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading log with strategy note
topics.japaneseName: 読書記録と方略メモ
topics.description: Keep a brief reading log that records title, interesting part, unknown word strategy, and one rereading or discussion note.
evidence: Record a title and one text-based response in a log
Name the strategy used for an unknown word or confusing part
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to complete a reading-log entry after independent or shared reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Climax and resolution in narrative
topics.japaneseName: 物語の山場と結末を読む
topics.description: Identify a turning point, climax, and resolution in a story and explain how scenes build toward them.
evidence: Mark the scene where the story’s problem or feeling changes strongly
Explain how the ending responds to the turning point
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to map a story into buildup, turning point, and resolution with evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Dialect, standard language, and character voice
topics.japaneseName: 方言・共通語と人物の語り口
topics.description: Notice how dialect, standard language, speech endings, and word choice can show character, place, relationship, or situation.
evidence: Identify a phrase that sounds regional, casual, polite, or character-specific
Explain what the wording suggests about place, relationship, or speaker
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to compare two character lines and explain how language changes the voice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing a peer interpretation note
topics.japaneseName: 友だちの読みと自分の読みを比べる
topics.description: Compare a peer’s interpretation with one’s own by naming shared evidence, different evidence, or a different viewpoint.
evidence: State one similarity or difference between two interpretations
Point to the evidence each interpretation used
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to compare their reading note with a peer’s note and identify evidence for each.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Narrator distance and reliability introduction
topics.japaneseName: 語り手との距離と信頼性への気付き
topics.description: Notice whether a narrator is close to events, limited in knowledge, biased, humorous, or inviting readers to question the telling.
evidence: Identify what the narrator seems to know or not know
Use a narration detail to explain whether readers should fully trust the telling
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to describe the narrator’s distance from events and cite one clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Register shift between speech and letter writing
topics.japaneseName: 話し言葉と手紙の言葉遣いの切り替え
topics.description: Shift between spoken classroom language and written letter language while keeping audience, purpose, and relationship in view.
evidence: Identify wording that fits speech but not a letter, or the reverse
Revise one sentence so it better fits the audience and medium
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to adapt a spoken message into a short letter sentence and explain the wording change.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reviewing a theme response for evidence
topics.japaneseName: 主題についての考えを根拠で見直す
topics.description: Review a literary response by checking whether a theme or message claim is supported with scene, character, or language evidence.
evidence: Underline the theme claim and the evidence used to support it
Add or replace evidence when a claim is weak
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to revise a theme response by checking whether the evidence actually supports the claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Connecting themes and motifs across works
topics.japaneseName: 複数作品の主題とモチーフをつなぐ
topics.description: Compare how two literary works develop related themes, repeated images, motifs, or character choices.
evidence: Name a motif or theme that appears across two works
Explain one similarity and one difference in how the works develop it
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to compare two texts for a related theme or motif and explain the evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical sayings and modern use
topics.japaneseName: 古くからの言い回しと現代での使い方
topics.description: Connect familiar proverbs, idioms, and older sayings to modern situations while noticing that form and meaning may not map literally.
evidence: Explain a classical or older saying in a modern situation
Identify one word or expression that feels older, fixed, or culturally inherited
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to explain how a familiar saying could fit a modern classroom or story situation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Matching reading goal and strategy to genre
topics.japaneseName: ジャンルに合わせた読む目的と方略
topics.description: Set a reading purpose and choose a strategy that fits genre, such as poem, story, report, biography, or classical saying.
evidence: State a reading purpose before reading a selected genre
Choose a strategy that fits the genre and explain the choice
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to select a reading strategy before reading two different genres and compare the choices.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Perspective and time structure in literature
topics.japaneseName: 視点と時間構成を読む
topics.description: Analyze how viewpoint, flashback, order of events, pacing, and omitted information shape a literary text.
evidence: Map event order against narration order
Explain how viewpoint or time order changes the reader’s understanding
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map narrative time and explain one effect of the chosen order.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Regional language and identity in texts
topics.japaneseName: 地域の言葉と人物・場面の関係
topics.description: Interpret how regional language, standard language, and character voice contribute to identity, setting, community, and reader response.
evidence: Identify a regional or socially marked expression
Explain what the expression suggests about identity, place, or relationship
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a passage for language variation and explain its role in characterization or setting.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Interpretation claim-evidence log
topics.japaneseName: 解釈の主張と根拠の記録
topics.description: Keep a log that pairs interpretation claims with evidence, uncertainty, and revision notes during literary reading.
evidence: Record an interpretation claim with at least one evidence detail
Revise the claim after discussion or rereading
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to maintain a claim-evidence log for a story or poem and update one entry after discussion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracking symbols, motifs, and pattern changes
topics.japaneseName: 象徴・モチーフ・反復の変化を追う
topics.description: Track recurring images, symbols, phrases, or situations and explain how their meaning changes across a literary work.
evidence: Collect repeated details across a work
Explain how the meaning of a repeated detail shifts by context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to track a motif or symbol across several scenes and explain the pattern.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Honorifics and character relationships
topics.japaneseName: 敬語と人物関係の読み取り
topics.description: Use respectful, humble, and polite forms in dialogue to infer status, distance, conflict, solidarity, or changing relationships.
evidence: Identify an honorific or speech-level choice in dialogue
Explain what it suggests about the relationship or situation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze a short dialogue for speech level and relationship cues.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on interpretive moves in seminar
topics.japaneseName: 読解討議で使った解釈の動きを振り返る
topics.description: Reflect on seminar participation by naming moves such as quoting evidence, linking ideas, asking a question, revising a claim, or summarizing tension.
evidence: Name one interpretive move used during discussion
Identify one moment when evidence or another speaker changed the interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to complete a brief seminar reflection focused on interpretive moves, not general effort.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Theme continuity between classical and modern texts
topics.japaneseName: 古典と現代文の主題のつながり
topics.description: Compare how a classical and a modern text approach related themes, values, emotions, or human relationships.
evidence: Identify a related theme or emotion in one classical and one modern text
Explain how expression differs across eras while the concern remains connected
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare a supported classical excerpt with a modern text on a related theme.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical paraphrase with culture note
topics.japaneseName: 古典の現代語訳と文化メモ
topics.description: Paraphrase a supported classical phrase into contemporary Japanese and add a short note about custom, value, setting, or expression.
evidence: Produce a contemporary paraphrase of a supported classical phrase
Add a culture note that explains context rather than only word meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to paraphrase a classical line and add a culture note about the expression or situation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Self-monitoring during supported classical reading
topics.japaneseName: 古典を読むときの自己点検
topics.description: Monitor comprehension during classical reading by marking unknown forms, using notes, checking paraphrase, and separating literal support from interpretation.
evidence: Mark one confusing form or phrase and select a support strategy
Revise a paraphrase after checking a note or discussion
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a supported classical excerpt with confusion marks, supports used, and a revised paraphrase.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Contemporary literature and social context
topics.japaneseName: 現代文学と社会的文脈
topics.description: Interpret contemporary literary scenes by connecting narrative technique, language choice, social context, and issue framing.
evidence: Connect a literary detail to a social or cultural context
Explain how narrative technique shapes the issue or situation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze a contemporary literary scene with one textual detail and one context note.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Genre history and media culture
topics.japaneseName: ジャンルの歴史とメディア文化
topics.description: Analyze how older genres, rhetorical patterns, and media forms shape contemporary expression and reader expectations.
evidence: Identify a genre convention inherited or adapted in a contemporary text
Explain how medium changes the convention or audience effect
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare a contemporary text with an inherited genre or rhetorical pattern.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Portfolio reflection on literary context
topics.japaneseName: 文学と文脈を結ぶポートフォリオ振り返り
topics.description: Use portfolio evidence to reflect on how context, source notes, peer discussion, and rereading changed literary interpretation.
evidence: Select portfolio evidence showing an interpretation changed
Explain which context or discussion evidence caused the change
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose two portfolio artifacts and explain how their interpretation developed.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Applying a critical lens as literary method
topics.japaneseName: 批評の観点を方法として使う
topics.description: Apply a named critical lens or interpretive method to a literary text while distinguishing method, evidence, and personal response.
evidence: Name the lens or method used for interpretation
Select evidence that the lens makes newly visible or important
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to interpret a passage using a critical lens and explain what the lens changes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical intertextual allusion
topics.japaneseName: 古典の引用・踏まえを読む
topics.description: Recognize when a modern or classical text invokes an earlier phrase, image, genre, poem, or kanbun pattern and explain the added meaning.
evidence: Identify a possible allusion or inherited phrase
Explain what the earlier source or genre adds to the interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze a passage with an allusion or inherited expression and explain its interpretive effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising interpretation from feedback
topics.japaneseName: フィードバックで解釈を改める
topics.description: Use teacher, peer, or secondary-source feedback to revise a literary interpretation for evidence, scope, alternative readings, and terminology.
evidence: Classify feedback as evidence, scope, terminology, or alternative interpretation
Revise an interpretation and explain what changed
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an interpretation paragraph using feedback and document the revision decision.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Capstone literary-cultural synthesis
topics.japaneseName: 文学と文化の総合的解釈
topics.description: Synthesize multiple literary, classical, cultural, and critical sources into a coherent interpretation with explicit source roles.
evidence: Map how at least two texts or sources contribute to one interpretation
Explain a tension or contrast between sources rather than flattening them
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to produce an interpretation map connecting literary text, cultural context, and critical source.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Language, identity, and public discourse
topics.japaneseName: 言葉・アイデンティティ・公共的言説
topics.description: Analyze how language choices in public, literary, and cultural discourse construct identity, inclusion, exclusion, authority, or community.
evidence: Identify a language choice that frames identity or community
Explain the social or cultural effect of the wording in context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze a public or literary passage for language choices that construct identity or community.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Transferring interpretation strategies across genres
topics.japaneseName: 解釈方略をジャンル間で転用する
topics.description: Reflect on how interpretation strategies transfer across literary, classical, informational, public, and multimodal texts in a final portfolio.
evidence: Name a strategy that worked across at least two genres
Explain a situation where the strategy had to be adapted or limited
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to use portfolio evidence to explain how one reading strategy transferred across genres.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Cohesion devices in explanatory prose
topics.japaneseName: 説明文の結束表現
topics.description: Notice how demonstratives, repeated terms, conjunctions, and sentence endings connect ideas across a secondary-school explanatory paragraph.
evidence: Mark at least three cohesion devices in a paragraph
Explain what each device connects for the reader
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate cohesion devices in an explanatory paragraph and describe how they guide the reader.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Separating source summary from personal stance
topics.japaneseName: 資料の要約と自分の立場の区別
topics.description: Summarize an informational source accurately before adding personal evaluation, agreement, or doubt.
evidence: Write a neutral two-sentence source summary
Add a separate sentence that clearly signals personal stance or question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a short source and ask for a neutral summary followed by a separately marked response.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparison response paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 比較して述べる応答段落
topics.description: Write a paragraph that compares two texts, examples, or views with one shared point and one difference supported by evidence.
evidence: State one comparison criterion
Use evidence from both items being compared
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write one comparison paragraph using a shared criterion and evidence from two short texts.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Question chains in discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いで問いをつなぐ
topics.description: Use follow-up questions to connect a peer comment to evidence, definition, example, or implication.
evidence: Ask a follow-up question that refers to a peer contribution
Explain whether the question asks for evidence, definition, example, or implication
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a discussion, ask the student to contribute a follow-up question and label the kind of thinking it invites.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for evidence gaps
topics.japaneseName: 根拠の不足を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen to a short claim and identify where the speaker needs a reason, example, source, or clearer definition.
evidence: Name one unsupported part of a spoken claim
Suggest what kind of evidence would strengthen it
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play or read a brief oral claim and ask the student to identify an evidence gap and a possible support type.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical sound and rhythm entry points
topics.japaneseName: 古典の音とリズムへの入口
topics.description: Notice repeated sounds, rhythm, and familiar expressions in short classical sayings, poems, or quoted lines without full grammar analysis.
evidence: Read a short classical quotation aloud with support
Identify one sound, rhythm, or familiar expression that differs from modern prose
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to read a short classical quotation aloud and describe one rhythm or expression they noticed.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Before-and-after revision log
topics.japaneseName: 推敲の前後を記録する
topics.description: Keep a brief record of a draft sentence before revision, the change made, and the reason for the change.
evidence: Save one before-and-after sentence pair
Explain the revision reason using a criterion such as clarity, evidence, or connection
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise one sentence and record what changed and why.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Setting and mood evidence in literature
topics.japaneseName: 文学の場面設定と雰囲気の根拠
topics.description: Explain how setting details, weather, time, place, and sensory words create mood or expectation in a literary text.
evidence: Select two setting details from a passage
Explain how those details shape mood or expectation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a literary passage and ask for two setting details that influence the mood.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Clause compression and nominalization
topics.japaneseName: 節の圧縮と名詞化
topics.description: Recognize how clauses are compressed into noun phrases or nominalized expressions in dense informational and literary sentences.
evidence: Underline a compressed clause or nominalized expression
Paraphrase it into a fuller clause without changing the core meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to expand a nominalized expression in a dense sentence and explain the information it packages.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Integrating infographic and prose evidence
topics.japaneseName: 図解と本文の根拠を統合する
topics.description: Read an infographic, chart, or diagram together with prose and explain how the visual evidence supports, qualifies, or complicates the text.
evidence: Cite one prose detail and one visual detail
State whether the visual supports, qualifies, or complicates the prose claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to interpret a short prose passage with an infographic and explain the relationship between them.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising explanation for audience knowledge
topics.japaneseName: 読者の知識に合わせて説明を直す
topics.description: Revise explanatory writing by adding background, defining terms, or simplifying dense wording for a specified reader.
evidence: Identify what the target reader may not know
Revise one sentence or paragraph to bridge that knowledge gap
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a draft explanation and a target reader, then ask for one audience-focused revision.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Facilitator summary at a turning point
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いの転換点で要約する
topics.description: Summarize the current state of discussion when a group shifts from exploring views to deciding criteria, evidence, or next action.
evidence: Summarize at least two contributions without taking over the discussion
State the next question or criterion the group should address
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a small discussion, ask the student to pause the group, summarize the current point, and propose the next question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracking concession and contrast while listening
topics.japaneseName: 譲歩と対比を聞き取る
topics.description: Track spoken markers that show concession, contrast, qualification, or change of stance during explanation or discussion.
evidence: Note a concession or contrast marker from speech
Explain how it changes the speaker meaning or stance
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play or read a short exchange and ask the student to identify a concession or contrast move and its effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Honorific effects in explanation and narrative
topics.japaneseName: 説明や物語における敬語の効果
topics.description: Explain how respectful, humble, or polite forms shape relationships, distance, and authority in explanatory or narrative passages.
evidence: Identify an honorific form in context
Explain what relationship, distance, or social effect it creates
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate honorific expressions in a passage and describe their effect on reader interpretation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Inquiry log source decision
topics.japaneseName: 探究記録で資料選択を説明する
topics.description: Record why a source was selected, rejected, or kept for comparison during inquiry reading.
evidence: Write a one-line source decision with a reason
Use a criterion such as relevance, reliability, recency, perspective, or evidence type
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to review three sources and log why one should be used, compared, or rejected.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Poetic image and symbol networks
topics.japaneseName: 詩のイメージと象徴のつながり
topics.description: Track recurring images, colors, sounds, or objects across a poem and explain how they build a pattern of meaning.
evidence: Mark at least three related images or symbols
Explain the pattern they create rather than treating each item separately
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a poem for repeated images and explain the network of meaning they form.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Gradations of certainty in modality
topics.japaneseName: モダリティによる確かさの段階
topics.description: Distinguish expressions that show certainty, probability, obligation, possibility, doubt, and recommendation in argument and commentary.
evidence: Sort modality expressions by strength or certainty
Revise a claim by adjusting modality to fit the evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to rank modality expressions in a passage and revise one claim for appropriate certainty.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparative reading of media framing
topics.japaneseName: メディアの枠組みを比較して読む
topics.description: Compare two media texts on the same issue and identify how headline, evidence order, image, or wording frames the issue differently.
evidence: Name one framing choice in each text
Explain how the choices guide reader attention or evaluation differently
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student two short media texts on one issue and ask for a comparison of framing choices.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Section functions in argumentative essays
topics.japaneseName: 意見文の各部分の役割
topics.description: Plan argument sections by function: issue framing, claim, reasons, evidence, counterargument, rebuttal, and conclusion.
evidence: Label the function of each section in an argument outline
Move or revise one section so the argument sequence becomes clearer
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate an argument outline for section function and improve one weak sequence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Debate flow note-taking
topics.japaneseName: 討論の流れを記録する
topics.description: Record claims, evidence, objections, replies, and unresolved questions during a formal or classroom debate.
evidence: Use a flow note structure for at least two turns on each side
Identify one unresolved question after the exchange
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a debate, ask the student to take flow notes and name the strongest unresolved issue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evaluating evidence in rebuttal while listening
topics.japaneseName: 反論の根拠を聞いて評価する
topics.description: Listen to a rebuttal and judge whether it directly answers the opposing claim with relevant evidence.
evidence: Identify the opposed claim and the rebuttal evidence
Judge whether the rebuttal answers the claim directly or shifts the topic
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play or read a rebuttal and ask the student to evaluate whether the evidence addresses the original claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Function-word patterns in supported kobun
topics.japaneseName: 古文の機能語の型
topics.description: Recognize recurring particles, auxiliary verbs, and sentence-ending patterns in supported classical passages.
evidence: Mark a recurring classical function word or ending
Use a note or gloss to explain its approximate role in the passage
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a supported kobun excerpt for repeated function-word patterns.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Argument revision conference reflection
topics.japaneseName: 意見文の推敲面談を振り返る
topics.description: Use peer or teacher conference feedback to identify one argument-level revision and explain why it improves reasoning.
evidence: Record one feedback point about claim, reason, evidence, or rebuttal
Explain the planned revision in terms of argument strength
topics.assessmentPrompt: After feedback, ask the student to write a short reflection naming one argument-level revision and its reason.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Narrative irony and distance
topics.japaneseName: 語りの皮肉と距離
topics.description: Identify when a narrator, speaker, or character wording creates irony, distance, or double meaning in a literary passage.
evidence: Select wording that signals irony or distance
Explain the difference between surface meaning and implied meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a literary passage for wording that creates irony or distance.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Register and audience as rhetorical choices
topics.japaneseName: 文体と読者を修辞的に選ぶ
topics.description: Explain how sentence ending, vocabulary, quotation style, and modality change when a public text addresses different readers.
evidence: Identify two register choices in a public text
Revise one sentence for a different audience and explain the rhetorical change
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to adapt a public-text sentence for a different audience and justify the register changes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rhetorical structure of public statements
topics.japaneseName: 公的な声明文の修辞構造
topics.description: Analyze how public statements frame occasion, responsibility, evidence, values, action, and audience relationship.
evidence: Label major rhetorical sections in a public statement
Explain how one section manages audience trust or action
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a public statement and ask them to label its rhetorical structure and one audience effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Problem-method-result inquiry report
topics.japaneseName: 課題・方法・結果で書く探究報告
topics.description: Write an inquiry report section that separates the research problem, method of source gathering, result, and remaining question.
evidence: Include labeled or clearly separated problem, method, and result moves
State one limitation or remaining question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to draft a short inquiry report using problem, method, result, and remaining-question moves.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Role rotation in issue roundtables
topics.japaneseName: 課題討議で役割を交替する
topics.description: Rotate roles such as summarizer, evidence checker, questioner, and perspective connector during a contemporary-issue roundtable.
evidence: Perform one assigned discussion role with evidence from the issue materials
Switch roles and explain how the new role changes listening priorities
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a roundtable, assign rotating roles and ask the student to reflect on how each role shaped their contribution.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing news and commentary frames while listening
topics.japaneseName: ニュースと解説の枠組みを聞き比べる
topics.description: Listen to informational and commentary-style media on one issue and compare how fact selection, tone, and evaluation differ.
evidence: Note one fact-selection or tone difference
Explain which segment reports, explains, evaluates, or persuades
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play two short media excerpts on the same issue and ask the student to compare their frames and purposes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical quotation in public discourse
topics.japaneseName: 公的表現における古典引用
topics.description: Explain how classical sayings, poems, or inherited expressions are used to add authority, shared memory, irony, or cultural resonance in modern public discourse.
evidence: Identify a classical or inherited expression in a modern context
Explain its rhetorical or cultural effect for the audience
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze a modern passage that quotes or alludes to inherited language and explain the effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on the inquiry source trail
topics.japaneseName: 探究の資料探索過程を振り返る
topics.description: Reflect on how search terms, source types, and evaluation criteria changed during an inquiry task.
evidence: Show at least two stages of source searching
Explain one change that improved source relevance or credibility
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a source-trail reflection after completing an inquiry search.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Short literary contextualization analysis
topics.japaneseName: 文学作品を文脈化して短く分析する
topics.description: Use one historical, social, media, or language-culture context to explain a literary choice without reducing the work to background information.
evidence: Name one relevant context and one textual detail
Explain how the context clarifies the detail while preserving literary interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a short analysis connecting one literary detail with one contextual factor.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Definition boundaries in argument
topics.japaneseName: 論述における定義の範囲管理
topics.description: Control how key terms are defined, narrowed, contrasted, or excluded in logical and academic argument.
evidence: Identify the boundary of a key term in an argument
Revise a claim to define or limit a term more precisely
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to find an undefined key term in an argument and revise the sentence to control its boundary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading academic abstract structure
topics.japaneseName: 学術的要旨の構造を読む
topics.description: Identify problem, method, result, implication, and limitation moves in an academic abstract or abstract-like summary.
evidence: Label at least three abstract moves
Explain how a limitation or implication changes the scope of the claim
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student an abstract-like passage and ask them to label its rhetorical moves.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Critical response with counterinterpretation
topics.japaneseName: 別解をふまえた批評的応答
topics.description: Write a critical response that presents an interpretation, acknowledges a plausible counterinterpretation, and explains why the chosen reading is stronger or narrower.
evidence: State one interpretation and one plausible counterinterpretation
Use textual evidence to qualify or defend the chosen interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a critical response paragraph that includes and evaluates a counterinterpretation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Designing colloquium questions
topics.japaneseName: コロキウムの問いを設計する
topics.description: Design interpretive or logical questions that invite evidence-based exchange rather than simple opinion sharing.
evidence: Write two questions that require textual or source evidence
Explain the kind of reasoning each question is meant to elicit
topics.assessmentPrompt: Before a colloquium, ask the student to prepare evidence-seeking questions and explain their purpose.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for method, evidence, and limitation
topics.japaneseName: 方法・根拠・限界を聞き分ける
topics.description: Listen to a presentation and distinguish how the speaker gathered evidence, what the evidence shows, and what limitation remains.
evidence: Note method, evidence, and limitation separately
Ask one follow-up question about a limitation or evidence scope
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a presentation, ask the student to complete method-evidence-limitation notes and pose a follow-up question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing kanbun paraphrases
topics.japaneseName: 漢文の現代語訳を比較する
topics.description: Compare two supported modern paraphrases of a kanbun passage and explain how word order, omitted subjects, or modal choices affect meaning.
evidence: Identify one difference between two paraphrases
Explain how the difference affects interpretation or tone
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a supported kanbun passage with two paraphrases and ask for one interpretive comparison.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on interpretive assumptions
topics.japaneseName: 解釈の前提を振り返る
topics.description: Identify an assumption behind a student interpretation and revise or qualify the interpretation after considering alternative evidence.
evidence: Name one assumption behind an interpretation
Revise or qualify the interpretation based on alternative evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: After discussion, ask the student to identify one interpretive assumption and write a revised claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using literary theory terms in context
topics.japaneseName: 文学理論の語を文脈で使う
topics.description: Use terms such as narrator, symbol, motif, context, genre, and discourse only when they clarify a specific textual reading.
evidence: Define a critical term in student-friendly language
Apply it to a specific textual detail without replacing interpretation with labels
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to apply one critical term to a passage and explain what the term helps them see.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Metadiscourse for navigating extended argument
topics.japaneseName: 長い論述を導くメタ談話
topics.description: Use and interpret phrases that announce scope, sequence, contrast, concession, source role, and conclusion in extended argument.
evidence: Identify metadiscourse that guides reader navigation
Revise an extended argument section to add or improve navigation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate metadiscourse in an extended argument and revise one transition or signpost.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading multi-source public controversies
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料で公共的論争を読む
topics.description: Read a public controversy across official, journalistic, expert, and citizen sources while tracking source role and value conflict.
evidence: Classify at least three source roles
Explain one value conflict or evidence conflict across sources
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map sources in a public controversy and explain where evidence or values conflict.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Integrating sections in a capstone paper
topics.japaneseName: 卒業論文的文章の章節統合
topics.description: Revise section openings, endings, and transitions so a capstone literary, cultural, or logical argument reads as one coherent paper.
evidence: Identify a weak section boundary
Revise the boundary to state source role, claim progression, or next question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise two section boundaries in a capstone paper and explain the integration problem solved.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Moderator notes for symposium synthesis
topics.japaneseName: シンポジウムを統合する司会メモ
topics.description: Take moderator notes that connect speakers by claim, evidence type, method, agreement, tension, and unresolved question.
evidence: Record links among at least three speaker contributions
Use the notes to pose a synthesis question or transition
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a symposium, ask the student to prepare moderator notes and use them for a synthesis transition.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evaluating cross-examination question quality
topics.japaneseName: 反対尋問の問いの質を評価する
topics.description: Evaluate whether questions in a defense, debate, or symposium clarify evidence, expose assumptions, test method, or merely challenge tone.
evidence: Classify a question by its reasoning function
Revise a weak question into one that tests evidence, method, or assumption
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a set of cross-examination questions and ask them to classify and improve one.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical-modern language identity synthesis
topics.japaneseName: 古典と現代を通した言語とアイデンティティの統合
topics.description: Synthesize how inherited expressions, classical texts, dialects, registers, and public discourse shape language identity across time.
evidence: Connect one classical or inherited expression with one modern language-identity issue
Use textual evidence rather than only personal opinion
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write or present a synthesis claim connecting classical-modern expression and language identity.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Final portfolio transfer claim
topics.japaneseName: 最終ポートフォリオで学びの転用を述べる
topics.description: Use portfolio evidence to claim how a reading, writing, speaking, or inquiry strategy transfers to future academic, civic, or workplace language tasks.
evidence: Select two portfolio artifacts from different genres or modes
Write a transfer claim linking the artifacts to future language use
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose portfolio artifacts and write a transfer claim about future language tasks.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing canonical and contemporary readings
topics.japaneseName: 古典的作品と現代的読みの比較
topics.description: Compare a canonical or widely taught literary interpretation with a contemporary reading that shifts focus, values, or language identity.
evidence: State the focus of each interpretation
Explain how a contemporary lens changes what counts as important evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two interpretations of a literary work and explain how their evidence priorities differ.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Finding word chunks in spaceless kana text
topics.japaneseName: 分かち書きのないかな文のまとまり
topics.description: Use familiar words, particles, and sentence meaning to read short kana sentences without relying on spaces.
evidence: Read a short kana sentence in meaningful chunks
Point to the particle or familiar word that helped find a boundary
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a short kana sentence aloud and show where the word chunks are.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Number and calendar kanji as meaning signs
topics.japaneseName: 数と暦の漢字を意味で読む
topics.description: Recognize number, day, month, and simple calendar kanji as characters that carry meaning in dates, schedules, and classroom routines.
evidence: Read familiar number or calendar kanji in a date or schedule
Explain the meaning of the kanji in context
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a classroom schedule or calendar and ask which kanji show number, day, or month meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Action and feeling words in stories
topics.japaneseName: 物語の動きと気持ちの言葉
topics.description: Use verbs and feeling words to describe what a character does and how a character may feel in a familiar story.
evidence: Choose an action word and a feeling word for a character
Connect the feeling word to a picture, action, or spoken line
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a story, ask the child to name what a character did and what feeling word fits that moment.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Event connector words in retelling
topics.japaneseName: できごとをつなぐ言葉
topics.description: Use early sequence words such as first, then, and after that to connect events in a spoken or written retelling.
evidence: Retell two or three events using sequence language
Keep the order of events clear for a listener
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to retell a familiar story using words that show the event order.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Matching labels to factual details
topics.japaneseName: ラベルと事実を結びつける
topics.description: Use labels, arrows, and short captions in an informational picture to identify the named part or factual detail.
evidence: Match a label to the correct picture part
Tell one fact the label or caption gives
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show an illustrated informational page and ask the child to match labels to parts and say one fact.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Accurate copying of short sentences
topics.japaneseName: 短い文を正しく写す
topics.description: Copy a short sentence with readable kana, punctuation, and character order while checking for omitted or added characters.
evidence: Copy a short sentence with all characters in order
Check and correct one copying error when prompted
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to copy a short sentence and then check it against the model.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Partner retell with one remembered detail
topics.japaneseName: 友だちに一つの詳しさを入れて話す
topics.description: Tell a partner the main event from a story or experience and include one detail the partner can repeat back.
evidence: Tell a main event clearly to a partner
Include one detail that the partner can identify
topics.assessmentPrompt: Pair students and ask each child to retell one event with a detail the partner can repeat.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Checking an unknown kana word
topics.japaneseName: 読めないかな語を確かめる
topics.description: Use rereading, picture clues, a classmate, or a teacher to check an unfamiliar kana word instead of skipping it silently.
evidence: Name one way to check an unknown kana word
Use a checking strategy during a short reading
topics.assessmentPrompt: During reading, ask the child what they can do when a kana word is unclear and observe the strategy used.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Particle boundaries for fluent reading
topics.japaneseName: 助詞を手がかりにすらすら読む
topics.description: Use familiar particles to separate phrases and read mixed kana-kanji sentences with smoother phrasing.
evidence: Read a sentence aloud with phrase breaks near particles
Identify the particle that helped separate the phrase
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to mark helpful particles in a sentence before reading it aloud.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji meaning fields for home, school, and nature
topics.japaneseName: 家庭・学校・自然の漢字のまとまり
topics.description: Group taught kanji into familiar semantic fields and use the group to remember, read, and choose words.
evidence: Sort taught kanji or words into a semantic group
Explain why one word belongs in the group
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to sort familiar kanji words into home, school, nature, or another meaningful group.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Time-order words for recounts
topics.japaneseName: 順序を表す言葉で経験を話す
topics.description: Use time and order words to retell an experience or informational sequence clearly.
evidence: Use at least two order words in a recount
Keep the sequence understandable without teacher prompting
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to recount an experience using words that show when each part happened.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Direct speech and character voice
topics.japaneseName: 会話文と人物の声
topics.description: Notice direct speech in a story and explain what it shows about a character feeling, relationship, or action.
evidence: Find a direct-speech line in a story
Tell what the line shows about the character or situation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to choose one spoken line from a story and say what it tells the reader.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading a simple table result
topics.japaneseName: 簡単な表から結果を読む
topics.description: Read a simple class table or tally chart and state one accurate result using number or comparison language.
evidence: Point to the table cell or tally that supports the result
State one result without adding unsupported interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a class tally table and ask for one result the table shows.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising a sentence by adding one detail
topics.japaneseName: 一文に詳しさを加えて直す
topics.description: Revise a simple sentence by adding a when, where, how, or what-kind detail that helps the reader picture the event.
evidence: Identify a vague sentence in a draft
Add one relevant detail without changing the main event
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to choose one sentence in a draft and add a useful detail for the reader.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for sequence in instructions
topics.japaneseName: 手順を聞いて順番をとらえる
topics.description: Listen to two- or three-step classroom instructions and restate or perform them in order.
evidence: Repeat the steps in order
Perform the instruction sequence without reversing steps
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a short multi-step instruction and ask the child to repeat or carry out the steps in order.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Naming a reading goal before and after reading
topics.japaneseName: 読む前後にめあてを言う
topics.description: Say what to find out before reading and check afterward whether the goal was met or changed.
evidence: State a simple reading goal before reading
Say after reading whether the goal was met and why
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to name a reading goal before a short text and reflect on it afterward.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using component analogy for new kanji words
topics.japaneseName: 構成要素の類推で新しい漢字語を読む
topics.description: Use a familiar kanji component or word family to make a reasonable first attempt at reading or understanding a new taught word.
evidence: Name the familiar component or word family used as a clue
Check the attempt with context, dictionary, or teacher support
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show a new grade-level kanji word and ask what familiar component or word family may help.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Multiple meanings of common words from context
topics.japaneseName: 文脈でよく使う語の意味を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose the context-appropriate meaning of a familiar word that has more than one use in stories or explanations.
evidence: Identify two possible meanings of a familiar word
Choose the meaning that fits the sentence or paragraph
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give two sentences using the same word differently and ask the child to explain each meaning from context.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Context roles of ni and de
topics.japaneseName: 「に」「で」の文中での役割
topics.description: Notice common location, time, means, and target roles signaled by ni and de in age-appropriate sentences.
evidence: Identify whether a particle phrase tells place, time, means, or target
Explain how the particle phrase changes sentence meaning
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to sort particle phrases by the kind of information they add to the sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Character motive from text clues
topics.japaneseName: 叙述を手がかりに人物の理由を読む
topics.description: Infer why a character acts or speaks in a certain way using action, dialogue, and narration clues.
evidence: State a character motive in a sentence
Point to a text clue that supports the motive
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child why a character acted that way and which words in the story helped them decide.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing two explanatory paragraphs
topics.japaneseName: 二つの説明段落を比べる
topics.description: Compare two short explanatory paragraphs by question answered, main point, example, or order of information.
evidence: Name one way the paragraphs are alike or different
Use a sentence or heading from each paragraph as evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give two short explanatory paragraphs and ask the child to compare what each one explains.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source note in own words
topics.japaneseName: 資料メモを自分の言葉で書く
topics.description: Write a short note from one source using own words while preserving the factual meaning.
evidence: Record the source title or page
Write one note that is not copied word for word but keeps the fact accurate
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to read a short source sentence and make one own-words note card.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Ending sentence for a paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 段落の終わりの文
topics.description: Write a paragraph ending sentence that restates, concludes, or connects back to the topic without adding a new unrelated idea.
evidence: Identify the topic of the paragraph
Write an ending sentence that closes the paragraph coherently
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a short paragraph draft and ask them to add or revise the ending sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Asking a peer question after a presentation
topics.japaneseName: 発表後に友だちへ質問する
topics.description: Ask a question after a peer presentation that refers to a fact, example, order, or unclear point from the presentation.
evidence: Ask a question that refers to the presentation content
Explain whether the question asks for fact, example, order, or clarification
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a short presentation, ask the child to prepare and ask one content-linked question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Relation patterns inside kanji compounds
topics.japaneseName: 熟語の中の語の関係
topics.description: Notice common relationships inside kanji compounds, such as modifier-head, similar meaning, opposite meaning, or verb-object relationships.
evidence: Sort familiar compounds by relationship pattern
Explain how the kanji parts work together in one compound
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to group familiar kanji compounds by how their parts are related.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Abstract feeling and evaluation words
topics.japaneseName: 気持ちや評価を表す抽象語
topics.description: Use abstract words for feeling, value, quality, and evaluation when discussing stories, explanations, and peer writing.
evidence: Choose an abstract feeling or evaluation word that fits a passage
Support the word choice with a text or draft detail
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to choose an evaluation word for a passage or draft and give evidence for that choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sentence-ending style consistency
topics.japaneseName: 文末表現のそろえ方
topics.description: Notice and revise inconsistent sentence endings in a paragraph so the style matches purpose and audience.
evidence: Find an inconsistent sentence ending in a draft
Revise it to match the paragraph style
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a paragraph with mixed endings and ask them to revise for style consistency.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Viewpoint shift in retelling
topics.japaneseName: 視点を変えて物語を語り直す
topics.description: Explain how a story event sounds different when retold from another character viewpoint.
evidence: Retell one event from a different character viewpoint
Explain what information or feeling changed in the retelling
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to retell a story event from another character viewpoint and identify what changed.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Examples and non-examples in explanations
topics.japaneseName: 説明文の例と反例
topics.description: Use examples and non-examples to clarify the boundary of a concept in an explanatory text.
evidence: Identify an example and a non-example or contrast
Explain how the example clarifies the concept
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to find an example in an explanatory text and describe what it helps define.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Caption and credit for a visual source
topics.japaneseName: 図や写真に説明と出典をつける
topics.description: Add a clear caption and simple source credit when using a photo, diagram, or chart in a report or presentation.
evidence: Write a caption that explains what the visual shows
Record a simple source credit for the visual
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the child to add a caption and source credit to a visual in a report draft.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising for paragraph focus
topics.japaneseName: 段落の中心が伝わるように直す
topics.description: Revise a paragraph by removing, moving, or adding sentences so every sentence supports the paragraph focus.
evidence: Identify the paragraph focus
Revise one sentence that is missing, misplaced, or unrelated
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the child a paragraph draft and ask them to revise one sentence to improve focus.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for compare and contrast signals
topics.japaneseName: 比べる話の合図を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen for words and structure that show two ideas are being compared or contrasted in an explanation or discussion.
evidence: Note a compare or contrast word from speech
State what two ideas were being compared
topics.assessmentPrompt: Read a short comparative explanation aloud and ask the child to identify the compared ideas and signal words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Jukugo nominalization in academic words
topics.japaneseName: 学習語の熟語と名詞化
topics.description: Recognize compound nouns and nominalized school words as compact ways to express actions, processes, and abstract ideas.
evidence: Identify an academic compound noun in a school text
Paraphrase it into a fuller phrase or sentence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose a compound academic word and explain the action or idea it compresses.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Opinion words by strength and register
topics.japaneseName: 意見を表す語の強さと文体
topics.description: Choose words for opinion, certainty, doubt, and recommendation that fit evidence strength and audience.
evidence: Rank opinion expressions by strength
Revise a sentence so the opinion wording fits the evidence
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an opinion sentence using a weaker, stronger, or more formal expression.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Passive and causative meaning effects
topics.japaneseName: 受け身と使役の意味の効果
topics.description: Explain how passive or causative forms shift attention to receiver, actor, cause, responsibility, or perspective in a sentence.
evidence: Identify a passive or causative expression in context
Explain how the form changes focus or responsibility
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student paired sentences and ask how the passive or causative form changes meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Quoting and commenting on literary evidence
topics.japaneseName: 文学の根拠を引用して考えを述べる
topics.description: Select a short quoted phrase from a literary text and add commentary explaining how it supports an interpretation.
evidence: Quote a short phrase accurately
Write or say commentary connecting the quote to an interpretation
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose one phrase from a story and explain how it supports their idea about theme or character.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rating evidence relevance from sources
topics.japaneseName: 資料の根拠が問いに合うか評価する
topics.description: Judge whether a source detail strongly supports, partly supports, or does not support an inquiry question.
evidence: Rate at least three source details for relevance
Explain one rating in relation to the inquiry question
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student an inquiry question and source notes, then ask them to rate which evidence is most relevant.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Aligning report introduction and conclusion
topics.japaneseName: 報告文の初めと終わりを対応させる
topics.description: Revise an informational report so the conclusion answers the question or purpose set up in the introduction.
evidence: Identify the report question or purpose in the introduction
Revise the conclusion so it answers or reflects that purpose
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare the introduction and conclusion of a report and revise for alignment.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Referring to a source in panel discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いで資料にふれて発言する
topics.description: During a small panel or group discussion, refer to a source, chart, or text detail to support a comment or question.
evidence: Name or point to the source being used
Connect the source detail to a comment or question
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a group discussion, ask the student to make one source-supported contribution.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using a task-criteria checklist for revision
topics.japaneseName: めあてのチェックリストで推敲する
topics.description: Use a short checklist based on purpose, evidence, organization, and language to revise a reading response, report, or presentation.
evidence: Mark checklist criteria against a draft or rehearsal
Make one revision linked to a checklist criterion
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to use a criteria checklist and explain one revision made from it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing kanji, kana, or furigana for public writing
topics.japaneseName: 公開する文章の漢字・かな・ふりがな選択
topics.description: Choose kanji, kana, or furigana in public-facing writing based on audience, readability, and word familiarity.
evidence: Identify a word where notation choice affects readability
Revise notation or add furigana for the target audience
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to review a public-facing draft and justify one kanji, kana, or furigana choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Discipline word-family map
topics.japaneseName: 教科語彙の語族マップ
topics.description: Build a word-family map for an inquiry topic, grouping related compounds, verbs, adjectives, and source-specific terms.
evidence: Group related words from at least two sources
Explain how one word family helps understand the inquiry topic
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to make a word-family map for an inquiry topic and explain one cluster.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Concession and contrast sentence control
topics.japaneseName: 譲歩と対比を表す文の調整
topics.description: Use concession and contrast expressions to show limits, alternative views, and balanced reasoning in opinion or report writing.
evidence: Identify a concession or contrast expression
Revise a sentence to show a limit or alternative view clearly
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an opinion sentence by adding a concession or contrast expression.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing literary style across works
topics.japaneseName: 作品どうしの文体を比べる
topics.description: Compare two literary works by narration, sentence rhythm, word choice, imagery, or dialogue style and explain the effect.
evidence: Name one style feature in each work
Explain how the feature changes mood, viewpoint, or reader response
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare the style of two short literary excerpts and explain one effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Synthesizing notes from multiple sources
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料のメモを統合する
topics.description: Combine notes from multiple sources by subtopic, agreement, difference, or evidence type rather than listing sources one by one.
evidence: Group notes from at least two sources under one subtopic
State whether the sources agree, differ, or provide different evidence types
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student notes from multiple sources and ask them to group and synthesize them by subtopic.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Final proofread before publication
topics.japaneseName: 公開前の最終校正
topics.description: Use a final proofreading pass for names, titles, source details, punctuation, kana-kanji conversion, and layout before sharing a text.
evidence: Use a final-proof checklist on a draft
Correct at least one publication-level error or explain that no error was found
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to perform a final proofread on a report or public-facing text using a checklist.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Balancing slides, evidence, and speech in presentation
topics.japaneseName: 資料・根拠・話し方のバランス
topics.description: Design a presentation so slides or posters support the spoken explanation without replacing evidence-based speaking.
evidence: Use a visual that supports one evidence point
Explain the evidence orally rather than only reading the visual
