topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to present one evidence point with a visual and explain how the visual supports the speech.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on source use and audience impact
topics.japaneseName: 資料活用と聞き手への伝わり方を振り返る
topics.description: Reflect on how source choice, evidence selection, notation, and presentation choices affected audience understanding.
evidence: Name one source or presentation choice that helped the audience
Set one improvement goal for a future inquiry or presentation task
topics.assessmentPrompt: After an inquiry presentation, ask the student to reflect on source use and audience impact.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji component and word-origin notebook
topics.japaneseName: 漢字の構成と語源ノート
topics.description: Record kanji components, readings, word families, and origin clues as memory aids rather than isolated character facts.
evidence: Create a notebook entry that links a kanji component to two or more related words.
Use the entry to explain or remember an unfamiliar compound.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build one kanji notebook entry and explain how the component helps with a new word.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Proofreading homophones and okurigana as pairs
topics.japaneseName: 同音異義語と送り仮名を組で校正する
topics.description: Proofread a draft by checking homophone kanji choices and okurigana endings together against sentence meaning.
evidence: Identify a homophone or okurigana risk in a sentence.
Correct the form and explain how the sentence meaning guided the choice.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a short draft with homophone and okurigana risks and ask for corrections with reasons.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing problem-solution and cause-effect structures
topics.japaneseName: 問題解決と因果の文章構成を比べる
topics.description: Compare how explanatory texts organize problems, causes, effects, solutions, and supporting evidence.
evidence: Label whether a paragraph mainly uses problem-solution or cause-effect structure.
Explain how the structure changes what evidence the reader expects.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two explanatory paragraphs and identify their organizing structures.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Literary quotation to inference chain
topics.japaneseName: 引用から推論へつなぐ
topics.description: Connect a short literary quotation to an inference about character, theme, narrator, or repeated detail.
evidence: Select a short quotation that supports an interpretation.
Write or say the inference chain from quoted words to interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to quote one phrase and explain the inference it supports.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Inquiry note category system
topics.japaneseName: 探究メモの分類システム
topics.description: Classify inquiry notes by fact, example, opinion, question, source, and possible use in a report or presentation.
evidence: Tag source notes with categories such as fact, example, opinion, or question.
Use categories to choose notes for one report section or discussion point.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to categorize inquiry notes and select which notes belong in a report section.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Building on a source-supported comment
topics.japaneseName: 資料に基づく発言を受けて話す
topics.description: Respond to a peer comment by adding, questioning, or comparing source evidence rather than changing topic.
evidence: Refer to a peer comment and a source detail in the same turn.
Use the response to add, question, or compare evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a source-based discussion, ask the student to build on a peer comment with evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Polite request register in letters and email
topics.japaneseName: 手紙・メールの丁寧な依頼表現
topics.description: Choose polite request, thanks, and closing expressions that fit a classroom letter or simple email audience.
evidence: Identify request and thanks expressions in a model letter or email.
Revise a request so it matches the intended reader and purpose.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a short request note for a teacher, classmate, or community reader.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on evidence gaps in argument
topics.japaneseName: 意見の根拠不足を振り返る
topics.description: Review an opinion or presentation plan to identify where more evidence, example, or explanation is needed.
evidence: Mark one claim that needs stronger evidence or explanation.
Set a revision action that would fill the gap.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to review an opinion plan and name one evidence gap plus a revision step.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Furigana and notation policy for public texts
topics.japaneseName: 公開文のふりがな・表記方針
topics.description: Decide when a public-facing text needs furigana, kana substitution, or kanji retention for audience accessibility.
evidence: Identify a word whose notation may block a target reader.
Choose furigana, kana, or kanji and explain the audience reason.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to review a public text draft and justify two notation choices.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji compound families and register
topics.japaneseName: 漢字熟語の語族と文体
topics.description: Group compounds by shared morpheme and notice which words sound everyday, academic, public, or technical.
evidence: Group compounds by shared morpheme or semantic family.
Explain the register difference between two related compounds.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to make a compound-family map and label which words fit school reports or public texts.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Theme-summary synthesis across texts
topics.japaneseName: 複数作品の主題を要約して統合する
topics.description: Summarize how two literary works approach a related theme and synthesize one shared or contrasting insight.
evidence: Write a concise theme summary for each work.
State one synthesis claim comparing the works.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare theme summaries from two works and write one synthesis claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Resolving conflicting source claims
topics.japaneseName: 資料どうしの食い違いを考える
topics.description: Notice when sources disagree and use date, purpose, evidence type, and author viewpoint to explain the conflict.
evidence: Identify a conflict between two source claims.
Suggest a reason for the conflict based on source date, purpose, evidence, or viewpoint.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student two conflicting source excerpts and ask for a likely reason for the difference.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising a report for source integration
topics.japaneseName: 資料を統合する報告文の推敲
topics.description: Revise report paragraphs so source evidence is introduced, paraphrased or quoted, and connected to the report purpose.
evidence: Identify a source detail that is dropped into a report without explanation.
Revise the sentence or paragraph to integrate the source role.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a report paragraph so a source detail supports the inquiry purpose.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for counterevidence in debate
topics.japaneseName: 討論で反証を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen during debate for evidence that challenges a claim, weakens a reason, or raises a condition.
evidence: Record one piece of counterevidence from a speaker.
Explain which claim or reason the counterevidence challenges.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a debate, ask the student to chart counterevidence and the claim it challenges.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presentation handout layout and proofing
topics.japaneseName: 発表資料のレイアウトと校正
topics.description: Prepare a handout or slide with readable headings, source labels, balanced text, and final proofreading before presentation.
evidence: Apply a layout choice that makes a handout or slide easier to read.
Proofread headings, source labels, and notation before sharing.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a presentation handout for layout and final proofing.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on strategy transfer to lower secondary
topics.japaneseName: 中学校へつながる学習方略の振り返り
topics.description: Identify reading, writing, speaking, and inquiry strategies that can transfer from elementary tasks to lower-secondary language learning.
evidence: Name one strategy from an elementary language task.
Explain how the strategy could transfer to a lower-secondary task.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose portfolio evidence and write a transfer reflection for middle-school Japanese.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Glossing morphemes in short classical quotations
topics.japaneseName: 短い古典引用の語を注釈する
topics.description: Use teacher support, notes, and context to gloss key morphemes in a short classical quotation without full translation.
evidence: Annotate one or two key classical words or endings in a quotation.
Explain how the gloss changes modern understanding of the line.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a short classical quotation and explain one gloss.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Academic kanji morpheme note
topics.japaneseName: 学習語の漢字形態素メモ
topics.description: Record how a kanji morpheme contributes meaning across subject-area academic words in lower-secondary texts.
evidence: Choose one kanji morpheme and collect related academic words.
Explain how the morpheme supports inference in a new word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to make a morpheme note from words found in a textbook or source text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Headline framing in media texts
topics.japaneseName: メディア見出しの枠組み
topics.description: Notice how headlines, images, and first sentences frame a topic before evidence is presented.
evidence: Identify one framing choice in a headline or lead sentence.
Explain how the framing guides reader attention.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student two headlines on the same topic and ask how each frames the issue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising explanatory cohesion in paragraphs
topics.japaneseName: 説明段落の結束性を推敲する
topics.description: Revise an explanatory paragraph by improving referents, repeated terms, connectors, and sentence order.
evidence: Identify a cohesion problem in a paragraph.
Revise reference, repetition, connector, or order to improve flow.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a paragraph for cohesion and explain which cue was improved.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence protocol for group discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いの根拠提示プロトコル
topics.description: Use a discussion protocol that requires claim, source or text evidence, question, and response before moving to a new point.
evidence: Contribute a claim with source or text evidence during discussion.
Use the protocol to ask for or respond to evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a group task, ask the student to use a claim-evidence-question-response protocol.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for definition-example patterns
topics.japaneseName: 定義と例示の構造を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen to an explanation and separate the definition, example, elaboration, and speaker emphasis.
evidence: Note the definition and at least one example from a spoken explanation.
Explain how the example supports or clarifies the definition.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play or read an explanation and ask the student to map definition and example structure.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Expanding abstract noun networks
topics.japaneseName: 抽象名詞のネットワークを広げる
topics.description: Build networks of abstract nouns by definition, example, contrast, compound family, and subject-area use.
evidence: Map an abstract noun with examples, contrasts, and related compounds.
Use the network to interpret an unfamiliar academic sentence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create an abstract noun network from an explanatory text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on source summary and stance separation
topics.japaneseName: 要約と自分の立場の分離を振り返る
topics.description: Review whether notes and paragraphs clearly separate source summary, inference, evaluation, and personal stance.
evidence: Mark which sentences summarize the source and which state personal stance.
Revise one sentence that blurs summary and evaluation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a paragraph for source summary versus stance and revise one blurry sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing grammar in classical quotations
topics.japaneseName: 古典引用の文法を比べる
topics.description: Compare a short classical quotation with a modern paraphrase, noticing word order, endings, particles, or omitted subjects.
evidence: Identify one grammatical difference between quotation and paraphrase.
Explain how the difference affects meaning or tone.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare a supported classical line with a modern paraphrase.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Checking font variants in digital sources
topics.japaneseName: デジタル資料の字体差を確かめる
topics.description: Recognize that font, old/new character forms, and digital display choices can affect reading and citation accuracy.
evidence: Notice a font or character-form difference in a digital source.
Verify the intended character form before quoting, copying, or searching.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student digital source snippets with font or variant differences and ask how to verify the form.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rebuttal structure in argument reading
topics.japaneseName: 論説文の反論構造を読む
topics.description: Identify where an argument states an opposing view, concedes part of it, rebuts it, and returns to the main claim.
evidence: Label counterclaim, concession, rebuttal, and return-to-claim moves.
Explain how the rebuttal affects the strength of the argument.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a short argument for rebuttal structure.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Alternative interpretations of a symbol
topics.japaneseName: 象徴の別解を検討する
topics.description: Develop and compare two plausible interpretations of a recurring symbol or image using textual evidence.
evidence: State two possible interpretations of a symbol or recurring image.
Compare which evidence supports each interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to propose two interpretations for a symbol and compare their evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Facilitator move to return to evidence
topics.japaneseName: 司会として根拠に戻す発言
topics.description: Use facilitator language to return a discussion from opinion drift to textual, source, or data evidence.
evidence: Notice when discussion has moved away from evidence.
Use a facilitator prompt that asks the group to return to evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a seminar, ask the student to facilitate one turn that returns the group to evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Writing a counterclaim paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 反対意見を扱う段落を書く
topics.description: Write a paragraph that fairly states a counterclaim, concedes or limits it, and responds with evidence.
evidence: State a counterclaim without caricaturing it.
Respond with evidence and a clear relation to the main claim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to draft a counterclaim paragraph for a short argument.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Oral summary of source and stance
topics.japaneseName: 資料内容と立場を口頭で要約する
topics.description: Summarize a source orally while keeping source claim, evidence, and personal evaluation distinct.
evidence: Give an oral summary that names the source claim and evidence.
Add personal evaluation in a separate phrase or turn.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to orally summarize a source and then state their own evaluation separately.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on debate role and bias
topics.japaneseName: 討論の役割と偏りを振り返る
topics.description: Reflect on how assigned role, source selection, and prior assumptions shaped participation in debate or seminar.
evidence: Name one way a role or source choice shaped a contribution.
Identify one bias or assumption to check in a future discussion.
topics.assessmentPrompt: After debate, ask the student to write a reflection on role, source choice, and bias.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Glossing classical auxiliary meanings
topics.japaneseName: 古典助動詞の意味を注釈する
topics.description: Use notes and context to gloss high-frequency classical auxiliary meanings in supported passages.
evidence: Identify a supported auxiliary or ending in a passage.
Choose a context-appropriate gloss and explain the clue used.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to gloss selected auxiliary forms in a supported classical passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanbun phrase order paraphrase
topics.japaneseName: 漢文句の語順を現代語で言い換える
topics.description: Use introductory kanbun marks and context to paraphrase short phrases in modern Japanese order.
evidence: Identify the readback order for a supported kanbun phrase.
Produce a short modern-Japanese paraphrase that preserves the relation among words.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a supported kanbun phrase and ask for readback order and paraphrase.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Counterevidence in policy texts
topics.japaneseName: 政策的文章の反証を読む
topics.description: Identify evidence that complicates a policy argument by showing limits, costs, exceptions, or affected groups.
evidence: Find a source detail that complicates a policy claim.
Explain whether it shows a limit, cost, exception, or affected group.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to read a policy argument and identify one counterevidence detail.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source integration in argumentative essays
topics.japaneseName: 意見文に資料を統合する
topics.description: Integrate quoted, paraphrased, or summarized source material into an argumentative essay with commentary and source boundaries.
evidence: Introduce source material with clear purpose in an argument paragraph.
Add commentary that connects the source to claim, counterclaim, or rebuttal.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an argument paragraph so the source material is integrated and commented on.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Cross-examination notes in debate
topics.japaneseName: 討論の反対尋問メモ
topics.description: Prepare and use notes for questions that test definitions, evidence quality, assumptions, and consequences in debate.
evidence: Write a question that tests evidence, definition, assumption, or consequence.
Use the answer to adjust the debate position or rebuttal.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to prepare cross-examination notes and use one question in debate.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for stance shifts in media speech
topics.japaneseName: メディア発話の立場変化を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen for words, examples, and tone that show a speaker shifting from report to interpretation, criticism, or recommendation.
evidence: Mark a point where a speaker shifts stance or purpose.
Explain the language cue that signaled the shift.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play a short media speech and ask the student to identify a stance shift and its cue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sino-Japanese derivation and academic words
topics.japaneseName: 漢語の派生と学習語
topics.description: Use recurring Sino-Japanese morphemes to infer derived academic words across public, scientific, and social topics.
evidence: Identify a recurring morpheme in related academic words.
Use the morpheme to infer the meaning or relation of a new word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build a derivation map for academic words sharing one morpheme.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on high-school language goals
topics.japaneseName: 高校国語へ向けた言語目標の設定
topics.description: Use portfolio evidence to set language goals for high-school reading, writing, discussion, media, or classical language work.
evidence: Choose evidence from lower-secondary work that shows a strength or gap.
Set a high-school language goal tied to a specific domain.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to use portfolio evidence to write one high-school Japanese learning goal.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Contemporary Japanese genre convention map
topics.japaneseName: 現代の文章ジャンルの慣習マップ
topics.description: Map conventions of contemporary genres such as commentary, news, public notice, speech, interview, and essay.
evidence: Identify genre cues in a contemporary Japanese text.
Explain how a convention shapes reader expectation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to classify contemporary texts by genre cues and reader expectations.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source authority and rhetoric in media inquiry
topics.japaneseName: メディア探究における情報源の権威と修辞
topics.description: Evaluate how a media source builds authority through expertise, data, quotation, tone, design, and publication context.
evidence: Identify at least two authority-building moves in a media source.
Explain how those moves affect credibility or persuasion.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze how a media source builds authority for its claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Summary-position paragraph on a public issue
topics.japaneseName: 公共的課題の要約と立場の段落
topics.description: Write a paragraph that summarizes a public issue source and then states a limited position with evidence scope.
evidence: Separate neutral source summary from the writer position.
State a position with scope, limitation, or condition.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a summary-position paragraph from a public-issue source.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Modern literary voice and social context
topics.japaneseName: 近現代文学の語りと社会的文脈
topics.description: Analyze how voice, diction, perspective, and social context shape meaning in a modern literary passage.
evidence: Identify one voice or diction choice in a modern literary passage.
Connect the choice to social context or reader interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze how voice and context interact in a short modern literary passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Consensus protocol for contemporary issues
topics.japaneseName: 現代的課題討議の合意形成プロトコル
topics.description: Use a structured protocol to identify shared facts, disputed evidence, values, constraints, and tentative agreement.
evidence: Name shared facts and disputed points during a contemporary-issue discussion.
Help the group state tentative agreement or remaining disagreement.
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a roundtable, ask the student to guide the group through shared facts, disputes, and tentative agreement.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for argument frame and source role
topics.japaneseName: 論の枠組みと資料の役割を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen to a presentation or speech and identify the argument frame, source role, and evidence limitation.
evidence: State the speaker argument frame and the source role.
Identify one limitation or unanswered question in the oral argument.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to listen to a presentation and map its argument frame, source use, and limitation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Register change across language history
topics.japaneseName: 言葉の歴史における文体変化
topics.description: Compare historical and contemporary expressions to notice changes in register, politeness, genre, and public style.
evidence: Identify a register or style difference across eras.
Explain how social context or genre may have shaped the difference.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare inherited and contemporary expressions for register and public style.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on inquiry method and limitation
topics.japaneseName: 探究方法と限界を振り返る
topics.description: Reflect on how inquiry method, search terms, source type, and evidence limits shaped a contemporary Japanese task.
evidence: Name one method choice that affected the evidence found.
State a limitation and a next step for improving the inquiry.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a method-limitation reflection after an inquiry task.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Testing definitions in logical prose
topics.japaneseName: 論理的文章の定義を検証する
topics.description: Test whether an argument defines key terms consistently across examples, exceptions, and counterexamples.
evidence: Identify where a key term is defined, narrowed, or shifted.
Use an example or counterexample to test the definition boundary.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a logical text for a key definition and test it with a counterexample.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Hedging and evidence strength scale
topics.japaneseName: 根拠の強さに応じた限定表現
topics.description: Calibrate hedging, certainty, concession, and limitation expressions to match the strength of evidence in logical prose.
evidence: Rank stance expressions by certainty or claim strength.
Revise an overstrong or underqualified claim to fit the evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise claims by matching hedging language to evidence strength.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Academic term field map
topics.japaneseName: 学術語彙の分野マップ
topics.description: Map academic terms by field, definition, contrasting term, method, and limitation in logical or critical texts.
evidence: Place a term in a field with a definition and contrasting term.
Explain how the term changes meaning or force in another source.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build a field map for terms from two logical or critical texts.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Conceptual pair contrast in criticism
topics.japaneseName: 批評における対概念の整理
topics.description: Analyze paired concepts such as public/private, tradition/innovation, subject/object, or individual/society in argumentative or literary-critical prose.
evidence: Identify a conceptual pair used in a text.
Explain how the contrast structures the writer argument or interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a text for a conceptual contrast and explain its role.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanbun compounds and reference vocabulary
topics.japaneseName: 漢文由来の熟語と典拠語彙
topics.description: Recognize kanbun-derived compounds and reference vocabulary that carry inherited rhetorical or cultural force in modern texts.
evidence: Identify a kanbun-derived compound or reference expression.
Explain how the expression adds authority, compression, or cultural resonance.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to find a kanbun-derived expression in a modern or classical-linked passage and explain its effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Verifying advanced kanji style with references
topics.japaneseName: 参考資料で高度な漢字表記を確認する
topics.description: Use dictionaries, style references, and source context to verify advanced kanji, kana, okurigana, and variant choices.
evidence: Identify an advanced kanji or notation choice that needs verification.
Use a reference source to justify or revise the notation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to verify notation choices in a critical or classical commentary draft.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading method and evidence tables
topics.japaneseName: 方法と根拠の表を読む
topics.description: Read tables, outlines, or diagrams that connect research method, data, evidence, limitation, and conclusion.
evidence: Match evidence or data to the method that produced it.
Identify a limitation implied by the method or data type.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a method-evidence table and ask what claim it can and cannot support.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Critiquing an argument abstract
topics.japaneseName: 論文要旨を批判的に読む
topics.description: Critique whether an abstract clearly states problem, method, evidence, scope, limitation, and implication.
evidence: Label abstract moves such as problem, method, result, limitation, and implication.
Identify one missing or underdeveloped move that weakens the abstract.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to critique a short abstract-like passage for scope and limitation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Scholarly source position map
topics.japaneseName: 学術的資料の立場マップ
topics.description: Map sources by claim, method, evidence type, limitation, agreement, disagreement, and usefulness for a research question.
evidence: Place at least three sources in a position map.
Explain how two sources agree, disagree, or use different methods.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map source positions before writing a literature-review paragraph.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Layered annotation of digital sources
topics.japaneseName: デジタル資料の層別注釈
topics.description: Annotate digital sources in layers for quotation, paraphrase, claim, evidence, uncertainty, and follow-up question.
evidence: Use annotation categories that separate source words from personal comment.
Export or summarize annotations for a draft or oral defense.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a digital source with layers and turn the notes into a draft plan.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Critical-lens evidence grid
topics.japaneseName: 批評的観点の根拠グリッド
topics.description: Organize textual evidence according to a chosen critical lens and note evidence that complicates the lens.
evidence: Build an evidence grid for one literary-critical lens.
Mark evidence that supports and evidence that complicates the reading.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create a critical-lens grid for a literary passage or work.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Narrative time and symbolic system
topics.japaneseName: 語りの時間と象徴体系
topics.description: Analyze how time shifts, memory, foreshadowing, and symbols form a system of meaning across a literary work.
evidence: Trace at least two time shifts or symbolic recurrences.
Explain how the pattern shapes interpretation beyond plot summary.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map narrative time shifts and symbols in a literary excerpt.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical function-word chart
topics.japaneseName: 古典機能語チャート
topics.description: Chart recurring classical particles, auxiliary verbs, sentence endings, and gloss choices in supported texts.
evidence: Record a classical function word with form, rough meaning, and example context.
Use the chart to support a paraphrase or interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build a chart of selected function words from a classical passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Justifying a kanbun paraphrase
topics.japaneseName: 漢文の現代語訳を根拠づける
topics.description: Justify a kanbun paraphrase by referring to reading order, omitted subject, function words, and contextual meaning.
evidence: State a paraphrase choice and the reading-order or context clue behind it.
Compare one alternative paraphrase and explain the effect.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to justify one paraphrase choice in a supported kanbun passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising a critical-response thesis
topics.japaneseName: 批評的応答の中心主張を推敲する
topics.description: Revise a critical-response thesis so it is interpretive, arguable, scoped, and connected to textual evidence.
evidence: Identify whether a thesis is merely descriptive or genuinely interpretive.
Revise the thesis to include scope and evidence direction.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a literary or logical response thesis and explain the improvement.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Integrating sources in a literature-review paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 文献レビュー段落の資料統合
topics.description: Integrate multiple source positions in a literature-review paragraph using grouping, contrast, and limitation language.
evidence: Group sources by shared or contrasting position.
Write a paragraph sentence that synthesizes source positions rather than listing sources.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a literature-review paragraph from source listing to source synthesis.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Quotation integration and punctuation control
topics.japaneseName: 引用の統合と句読点の管理
topics.description: Control punctuation, quotation format, source signal phrases, and commentary boundaries in academic or critical writing.
evidence: Identify where quotation, paraphrase, and commentary begin and end.
Revise punctuation or signal phrase use to clarify source boundaries.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to proofread a paragraph with quotations and revise punctuation and signal phrases.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical text annotation conventions
topics.japaneseName: 古典本文の注釈記法
topics.description: Use consistent annotation marks for classical grammar, kanbun readback, glosses, and commentary notes.
evidence: Apply annotation marks consistently to a supported classical or kanbun passage.
Separate grammar notes, paraphrase notes, and interpretive comments.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a classical passage using agreed mark conventions.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Counterquestion follow-up in colloquium
topics.japaneseName: コロキウムで反問をつなぐ
topics.description: Ask follow-up questions that test evidence, definition, assumption, alternative interpretation, or method in a colloquium.
evidence: Ask a follow-up question linked to a previous claim or interpretation.
Explain whether the question tests evidence, definition, assumption, method, or alternative reading.
topics.assessmentPrompt: In a colloquium, ask the student to pose and classify a counterquestion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Responding to limitations in oral defense
topics.japaneseName: 口頭弁明で限界に応答する
topics.description: Respond to questions about evidence limits, method limits, scope, or alternative readings without overstating the argument.
evidence: Acknowledge a valid limitation or alternative in an oral response.
Reframe the claim so the central argument remains appropriately scoped.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to defend a claim orally after a question about limitation or evidence scope.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for assumptions in presentations
topics.japaneseName: 発表の前提を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen to an academic or critical presentation and identify unstated assumptions behind method, definition, or evidence selection.
evidence: Name one assumption that supports the speaker claim.
Ask a question that tests whether the assumption is warranted.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to listen to a presentation and write one assumption-testing question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Questioning source method and limitation
topics.japaneseName: 資料の方法と限界を問う
topics.description: Formulate listening questions that ask how a source was made, what evidence it contains, and what limitation remains.
evidence: Identify the method, evidence, and limitation in a spoken explanation.
Ask a follow-up question about one weak or unclear element.
topics.assessmentPrompt: After a source-based presentation, ask the student to pose a method or limitation question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Selecting evidence for an interpretive portfolio
topics.japaneseName: 解釈ポートフォリオの根拠選択
topics.description: Select portfolio evidence showing growth in literary interpretation, logical analysis, classical reading, or source-based writing.
evidence: Choose artifacts that show a specific interpretive or analytic skill.
Explain what each artifact proves about language growth.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to select portfolio evidence and write a short rationale for each artifact.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Feedback-to-revision audit trail
topics.japaneseName: 助言から推敲への記録
topics.description: Track how peer, teacher, or source feedback changed a claim, evidence selection, interpretation, style, or citation practice.
evidence: Record feedback and the exact revision decision that followed.
Explain why the revision improved accuracy, scope, or persuasiveness.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create an audit trail for revisions in a critical or research draft.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Signpost audit in extended argument
topics.japaneseName: 長い論述の道標表現を点検する
topics.description: Audit an extended argument for signposts that announce claim sequence, concession, source role, limitation, and conclusion.
evidence: Mark signposts and the discourse move each one introduces.
Add or revise a signpost where reader navigation is weak.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to audit an extended argument for signposting and revise one section boundary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source voice and metadiscourse control
topics.japaneseName: 資料の声とメタ談話の管理
topics.description: Use metadiscourse to distinguish source voice, writer voice, synthesis, critique, and implication in advanced writing.
evidence: Identify source voice, writer voice, and synthesis voice in a paragraph.
Revise wording so the source role and writer stance are clear.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a synthesis paragraph for source voice and metadiscourse clarity.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Disciplinary terminology glossary
topics.japaneseName: 分野横断の専門語グロッサリー
topics.description: Build a glossary that defines disciplinary terms, source-specific uses, translation/paraphrase choices, and register constraints.
evidence: Write glossary entries that include definition, source context, and register note.
Use the glossary to revise term consistency in an extended argument.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build and apply a glossary for a capstone or research paper.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Adapting public register and lexis
topics.japaneseName: 公開発信に向けた語彙と文体の調整
topics.description: Adapt specialized or academic vocabulary for a public audience without losing accuracy or nuance.
evidence: Identify specialized terms that need paraphrase, glossary, or retention.
Revise a passage for public audience while preserving key distinctions.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to adapt an academic paragraph for a public handout or speech.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji and orthography style sheet
topics.japaneseName: 漢字・表記スタイルシート
topics.description: Create and apply a style sheet for kanji, kana, okurigana, specialist terms, names, and source titles in a capstone text.
evidence: Create style-sheet entries for recurring notation decisions.
Apply the style sheet to revise a section consistently.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create a notation style sheet and use it for a final-proof pass.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Accessible multiscript review
topics.japaneseName: 多表記のアクセシビリティ点検
topics.description: Review a public-facing text for audience accessibility across kanji density, kana support, furigana, romanization, and layout.
evidence: Identify a multiscript accessibility issue for a target reader.
Revise notation or layout to improve access without oversimplifying content.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to review a public-facing capstone artifact for multiscript accessibility.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Final proof source crosscheck
topics.japaneseName: 最終校正で資料と本文を照合する
topics.description: Crosscheck source names, titles, dates, quotations, paraphrases, citations, and reference entries before publication.
evidence: Match an in-text citation, quotation, or paraphrase to the reference entry.
Correct a mismatch in source detail, quotation, or formatting.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to perform a source crosscheck on a final capstone section.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Publication layout copyediting
topics.japaneseName: 公開文書のレイアウト校閲
topics.description: Copyedit a public-facing document for heading hierarchy, spacing, figure captions, source labels, readability, and style consistency.
evidence: Identify a layout or copyediting issue that affects readability or source clarity.
Revise the document using a style sheet or publication checklist.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copyedit a public-facing report or presentation handout for publication readiness.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Public controversy source-role matrix
topics.japaneseName: 公共的論争の資料役割マトリクス
topics.description: Map sources in a public controversy by stakeholder, evidence type, value claim, expertise, and action implication.
evidence: Place sources in a matrix by stakeholder and evidence role.
Use the matrix to explain a conflict in values, evidence, or recommended action.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build a source-role matrix for a public controversy.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Final bibliography integrity check
topics.japaneseName: 最終参考文献の整合性確認
topics.description: Check that references are complete, used ethically, cited consistently, and separated from uncited background reading.
evidence: Identify missing, unused, or inconsistently formatted references.
Revise a bibliography or source list to match the citation and attribution plan.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to audit a final bibliography against in-text source use.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Synthesizing counterevidence in policy arguments
topics.japaneseName: 政策論の反証を統合して読む
topics.description: Synthesize counterevidence across policy sources to explain limits, tradeoffs, affected groups, or unresolved risk.
evidence: Connect counterevidence from at least two sources.
Explain how the counterevidence limits or reframes a policy claim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to synthesize counterevidence from multiple public issue sources.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Research method and limitation section
topics.japaneseName: 探究論文の方法と限界の節
topics.description: Write a method and limitation section that explains source selection, evidence boundaries, and unresolved questions.
evidence: Describe how sources or evidence were selected.
State at least one limitation and its effect on the argument scope.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to draft a method and limitation section for a capstone paper.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Capstone introduction-conclusion alignment
topics.japaneseName: 卒業論文的文章の序論と結論の対応
topics.description: Revise the introduction and conclusion of an extended paper so the final claim answers the opening problem and reflects actual evidence.
evidence: Compare the opening problem, thesis, and final conclusion.
Revise either introduction or conclusion to align claim scope and evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to audit a capstone paper introduction and conclusion for alignment.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Positioning literary and cultural sources
topics.japaneseName: 文学・文化資料の位置づけ
topics.description: Position literary, cultural, classical, and critical sources by interpretive role in a comparative reading.
evidence: Classify sources as primary text, context, critical interpretation, or cultural comparison.
Explain how one source changes or qualifies the interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map source roles for a comparative literary-cultural interpretation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence map for reinterpretation of canon
topics.japaneseName: 古典的解釈を読み替える根拠マップ
topics.description: Map evidence used in canonical and contemporary interpretations and explain how evidence priorities differ.
evidence: Map evidence used by two interpretations of a work.
Explain how a contemporary lens changes what counts as central evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare evidence maps for canonical and contemporary readings.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Modern reception of classical and kanbun expressions
topics.japaneseName: 古典・漢文表現の現代的受容
topics.description: Analyze how classical and kanbun expressions are received, quoted, transformed, or contested in modern contexts.
evidence: Identify a classical or kanbun expression in a modern reception context.
Explain whether the reception preserves, transforms, or challenges the original force.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze a modern use of a classical or kanbun expression.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Language identity and register case study
topics.japaneseName: 言語アイデンティティと文体の事例研究
topics.description: Analyze a case where dialect, register, public discourse, or inherited expression shapes identity and audience positioning.
evidence: Describe how language choice indexes identity or relationship.
Use textual or discourse evidence rather than only personal impression.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to prepare a short case study on language identity and register.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Designing question sequences for symposium
topics.japaneseName: シンポジウムの問いの連鎖設計
topics.description: Design a sequence of questions that moves from clarification to method, evidence, implication, and synthesis.
evidence: Write a question sequence with at least three reasoning functions.
Use the sequence to connect speakers or source positions.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to design and use a symposium question sequence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reframing limitations in public Q&A
topics.japaneseName: 公開質疑で限界を言い換える
topics.description: Respond to public Q&A by acknowledging limitations, reframing scope, and indicating next evidence or action.
evidence: Acknowledge a limitation without abandoning the central claim.
Reframe the answer with scope, next evidence, or action path.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to answer public Q&A prompts about limitation and evidence scope.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for synthesis threads
topics.japaneseName: 統合の糸口を聞き取る
topics.description: Listen across speakers to identify recurring claims, tensions, shared evidence, unresolved questions, and possible synthesis threads.
evidence: Track at least two recurring threads across speakers.
State one synthesis question that connects the threads.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a panel, ask the student to note synthesis threads and propose a connecting question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Clustering audience concerns while listening
topics.japaneseName: 聞きながら聴衆の懸念を分類する
topics.description: Cluster audience questions by evidence, method, values, feasibility, source trust, and implication during public discussion.
evidence: Classify audience questions or concerns by reasoning type.
Use the cluster to prepare a synthesis response or moderation transition.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to classify audience questions during a presentation or symposium.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Capstone portfolio transfer argument
topics.japaneseName: 卒業ポートフォリオの転用論
topics.description: Use portfolio evidence to argue how language, literature, logic, classical, and media skills transfer to future contexts.
evidence: Select evidence from at least three language domains.
Write a transfer argument that names future academic, civic, or workplace contexts.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a portfolio transfer argument using capstone evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Public language readiness self-assessment
topics.japaneseName: 公共的な言語使用への準備度自己評価
topics.description: Assess readiness for public language tasks by reviewing evidence quality, ethical source use, audience adaptation, and response to critique.
evidence: Rate readiness across evidence, ethics, audience, and critique response.
Set one concrete improvement action before final publication or presentation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to complete a readiness self-assessment before a final public language task.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Visual abstract of argument structure
topics.japaneseName: 論証構造の図解要旨
topics.description: Create or read a visual abstract that shows claim, source roles, counterevidence, limitation, and conclusion in an extended argument.
evidence: Map claim, source role, counterevidence, limitation, and conclusion in a visual abstract.
Use the visual abstract to explain how the argument is structured.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create a visual abstract of an extended argument and explain its structure.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sorting confusable hiragana shapes
topics.japaneseName: 似ているひらがなの形を分ける
topics.description: Distinguish visually similar hiragana by stable shape features while matching each sign to its sound.
evidence: Sort confusable kana cards by shape and sound without relying only on word memory.
Explain one visible difference between two similar hiragana.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to sort mixed confusable hiragana cards and name one difference in shape.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Small kana size and placement
topics.japaneseName: 小さいかなの大きさと位置
topics.description: Recognize and write small kana as size-marked spelling cues rather than full-size mora signs.
evidence: Identify small kana in words and explain that size changes the reading.
Place small kana legibly in copied words.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copy words with small kana and mark which kana are small.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Mora clap to kana self-check
topics.japaneseName: 拍とかなの対応をたしかめる
topics.description: Use mora clapping or tapping to check whether a copied kana word has the expected number and order of sound units.
evidence: Tap or clap a word into mora units before checking the written kana.
Find one missing, extra, or misplaced kana by comparing sound units to spelling.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student short kana words to clap, copy, and self-check for missing or extra mora.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Copying katakana names and labels
topics.japaneseName: カタカナの名前・ラベルを写す
topics.description: Copy short katakana names and labels with readable stroke shape, size, and spacing.
evidence: Copy a short katakana name or label with stable character shapes.
Keep each character separated and aligned in the writing space.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copy katakana labels from classroom objects and check readability.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Self-check marks after sentence copying
topics.japaneseName: 文を写した後の確認印
topics.description: After copying a short sentence, check characters, punctuation, and spacing with a visible self-check routine.
evidence: Use a check mark or underline to verify copied punctuation and characters.
Correct one copying error without rewriting unrelated text.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copy two short sentences and mark the places they checked.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji as meaning signs in picture contexts
topics.japaneseName: 絵と結びつく意味を表す漢字
topics.description: Connect early kanji to meaning in picture, calendar, number, and classroom-label contexts.
evidence: Match early kanji to a picture, number, calendar item, or classroom object.
Explain the meaning link without treating the kanji as only a sound sign.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to match early kanji cards to pictures and explain the meaning connection.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing kanji by stroke count
topics.japaneseName: 画数で漢字をくらべる
topics.description: Count and compare strokes in early kanji as a way to attend to complete character form.
evidence: Count strokes in taught kanji with a model.
Use stroke count to notice a missing or extra stroke in a copied kanji.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two early kanji by stroke count and find a copying error.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sorting word cards by sound and meaning
topics.japaneseName: 音と意味でことばカードを分ける
topics.description: Sort familiar word cards by beginning sound, meaning field, or picture category to build early word awareness.
evidence: Sort word cards by a sound category and by a meaning category.
Explain why one word belongs in a chosen group.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give familiar word cards and ask the student to sort them once by sound and once by meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Finding a word in a picture dictionary
topics.japaneseName: 絵じてんでことばを見つける
topics.description: Use picture, kana label, and category cues to find a familiar or unknown word in a child dictionary.
evidence: Use picture or category clues to find a target word.
Read or copy the kana label for the found word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to find a named object or action in a picture dictionary and copy its label.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Adding who or what to one sentence
topics.japaneseName: 一文にだれ・何をくわえる
topics.description: Revise a very short sentence by adding who, what, where, or when so the reader can understand the event.
evidence: Identify a missing who, what, where, or when detail in a short sentence.
Add one detail while keeping the sentence readable.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student short event sentences and ask them to add one needed detail for the reader.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracking shared reading with finger or pointer
topics.japaneseName: 指でたどって一緒に読む
topics.description: Track print direction, line order, and word chunks during shared reading without losing place.
evidence: Follow the current line and direction during shared reading.
Return to the correct place after a pause or page turn.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During shared reading, ask the student to track the text and point to where reading resumes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Answering a question from a picture caption
topics.japaneseName: 写真・絵の説明から質問に答える
topics.description: Use picture and caption together to answer a simple factual question orally.
evidence: Point to the picture or caption that answers a factual question.
Answer orally using a word or phrase from the caption.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show a picture-caption page and ask the student to answer a factual question with evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kana homophone context in listening and writing
topics.japaneseName: 同じ音のことばを文脈で書き分ける
topics.description: Use sentence context to distinguish familiar same-sound words when listening, reading, or writing in kana and early kanji.
evidence: Choose the intended same-sound word from a simple sentence context.
Explain which context clue showed the intended meaning.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Read short sentences aloud and ask the student to choose the intended same-sound word card.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Checking kanji and okurigana while copying words
topics.japaneseName: 漢字と送りがなを確認して写す
topics.description: Copy mixed kanji-kana words while checking where the kanji stem ends and okurigana begins.
evidence: Mark or say the kanji part and okurigana part of a copied word.
Correct a missing or extra okurigana in a familiar word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copy mixed words and mark the kanji stem and okurigana boundary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji compound picture labels
topics.japaneseName: 絵ラベルで熟語を読む
topics.description: Read familiar grade 2 kanji compounds in picture labels by using meaning fields and known kanji.
evidence: Match a familiar kanji compound to a picture or classroom label.
Name one kanji in the compound that gives a meaning clue.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to match compound labels to pictures and explain one kanji meaning clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing kanji words to fit a sentence
topics.japaneseName: 文に合う漢字語を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose a familiar kanji word that fits the sentence meaning rather than only matching a sound.
evidence: Select the kanji word that fits a sentence context.
Reject a same-sound or similar-looking word that does not fit the meaning.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give sentence frames with word choices and ask the student to choose and explain the best word.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Gojuon order for dictionary practice
topics.japaneseName: 五十音順で辞書を使う練習
topics.description: Use gojuon order and first kana to locate simple headwords in a child dictionary or word list.
evidence: Place words in approximate gojuon order by first kana.
Use first kana and order cues to find a headword.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to order word cards and then find two words in a child dictionary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Clean rewriting after feedback
topics.japaneseName: 助言の後に清書する
topics.description: Rewrite a short draft cleanly after feedback, preserving content while improving legibility, margins, and corrections.
evidence: Transfer a corrected short draft into a clean copy.
Keep punctuation, indentation, and corrected characters visible and readable.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a marked short draft and ask the student to produce a clean copy.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Ordering sentence strips into a paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 文カードを並べて段落にする
topics.description: Arrange sentence strips into a short paragraph by event order, explanation order, or beginning-middle-end flow.
evidence: Order sentence strips into a coherent short paragraph.
Explain one clue that shows why two sentences belong next to each other.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give mixed sentence strips and ask the student to order them and explain one ordering clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Line breaks for copied direct speech
topics.japaneseName: 会話文を行を変えて写す
topics.description: Copy short narrative passages with direct speech while preserving quotation marks, line breaks, and speaker changes.
evidence: Copy direct speech with quotation marks and line breaks in the correct places.
Identify where the speaker changes in a short passage.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copy a short dialogue passage and mark each speaker change.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading step captions in explanations
topics.japaneseName: 説明の手順キャプションを読む
topics.description: Use numbered or ordered captions to follow a simple process in an informational text.
evidence: Put captioned steps into the correct order.
Use one caption to explain what happens next in the process.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give captioned process pictures and ask the student to order and explain the steps.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reason connectors in story retelling
topics.japaneseName: 物語の理由をつなぐことば
topics.description: Use because, so, then, and similar connectors to retell why a character acted or changed.
evidence: Retell a story moment with a reason connector.
Link a character action to a cause from the text.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to retell a character action using a reason connector and a text clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing feeling words by intensity
topics.japaneseName: 気持ちの強さに合うことばを選ぶ
topics.description: Choose among familiar feeling words by intensity and fit with a story situation.
evidence: Rank familiar feeling words from weak to strong or calm to intense.
Choose a feeling word that fits a character moment and give a reason.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a story scene and feeling-word bank, then ask the student to choose and justify one word.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rereading aloud to self-correct
topics.japaneseName: 声に出して読み直し、直す
topics.description: Reread a short sentence or paragraph aloud to notice omitted words, awkward order, or punctuation mistakes.
evidence: Reread aloud and identify one place that needs correction.
Make a focused correction without changing the whole draft.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to reread a short draft aloud and mark one correction they noticed.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji component position: left-right and top-bottom
topics.japaneseName: 漢字部品の左右・上下の位置
topics.description: Notice how kanji components are arranged left-right, top-bottom, enclosing, or stacked to support reading and writing.
evidence: Label the component positions in a taught or related kanji.
Use component position to explain a handwriting balance choice.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to mark component positions in grade 3 kanji and explain one layout choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Looking up an unknown kanji compound reading
topics.japaneseName: 知らない熟語の読みを調べる
topics.description: Use reading guesses, components, or dictionary tools to verify the reading and meaning of an unfamiliar kanji compound.
evidence: Record a guessed reading or lookup route for an unknown compound.
Verify reading and meaning with a dictionary or class reference.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give an unfamiliar compound from a grade-level text and ask the student to document how they found its reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Repairing dictionary headwords for inflected verbs
topics.japaneseName: 活用した動詞を見出し語にもどす
topics.description: Turn a common inflected verb form back toward a dictionary headword for lookup or spelling verification.
evidence: Identify that an inflected verb needs a base form for lookup.
Use okurigana or sentence context to propose a likely headword.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose dictionary headwords for inflected verbs from short sentences.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Paragraph topic-label outline
topics.japaneseName: 段落の話題ラベルで構成を作る
topics.description: Plan a short report or opinion paragraph by assigning a topic label to each paragraph or sentence group.
evidence: Write a topic label for each planned paragraph or sentence group.
Move one detail that does not fit its topic label.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create topic labels for a short draft plan and move one misplaced detail.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Marking copied words and own words in source notes
topics.japaneseName: 資料メモで写したことばと自分のことばを分ける
topics.description: Distinguish copied source words from own-word notes when taking simple notes from one source.
evidence: Mark copied words and own-word notes differently.
Explain why one note should be copied exactly or rewritten in own words.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to take notes from a short source and mark copied wording versus own wording.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Fixing small-tsu and long-vowel errors in romaji input
topics.japaneseName: ローマ字入力の促音・長音ミスを直す
topics.description: Use mora awareness to correct common digital input errors involving small tsu, small kana, and long vowels.
evidence: Identify a small-tsu, small-kana, or long-vowel error after romaji input.
Correct the input and explain which sound unit changed.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give typed kana words with input errors and ask the student to correct and explain them.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Guessing then verifying on-yomi or kun-yomi from context
topics.japaneseName: 文脈で音訓を予想し確かめる
topics.description: Make a context-based reading guess for a familiar kanji and then verify it against word family, okurigana, or dictionary evidence.
evidence: State a likely on-yomi or kun-yomi and the clue used.
Verify the reading with a word family, okurigana cue, or dictionary.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to read kanji words in context, write a reading guess, and verify it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Definition-example explanatory paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 定義と例で説明する段落
topics.description: Write a short explanatory paragraph that introduces a term or topic and supports it with one clear example.
evidence: Write a sentence that defines or introduces the topic.
Add one example sentence that clearly supports the definition or explanation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a definition-example paragraph about an observation or school topic.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing a poem word and explaining its effect
topics.japaneseName: 詩のことばを選び効果を説明する
topics.description: Choose one image, sound, or feeling word in a poem and explain how it shapes the reader response.
evidence: Select one poem word that creates image, rhythm, sound, or feeling.
Explain the effect of that word using a reason or text clue.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose one important poem word and explain its effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Peer feedback on oral reading fluency
topics.japaneseName: 音読のよさを友だちに伝える
topics.description: Listen to a classmate oral reading and give one specific comment about pause, voice, pace, or phrase grouping.
evidence: Name one specific oral-reading strength or target after listening.
Use a text cue such as punctuation or phrase group to support the feedback.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to listen to a peer reading and give one specific fluency comment.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing context guess with dictionary meaning
topics.japaneseName: 文脈の予想と辞書の意味を比べる
topics.description: Guess an unknown word meaning from context, then compare the guess with a dictionary entry or class reference.
evidence: Write or say a context-based meaning guess for an unknown word.
Compare the guess with a dictionary meaning and revise if needed.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to guess, look up, and revise the meaning of an unknown word from a grade-level text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Turning a table fact into a sentence report
topics.japaneseName: 表の事実を文で報告する
topics.description: Read a simple table and turn one factual value or comparison into a clear sentence.
evidence: Locate a factual value or comparison in a simple table.
Write or say a sentence that reports the fact accurately.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a small table and ask the student to write two factual report sentences from it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Labeling relation patterns inside jukugo
topics.japaneseName: 熟語の中の関係を見分ける
topics.description: Identify common relation patterns inside kanji compounds, such as modifier-head, similar meaning, opposite meaning, or action-object.
evidence: Label a familiar compound relation pattern with teacher-provided categories.
Explain how the kanji meanings combine in one compound.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to sort familiar jukugo by relation pattern and explain one compound.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Decision tree for multiple kanji readings
topics.japaneseName: 複数の読み方を選ぶ手順
topics.description: Use a simple decision tree for choosing among multiple kanji readings based on compound, okurigana, word family, and sentence context.
evidence: Apply a decision step to choose a likely kanji reading.
Name the clue used: compound, okurigana, word family, or context.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to use a reading-choice decision tree on words with familiar multireading kanji.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Context note for homophone kanji choices
topics.japaneseName: 同音異義語の文脈メモ
topics.description: Use sentence meaning and topic context to choose between same-sound kanji words and record the reason.
evidence: Choose the correct same-sound kanji word for a sentence.
Write a short context note explaining the choice.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give same-sound word choices in sentences and ask the student to choose and write the context clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Fact and opinion tags on source cards
topics.japaneseName: 資料カードに事実・考えを分けて書く
topics.description: Tag note-card entries as fact, example, explanation, or opinion when gathering information from sources.
evidence: Tag source-card notes by information role.
Explain why one note is a fact, example, explanation, or opinion.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to tag source-card notes from a short informational text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Ordering cause and example in an explanation paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 説明段落で原因と例の順序を整える
topics.description: Revise an explanatory paragraph so cause, result, example, and explanation sentences appear in a reader-friendly order.
evidence: Identify cause, result, example, and explanation sentences in a draft.
Move at least one sentence to improve paragraph order.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a mixed explanatory paragraph and ask the student to reorder and label sentence roles.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Boundary notes for quotation and summary
topics.japaneseName: 引用と要約の境目をメモする
topics.description: Mark whether a note is quoted wording, a short summary, or the student own comment when using a source.
evidence: Label a note as quotation, summary, or own comment.
Revise one note so the boundary between source words and own words is clearer.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to label source notes and revise one mixed note for clearer boundaries.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Desu/masu and da style consistency revision
topics.japaneseName: です・ます体とだ体の統一
topics.description: Revise a classroom draft for consistent sentence-ending style suited to audience and genre.
evidence: Find mixed sentence-ending styles in a short draft.
Revise endings to match a chosen style and audience.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a paragraph with mixed endings and ask the student to revise for style consistency.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Integrating visual, caption, and text notes
topics.japaneseName: 図・キャプション・本文を結びつけてメモする
topics.description: Combine information from a visual, caption, and prose sentence into one integrated note.
evidence: Identify what the visual shows, what the caption says, and what the prose adds.
Write one note that integrates the three sources of information.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to read a diagram page and write one integrated note from visual, caption, and prose.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Literary evidence sticky note
topics.japaneseName: 物語の根拠ふせん
topics.description: Mark a literary clue with a short note that names the inference it supports about character, scene, or theme.
evidence: Select a sentence or detail that supports an inference.
Write a short evidence note linking the detail to the inference.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to place two evidence notes in a story and explain one inference.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Turning a note into a discussion question
topics.japaneseName: メモを話し合いの質問にする
topics.description: Use a reading or source note to form a discussion question that invites evidence, comparison, or clarification.
evidence: Turn a note into a clear question for classmates.
Choose whether the question asks for evidence, comparison, or clarification.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose one note and turn it into a discussion question with a purpose label.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revision checklist for word choice and sentence order
topics.japaneseName: 語句選びと文の順序の推敲表
topics.description: Use a small checklist to revise precise word choice, sentence order, evidence, and reader clarity in a paragraph.
evidence: Use checklist items to identify one word-choice issue and one order issue.
Make a revision and state which checklist item it addresses.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a paragraph with a checklist and annotate two changes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji dictionary lookup log reflection
topics.japaneseName: 漢字辞典で調べた記録をふり返る
topics.description: Review a kanji lookup log to decide which route worked best: reading, component, radical, stroke count, or context.
evidence: Record lookup route, found reading, and meaning for an unknown kanji word.
Reflect on which lookup route was efficient and why.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to complete and review a lookup log for two kanji words from a grade-level text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji morpheme field web
topics.japaneseName: 漢字の意味単位を分野でつなぐ
topics.description: Group grade 5 kanji and compounds by recurring meaning units across subject-area vocabulary.
evidence: Build a web that links kanji morphemes to at least three related compounds.
Explain how a shared kanji narrows the meaning field of unfamiliar vocabulary.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to organize new grade 5 compounds into a morpheme field web and explain one unfamiliar word.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Paraphrasing jukugo from context
topics.japaneseName: 文脈を使って熟語を言い換える
topics.description: Use sentence context and compound structure to paraphrase abstract jukugo in accessible Japanese.
evidence: Identify the relation between parts of a compound before paraphrasing it.
Revise a paraphrase so it fits the sentence context.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student abstract compounds from a school text and ask for context-fit paraphrases.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reason notes for homophone kanji choice
topics.japaneseName: 同音異義語の漢字選択理由メモ
topics.description: Record a meaning-based reason when selecting among same-sound kanji words in reading or writing.
evidence: List candidate homophone spellings and discard ones that do not match the meaning.
Write a short reason note for the selected kanji word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose homophone kanji in context and write the reason for each choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Specialized subject words built from kanji
topics.japaneseName: 漢字から専門的な教科語をとらえる
topics.description: Interpret specialized social studies, science, and arts vocabulary by analyzing kanji meanings and word families.
evidence: Sort subject-area words by shared kanji or morpheme.
Use the shared kanji to explain a new subject word in context.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to sort subject-area terms and explain how kanji meanings support understanding.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Basic source reliability check
topics.japaneseName: 資料の信頼性を基本項目で確かめる
topics.description: Check author, date, purpose, and evidence type before using a source in an inquiry task.
evidence: Record author or publisher, date, and purpose for a source.
Explain one reason the source is useful or limited for the inquiry question.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student two sources and ask for a brief reliability checklist before note-taking.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sorting notes from multiple sources
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料のメモを観点別に分ける
topics.description: Sort note cards from several sources by inquiry subtopic while keeping source labels visible.
evidence: Group notes by subtopic rather than by source order alone.
Keep a source label attached to each note after sorting.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to sort mixed source notes into subtopic groups with source labels preserved.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Pairing diagram and prose evidence
topics.japaneseName: 図表と文章の根拠を組み合わせる
topics.description: Use a diagram, table, or graph together with prose evidence to support an explanatory statement.
evidence: Select one visual detail and one sentence that support the same point.
Explain how the visual detail changes or strengthens the prose evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to support an explanation with one diagram detail and one prose detail.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Section plan for an information report
topics.japaneseName: 説明的な報告文の構成計画
topics.description: Plan report sections from inquiry notes so each section has a focused question, evidence, and heading.
evidence: Create section headings that match grouped inquiry notes.
Assign relevant evidence to each section before drafting.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to turn source notes into a report section plan with headings and evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Summary with limited evidence selection
topics.japaneseName: 要点と根拠をしぼった要約
topics.description: Write a concise summary by naming the main point and limiting evidence to the most relevant details.
evidence: State the main point without copying whole paragraphs.
Choose only the evidence needed for the summary purpose.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to summarize an informational passage using a fixed evidence limit.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Marking author claim and reasons
topics.japaneseName: 筆者の主張と理由を印で分ける
topics.description: Mark an author claim, reasons, examples, and evidence in an argumentative or explanatory text.
evidence: Distinguish a claim from supporting reasons or examples.
Annotate the text with consistent marks for claim and support.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a short opinion text for claim, reasons, and evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing source purpose and audience
topics.japaneseName: 資料の目的と読者を比べる
topics.description: Compare sources on the same topic by asking who they address and what purpose each one serves.
evidence: Identify likely audience and purpose for two sources.
Explain how purpose affects what evidence is included or omitted.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two topic-related sources by audience, purpose, and evidence choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Data commentary sentence language
topics.japaneseName: 資料を説明する文の言葉
topics.description: Use precise comparative and causal language to comment on data from tables, graphs, or survey results.
evidence: Write a sentence that names a data pattern without exaggeration.
Choose a connective that shows comparison, increase, decrease, or cause.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a simple graph and ask for two accurate commentary sentences.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listing counterreasons for an opinion
topics.japaneseName: 意見に対する反対理由の整理
topics.description: Generate possible counterreasons before drafting an opinion paragraph so the argument anticipates readers.
evidence: List at least two plausible counterreasons to a stated opinion.
Select one counterreason that needs an evidence-based response.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to list counterreasons for an opinion and choose one to answer.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Ordering evidence in an argument paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 意見文の段落で根拠を順序立てる
topics.description: Order reasons, examples, and evidence in a paragraph so each sentence advances the claim.
evidence: Place a claim, reason, evidence, and comment in a coherent order.
Revise a paragraph where evidence appears before its reason is clear.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to reorder mixed argument sentences and explain the chosen order.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revision check for evidence gaps
topics.japaneseName: 根拠の不足を見つける推敲
topics.description: Use revision criteria to find claims, explanations, or examples that need stronger evidence.
evidence: Mark a place where a reader may ask why or how.
Add or replace evidence to close the gap without changing the topic.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an opinion paragraph by identifying and fixing one evidence gap.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Peer review questions about evidence
topics.japaneseName: 根拠を問う相互評価の質問
topics.description: Ask peer review questions that help a writer clarify evidence, examples, or reader assumptions.
evidence: Frame a question that targets a claim-evidence connection.
Use the peer answer to suggest a specific revision.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask pairs to exchange opinion drafts and produce one evidence-focused revision question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Building on evidence in discussion
