topics.japaneseName: 話し合いで根拠を受けて発言をつなぐ
topics.description: Respond to a peer by citing or extending the evidence already introduced in the discussion.
evidence: Refer to a previous speaker or source before adding a comment.
Extend the discussion with a new reason, example, or question.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a group discussion, ask the student to build on a peer comment using source evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presenting a source with a visual cue
topics.japaneseName: 資料を示しながら発表する
topics.description: Introduce source evidence during a short presentation by pointing listeners to a visual cue or handout location.
evidence: Name the source and direct listeners to the relevant visual part.
Explain the evidence without reading the visual text verbatim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to present one source finding while indicating the visual cue used as evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracking motif evidence in literature
topics.japaneseName: 物語のくり返し表現を根拠として追う
topics.description: Track repeated images, words, or situations as motif evidence for interpreting a literary work.
evidence: Collect repeated details with page or scene references.
Explain how the repeated detail develops a theme or character understanding.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to track a repeated detail across scenes and explain its interpretive value.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Character viewpoint quote note
topics.japaneseName: 登場人物の視点を引用で確かめる
topics.description: Use short quoted evidence to infer how a character sees an event or relationship.
evidence: Select a brief quote that reveals a character viewpoint.
Explain the viewpoint in the student own words.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose one quote and explain the character viewpoint it supports.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Line break effects in poems
topics.japaneseName: 詩の行分けが生む効果
topics.description: Interpret how a poem line break, spacing, or repetition shapes rhythm, emphasis, or image.
evidence: Identify a line break or spacing choice that affects reading.
Explain the effect on rhythm, emphasis, or imagery.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two line-break versions of a poem excerpt and explain the effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revising a theme response with evidence
topics.japaneseName: 主題への考えを根拠で推敲する
topics.description: Revise a literary theme response so the interpretation, evidence, and personal response remain connected.
evidence: Mark where the interpretation is unsupported or too general.
Add textual evidence that directly supports the theme response.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a theme response by adding or replacing one evidence sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reflecting on inquiry source choices
topics.japaneseName: 調査で選んだ資料をふり返る
topics.description: Reflect on why particular sources were selected and how the choices affected the final explanation.
evidence: Name one source that was especially useful and why.
Identify one source limitation or missing perspective for future inquiry.
topics.assessmentPrompt: After an inquiry report, ask the student to reflect on source choice and missing information.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading stamina strategy plan
topics.japaneseName: 長い文章を読み通す方略計画
topics.description: Plan and monitor strategies for reading longer literary or informational texts over multiple sessions.
evidence: Set checkpoints for summarizing or question marking during a long reading task.
Adjust the plan when attention, vocabulary, or evidence tracking breaks down.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to plan checkpoints for a long text and reflect on which strategy helped.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Abstract noun field map with kanji
topics.japaneseName: 漢字で抽象名詞の意味領域を整理する
topics.description: Map abstract grade 6 kanji nouns by meaning field, register, and subject context.
evidence: Group abstract nouns by shared kanji or semantic field.
Explain how field and register shape interpretation in a school text.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build a meaning-field map for abstract nouns from a grade 6 text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Noting exceptions in jukugo relations
topics.japaneseName: 熟語の関係と例外を記録する
topics.description: Recognize common compound relation patterns while recording exceptions that require context or dictionary checking.
evidence: Label the likely relation between kanji in a compound.
Flag an exception where the relation label is insufficient.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to label compound relations and write an exception note for one difficult compound.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji register in news and public texts
topics.japaneseName: ニュースや公共文での漢字の硬さを読む
topics.description: Use kanji compound density, furigana, and word choice to infer register in news or public information texts.
evidence: Identify compounds that make a text sound formal or public.
Rewrite one sentence for a younger audience while preserving meaning.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare a news-style sentence with a classroom rewrite and explain register changes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Vocabulary gradation for stance
topics.japaneseName: 立場の強さを表す語彙の段階
topics.description: Choose stance words with appropriate strength, certainty, and register for explanation or argument.
evidence: Order stance expressions from tentative to strong.
Replace an overstrong or vague stance word for the writing purpose.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise stance words in opinion sentences for strength and audience.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source claim and evidence matrix
topics.japaneseName: 資料ごとの主張と根拠の表
topics.description: Build a matrix comparing each source claim, evidence type, and relevance to the inquiry question.
evidence: Record claim, evidence, and relevance for each source in a matrix.
Use the matrix to choose which sources should appear in a synthesis.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to make a source matrix and justify one source inclusion or exclusion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Matrix for comparing author viewpoints
topics.japaneseName: 筆者の立場を比べる表
topics.description: Compare author viewpoints across sources using topic, stance, evidence, and omitted perspectives.
evidence: Summarize each author viewpoint in a compact matrix cell.
Identify one agreement, disagreement, or omission across sources.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare three source viewpoints and explain the most important difference.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Integrating table, graph, and prose evidence
topics.japaneseName: 表・グラフ・文章を合わせて読む
topics.description: Integrate numerical or visual evidence with prose claims when explaining an issue.
evidence: Match a table or graph pattern to a prose claim.
State what the visual evidence adds that the prose alone does not show.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to explain a claim using a graph, a table entry, and a prose detail.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source synthesis paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料を統合した段落
topics.description: Write a paragraph that combines evidence from multiple sources without listing sources mechanically.
evidence: Use at least two sources in one coherent paragraph.
Explain the relationship between the sources rather than placing facts side by side.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to draft a paragraph that synthesizes two source notes into one explanation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Responding to a counterclaim with evidence
topics.japaneseName: 反論に根拠で答える
topics.description: Acknowledge a counterclaim and respond with relevant evidence while maintaining a clear position.
evidence: State the counterclaim fairly before responding.
Use evidence that directly addresses the counterclaim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to add a counterclaim-response sentence pair to an opinion paragraph.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Matching argument introduction and conclusion
topics.japaneseName: 意見文の導入と結論を対応させる
topics.description: Draft and revise introduction and conclusion pairs so the final position returns to the opening issue.
evidence: Identify whether the conclusion answers the issue introduced at the beginning.
Revise either the introduction or conclusion to align stance and scope.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an argument opening and conclusion so they answer the same issue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Cohesion map for an explanatory report
topics.japaneseName: 説明文全体のつながりを図で見る
topics.description: Map section order, headings, connectives, and recurring key terms to check report cohesion.
evidence: Mark how each section connects to the next section.
Revise a heading, connective, or key term to strengthen cohesion.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to draw a cohesion map for a report draft and revise one weak connection.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revision for register and public audience
topics.japaneseName: 読み手に合わせた語調の推敲
topics.description: Revise vocabulary, sentence endings, and evidence explanations for a public or unfamiliar audience.
evidence: Identify wording that is too casual, too vague, or too specialized for the audience.
Revise while preserving the evidence and stance of the draft.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a public-facing paragraph for register, clarity, and evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Facilitation roles in discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いを進める役割発言
topics.description: Use facilitator, summarizer, evidence-checker, or timekeeper language to keep a discussion productive.
evidence: Make a role-based comment that advances the discussion process.
Invite quieter evidence or unresolved viewpoints into the discussion.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Assign discussion roles and ask the student to use one role comment with evidence reference.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Summary notes during debate
topics.japaneseName: 討論を聞きながら要点を記録する
topics.description: Take listening notes that distinguish claims, evidence, counterarguments, and unresolved issues during debate.
evidence: Record at least one claim, one evidence item, and one counterargument while listening.
Use the notes to summarize the strongest point on each side.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a short debate, ask the student to complete a listening summary note and report both sides.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence response in presentation Q&A
topics.japaneseName: 発表後の質問に根拠で答える
topics.description: Answer presentation questions by returning to source evidence, data, or limits of the inquiry.
evidence: Restate the question before answering with relevant evidence.
Acknowledge when the available evidence does not fully answer the question.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to answer two audience questions using evidence from the presentation sources.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening notes for counterarguments
topics.japaneseName: 反対意見を聞き取るメモ
topics.description: Listen for opposing reasons and evidence so a later response does not distort the other position.
evidence: Record the counterargument in neutral wording.
Separate the speaker claim from the evidence or example offered.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to listen to a peer argument and write a neutral counterargument note.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Analyzing shifts in literary perspective
topics.japaneseName: 物語の視点の変化を読む
topics.description: Analyze how a shift in narrator, character viewpoint, or time changes interpretation of a literary scene.
evidence: Identify the place where perspective or time frame shifts.
Explain how the shift changes what the reader knows or feels.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to mark a perspective shift and explain its effect on interpretation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing themes across genres
topics.japaneseName: ジャンルをこえて主題を比べる
topics.description: Compare how a story, poem, essay, or classical passage develops a related theme differently.
evidence: State a shared theme in two works from different genres.
Use genre features to explain a difference in theme development.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare a theme in two genres with one evidence point from each text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical phrase in modern context
topics.japaneseName: 古典の表現を現代の文脈で味わう
topics.description: Connect a short classical phrase, proverb, or fixed expression to modern meaning, rhythm, and cultural context.
evidence: Paraphrase a short classical phrase in modern Japanese.
Explain one cultural or expressive effect retained in the phrase.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to paraphrase a classical phrase and explain where it could be used today.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Register case study across situations
topics.japaneseName: 場面による言葉づかいの事例分析
topics.description: Compare how vocabulary, sentence endings, and politeness shift across home, school, public, and media contexts.
evidence: Identify register differences across two situation examples.
Explain how audience and purpose justify the language choice.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two versions of the same message and explain register differences.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Research portfolio source log
topics.japaneseName: 調査ポートフォリオの資料記録
topics.description: Maintain a source log that records inquiry question, source choice, note category, and revision use.
evidence: Record source metadata and the part of the final product each source supports.
Use the log to explain how source use changed during the inquiry.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to present a source log from a research portfolio and explain one source decision.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Transfer goal for middle-school language study
topics.japaneseName: 中学校につなげる国語学習の目標
topics.description: Set a transfer goal for applying evidence reading, source use, vocabulary analysis, or discussion strategies in lower secondary school.
evidence: Name one strategy from elementary Japanese that should transfer to middle school.
Set a concrete next-use goal with task context and success criterion.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a transfer goal linking an elementary strategy to a middle-school task.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Author intent in media messages
topics.japaneseName: メディアの発信意図を読む
topics.description: Infer how layout, evidence choice, headline wording, and audience assumptions reveal intent in a media message.
evidence: Identify headline, image, layout, or evidence choices that guide reader interpretation.
Explain the likely intent without claiming certainty beyond the evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze a media message for author intent using two visible features.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Final elementary language portfolio
topics.japaneseName: 小学校国語学習の総合ポートフォリオ
topics.description: Curate evidence from reading, writing, speaking, listening, kanji, and source-use work to show end-of-elementary readiness.
evidence: Select artifacts that represent several Japanese-language domains.
Write a reflection that names strengths, evidence, and next learning goals.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to curate a final elementary Japanese portfolio with evidence-based reflection.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Modifier scope diagram in secondary prose
topics.japaneseName: 中学校文章の修飾範囲図
topics.description: Diagram how modifiers, predicates, and sentence units connect in explanatory prose so meaning boundaries are visible.
evidence: Mark the word or phrase each modifier attaches to in a sentence.
Explain how changing the boundary would change the meaning.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to diagram modifier scope in two explanatory sentences and explain one ambiguous boundary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Function-word effects in explanatory text
topics.japaneseName: 説明文における助詞・助動詞の働き
topics.description: Interpret how particles, auxiliaries, and sentence endings signal relation, emphasis, certainty, or stance in explanatory text.
evidence: Identify a function word that changes relation or stance.
Paraphrase the sentence while preserving the function-word effect.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate particles or auxiliaries in an explanatory paragraph and explain their effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reason note for source reliability
topics.japaneseName: 資料の信頼性を理由づけるメモ
topics.description: Record a source reliability judgment with concrete reasons from author, date, purpose, evidence, and publication context.
evidence: Judge a source as usable, limited, or questionable for a task.
Write a reason note that cites a visible source feature.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student two sources and ask for reliability judgments with reason notes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing gist across multiple sources
topics.japaneseName: 複数資料の要旨を比べる
topics.description: Compare the gist of two or more sources on the same topic before forming a personal stance.
evidence: Write a one-sentence gist for each source.
Name one agreement, difference, or missing point across sources.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare gists from two short sources and identify a difference in focus.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Quote and paraphrase boundary in explanation
topics.japaneseName: 説明文での引用と言い換えの境界
topics.description: Use quotation, paraphrase, and student commentary in an explanatory paragraph without blurring source wording and own wording.
evidence: Mark quoted wording and paraphrased wording separately.
Add student commentary that explains why the evidence matters.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an explanatory paragraph so quote, paraphrase, and commentary are separated.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence-commentary revision loop
topics.japaneseName: 根拠と考察を往復する推敲
topics.description: Revise a paragraph by checking whether each evidence sentence is followed by commentary that connects it to the claim.
evidence: Locate a place where evidence lacks commentary.
Add or revise commentary so the evidence supports the paragraph claim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a paragraph by adding commentary after unsupported evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Turning a seminar question into a claim
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いの問いを主張へつなげる
topics.description: Use discussion questions to form tentative claims that can be tested against textual or source evidence.
evidence: Convert a genuine question into a tentative claim.
Name the evidence needed to test that claim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During seminar notes, ask the student to turn one question into a testable claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presenting an explanation from source cards
topics.japaneseName: 資料カードを使った説明発表
topics.description: Present a short explanation using source cards in an order that makes claim, evidence, and commentary clear to listeners.
evidence: Use source cards without reading them verbatim.
Signal the relation between source evidence and the explanation claim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to present a two-minute explanation using three prepared source cards.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Literary viewpoint evidence table
topics.japaneseName: 文学作品の視点を根拠で整理する表
topics.description: Collect evidence for narrator or character viewpoint in a table that separates scene, wording, inference, and uncertainty.
evidence: Record scene and wording evidence for a viewpoint inference.
Mark uncertainty when evidence permits more than one interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to complete a viewpoint evidence table for a narrative scene.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Poetry rhythm and image response
topics.japaneseName: 詩のリズムとイメージへの応答
topics.description: Explain how rhythm, line breaks, repetition, and images shape a personal but text-grounded response to a poem.
evidence: Identify one rhythm or line feature and one image.
Explain how the feature shapes the reader response.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a poem and write a short response connecting rhythm and image.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Recitation note for a classical phrase
topics.japaneseName: 古典表現の音読メモ
topics.description: Use recitation, rhythm, and gloss notes to make an initial connection between a short classical phrase and modern Japanese meaning.
evidence: Read a short classical phrase aloud with marked rhythm or pause.
Add a gloss note that connects the phrase to a modern expression or idea.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to recite a short classical phrase and annotate rhythm plus modern meaning.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Moving from personal to academic abstract terms
topics.japaneseName: 身近な言葉から抽象語へ広げる
topics.description: Bridge everyday descriptions to academic abstract nouns used in explanation, interpretation, and argument.
evidence: Match everyday expressions to more precise abstract terms.
Use the abstract term accurately in an explanatory sentence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise everyday wording into academic abstract terms in a short explanation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Checking headline against evidence
topics.japaneseName: 見出しと根拠の対応を確かめる
topics.description: Check whether a headline fairly represents the evidence, claim, and scope of the underlying text.
evidence: Compare headline wording with at least two evidence details.
Identify exaggeration, narrowing, or missing context when present.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give the student a headline and short article, then ask whether the headline fits the evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening summary of source and speaker stance
topics.japaneseName: 資料と話し手の立場を聞き分ける要約
topics.description: While listening to a short explanation, distinguish source information from the speaker stance or interpretation.
evidence: Summarize the source information stated by the speaker.
Separately summarize the speaker stance or evaluation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to listen to a source-based talk and write separate source and stance summaries.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revision goal and evidence log
topics.japaneseName: 推敲目標と根拠の記録
topics.description: Set a revision goal, record the evidence used to revise, and describe the before-and-after change.
evidence: Name one revision goal tied to evidence, audience, or clarity.
Record the change made and the reason for the change.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to keep a revision log for one paragraph revision with goal, evidence, and result.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Academic word families from kanji morphemes
topics.japaneseName: 漢字の意味単位から学術語の語族を作る
topics.description: Build academic word families by tracing recurring kanji morphemes across school texts and disciplines.
evidence: Group at least four words by a shared kanji morpheme.
Explain how the shared morpheme contributes to meaning in a new word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create a kanji morpheme word-family map from a secondary-school text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Nominalization effect in argument
topics.japaneseName: 論説における名詞化の効果
topics.description: Analyze how turning verbs or clauses into noun-like expressions compresses events, causes, and evaluations in argumentative prose, and how that compression changes responsibility, emphasis, or evidence flow.
evidence: Identify nominalized clauses or phrases in an argument.
Rewrite one nominalization into a fuller clause and compare the effect.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze nominalization in an argument paragraph and explain its effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reading hedging and modality in argument
topics.japaneseName: 論説の留保表現とモダリティを読む
topics.description: Interpret how hedging, certainty, obligation, and possibility shape the strength of an argument.
evidence: Mark expressions that weaken, strengthen, or qualify a claim.
Explain how modality affects the reader judgment of the claim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate hedging and modality in a short argument and explain stance strength.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Warrant and evidence diagram
topics.japaneseName: 理由づけと根拠の関係図
topics.description: Diagram the reasoning that links evidence to claim, making implicit warrants visible.
evidence: Separate claim, evidence, and warrant in an argument.
Explain when the warrant is assumed rather than directly stated.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to create a claim-evidence-warrant diagram for an argument paragraph.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rating evidence strength for counterclaims
topics.japaneseName: 反論の根拠の強さを評価する
topics.description: Evaluate how strongly counterclaim evidence challenges the original claim and what response it requires.
evidence: Rate counterclaim evidence as weak, partial, strong, or decisive with a reason.
Name the kind of response the evidence requires.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to evaluate counterclaim evidence and recommend a rebuttal strategy.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source bias and purpose matrix
topics.japaneseName: 資料の偏りと目的を整理する表
topics.description: Compare sources by purpose, likely audience, evidence type, omission, and potential bias.
evidence: Complete a source matrix with purpose, audience, evidence, and possible bias.
Explain how source purpose affects whether the source can support a claim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give three sources on one issue and ask the student to complete a bias-purpose matrix.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Auditing infographic data claims
topics.japaneseName: インフォグラフィックのデータ主張を点検する
topics.description: Audit whether an infographic claim follows from its data scale, labels, source, and visual emphasis.
evidence: Identify the data point, scale, or label that supports the claim.
Flag a visual emphasis that may exaggerate or narrow the data meaning.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to audit an infographic and judge whether the visual claim is fair.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Debate case outline
topics.japaneseName: ディベート立論の構成メモ
topics.description: Prepare a debate case with claim, definitions, reasons, evidence, anticipated counterclaim, and closing emphasis.
evidence: Create a case outline with claim, definitions, reasons, evidence, and counterclaim anticipation.
Explain why the evidence order supports the debate goal.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to prepare a debate case outline for a school issue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Facilitator protocol for returning to evidence
topics.japaneseName: 根拠へ戻す司会進行の手順
topics.description: Use facilitator language to return a discussion from opinion drift to source evidence and shared criteria.
evidence: Interrupt opinion drift with a neutral return-to-evidence move.
Name the shared criterion or source that should guide the next turn.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During a discussion role-play, ask the student to use a facilitator move that returns to evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparing interpretations of a literary symbol
topics.japaneseName: 文学的象徴の解釈を比べる
topics.description: Compare two interpretations of a symbol or motif by testing each against textual evidence.
evidence: State two possible interpretations of a symbol.
Use evidence to explain which interpretation is stronger or how both can coexist.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two interpretations of a symbol and defend the stronger reading.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Short essay on a poem image network
topics.japaneseName: 詩のイメージ連鎖についての小論
topics.description: Write a short literary essay explaining how repeated or contrasting images shape the meaning of a poem.
evidence: Identify an image network or contrast in the poem.
Write an evidence-based explanation of its effect.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a short essay about an image network in a poem.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical and modern honorific register comparison
topics.japaneseName: 古典と現代の敬語・文体比較
topics.description: Compare how relationship, respect, and narration are signaled in classical quotations and modern Japanese.
evidence: Identify an honorific or register cue in a classical or modern passage.
Explain how relationship or viewpoint changes across the comparison.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare a short classical quotation with a modern paraphrase for register effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Digital quotation and citation note
topics.japaneseName: デジタル引用と出典メモ
topics.description: Copy, quote, and cite digital source text accurately while preserving punctuation, line breaks, source title, and URL or access note.
evidence: Transcribe a short digital quotation accurately.
Record source title and access information in a consistent note.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to quote a digital source and prepare an accurate citation note.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Academic vocabulary collocation field
topics.japaneseName: 学術語の共起関係を整理する
topics.description: Notice which abstract nouns, verbs, and modifiers commonly combine in school argument and explanation.
evidence: Collect collocations around one abstract academic term.
Use a suitable collocation in an explanation or argument sentence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build a collocation field for an academic term and use it in writing.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Oral rebuttal listening notes
topics.japaneseName: 反論を聞き取るメモ
topics.description: Listen to a rebuttal and record claim, evidence, warrant, concession, and unanswered point.
evidence: Record the main rebuttal claim and supporting evidence.
Identify one unanswered point or weak link in the rebuttal.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to listen to a rebuttal and complete structured listening notes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Counterevidence entry in an inquiry log
topics.japaneseName: 探究ログに反証を記録する
topics.description: Record counterevidence in an inquiry log and reflect on whether it changes the question, claim, or source plan.
evidence: Add a counterevidence entry with source and relevance.
Decide whether the counterevidence changes the inquiry direction.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to update an inquiry log after finding counterevidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revision for audience knowledge gaps
topics.japaneseName: 読み手の知識不足を補う推敲
topics.description: Revise explanation by identifying where the intended audience lacks background knowledge, examples, or definitions.
evidence: Mark a place where the reader may lack background knowledge.
Add a definition, example, or bridge sentence that closes the gap.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a paragraph for an audience unfamiliar with the topic.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Argument thesis scope and limits
topics.japaneseName: 論説文の主張の範囲と限界
topics.description: Set a thesis that is specific enough to argue and honest about scope, exceptions, and limits.
evidence: Revise an overbroad thesis into a specific arguable claim.
State one limit, condition, or exception for the claim.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to narrow a broad thesis and add a scope limitation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Counterargument and rebuttal chain
topics.japaneseName: 反論と再反論の連鎖
topics.description: Build an argument chain that fairly states a counterargument, evaluates it, and responds with relevant evidence.
evidence: State a counterargument without distortion.
Write a rebuttal that directly addresses the counterargument evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to add a counterargument-rebuttal chain to an essay draft.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence hierarchy source matrix
topics.japaneseName: 根拠の序列を示す資料表
topics.description: Rank sources and evidence by relevance, reliability, specificity, and ability to answer the argument question.
evidence: Create a matrix that ranks evidence by several criteria.
Justify why one source should receive more weight than another.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to rank sources for an argument essay and justify the top evidence choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparative synthesis of media framing
topics.japaneseName: メディア・フレーミングの比較総合
topics.description: Synthesize how different media sources frame the same issue through headline, evidence, image, order, and omissions.
evidence: Compare at least two media frames using visible text or layout features.
Write a synthesis that names framing differences and their likely effect.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare media coverage of one issue and synthesize framing differences.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Designing cross-examination questions
topics.japaneseName: 反対尋問の質問設計
topics.description: Design cross-examination questions that reveal definition problems, evidence gaps, warrant weaknesses, or tradeoffs.
evidence: Write questions targeting different weaknesses in an opposing case.
Predict what answer would help the student rebuttal.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to prepare cross-examination questions from an opposing debate case.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using debate flow notes for reflection
topics.japaneseName: ディベートフローを使った省察
topics.description: Use debate flow notes to reflect on which claims were answered, dropped, strengthened, or weakened.
evidence: Mark claims on a flow sheet as answered, dropped, strengthened, or weakened.
Write a reflection about one strategic choice for future debate or writing.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to review a debate flow sheet and write a strategy reflection.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kobun auxiliary function card
topics.japaneseName: 古文助動詞の機能カード
topics.description: Create function cards for supported classical auxiliaries that connect form, meaning, example, and modern paraphrase.
evidence: Record form, meaning, and example for a classical auxiliary.
Use the card to paraphrase a supported classical phrase.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to make a function card for a classical auxiliary and use it in paraphrase.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanbun readback and paraphrase check
topics.japaneseName: 漢文の読み下しと現代語訳の確認
topics.description: Use basic kanbun marks and phrase order to check whether a readback and modern paraphrase preserve meaning.
evidence: Follow return marks or phrase-order support to produce a readback.
Check the modern paraphrase against the phrase-order meaning.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to read back a supported kanbun phrase and compare it with a modern paraphrase.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical-modern allusion response
topics.japaneseName: 古典と現代文の引用・典故への応答
topics.description: Respond to a modern text that alludes to classical language by explaining source, effect, and contemporary meaning.
evidence: Identify a classical allusion or inherited expression in a modern text.
Explain what effect the allusion adds to the modern text.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a response explaining a classical allusion in a modern passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Counterinterpretation of narrative irony
topics.japaneseName: 物語のアイロニーに対する別解釈
topics.description: Develop and evaluate a counterinterpretation of narrative irony, distance, or unreliable viewpoint.
evidence: State an initial interpretation and a plausible counterinterpretation.
Use textual evidence to compare the two readings.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare interpretations of irony or narrative distance in a literary passage.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Rewriting stance and modality in policy argument
topics.japaneseName: 政策論説の立場とモダリティを書き換える
topics.description: Revise stance and modality in a policy argument so certainty, obligation, and recommendation match the evidence.
evidence: Identify stance and modality expressions in a policy argument.
Rewrite one sentence to calibrate certainty or recommendation strength.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise stance and modality in a policy argument excerpt.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source integration with citation and commentary
topics.japaneseName: 出典表示と考察を伴う資料統合
topics.description: Integrate source evidence into an argument with citation, paraphrase or quote boundary, and commentary tied to thesis.
evidence: Use citation information and source boundary markers accurately.
Connect the source evidence to the thesis with commentary.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an essay paragraph for citation, source boundary, and commentary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: High-school language transfer portfolio
topics.japaneseName: 高校国語へつなぐ学習ポートフォリオ
topics.description: Curate lower-secondary evidence that shows readiness to transfer argument, media, classical, literary, and discussion strategies into high school.
evidence: Select artifacts from several lower-secondary Japanese domains.
Write a transfer reflection naming strengths, evidence, and next high-school goals.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to assemble a lower-secondary portfolio and write a transfer reflection.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Stance map for media speech listening
topics.japaneseName: メディア発話の立場マップ
topics.description: Map speaker stance shifts, evidence, framing, and implied audience while listening to media speech.
evidence: Mark where a speaker stance shifts or intensifies.
Connect the shift to evidence, framing, or audience appeal.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to listen to media speech and draw a stance map with supporting cues.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Mini editorial on a public issue
topics.japaneseName: 公共的な課題についての小論説
topics.description: Write a concise editorial on a public issue with claim, evidence, counterpoint, stance, and audience-aware closing.
evidence: Use a clear claim, evidence, and counterpoint in a short editorial.
Close with stance language calibrated for a public audience.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a short editorial on a public issue using at least two sources.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Refining a research question from source gaps
topics.japaneseName: 資料の不足から探究課題を焦点化する
topics.description: Refine a research question when sources reveal missing evidence, unclear terms, or too broad a scope.
evidence: Identify a source gap, unclear term, or scope problem.
Rewrite the research question so it can be answered with stronger evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a research question after reviewing a source-gap matrix.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Data claim and graph rhetoric
topics.japaneseName: データ主張と図表のレトリック
topics.description: Analyze how a public text uses graphs, percentages, samples, and captions to support or steer a claim.
evidence: Identify the stated claim and the data element used as support.
Explain one way graph design, scale, or caption wording affects interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a graph-supported article and explain how the visual presentation supports the claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source genre reliability heuristic
topics.japaneseName: 情報源ジャンルの信頼性判断
topics.description: Use genre, authorship, evidence type, update context, and audience purpose to make a first-pass reliability judgment.
evidence: Classify a source by genre and expected evidence conventions.
State a provisional reliability judgment with a reason and a needed follow-up check.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give three source excerpts on one issue and ask the student to rank first-pass reliability with stated checks.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Stakeholder vocabulary in public issues
topics.japaneseName: 公共的課題における利害関係者語彙
topics.description: Use stakeholder, policy, impact, and responsibility vocabulary to describe public issues without flattening positions.
evidence: Name stakeholder groups and the terms used to describe their positions.
Revise a summary so stakeholder terms are specific and neutral.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to rewrite a public-issue summary with more precise stakeholder vocabulary.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Digital comment register analysis
topics.japaneseName: デジタルコメントの文体分析
topics.description: Analyze how sentence endings, modality, emoji-like markers, quotation, and address terms shift stance in public digital comments.
evidence: Identify register cues in a comment thread or public response.
Explain how changing one cue changes the stance or audience relationship.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate digital comments for register cues and revise one for a different audience.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Infographic caption and source reading
topics.japaneseName: インフォグラフィックの注記・出典読解
topics.description: Read infographic captions, notes, source labels, time frames, and exclusions before accepting the displayed message.
evidence: Point to source, time frame, and exclusion notes in an infographic.
Explain how one note limits the claim a reader can make.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to inspect an infographic and write the strongest justified claim plus one limitation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Media framing comparison grid
topics.japaneseName: メディアのフレーミング比較表
topics.description: Compare how multiple media sources frame the same issue through headline, ordering, quoted voices, omitted context, and data selection.
evidence: Fill a grid comparing headline, first paragraph, quoted voices, and source basis.
Summarize the main framing difference without judging from preference alone.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two reports on one issue and explain the framing difference using the grid.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Inquiry interview question design
topics.japaneseName: 探究インタビューの質問設計
topics.description: Design interview questions that gather experience, evidence, reasons, and constraints without leading the respondent.
evidence: Separate neutral opening questions from follow-up evidence questions.
Revise a leading question into a neutral inquiry question.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to draft an interview protocol for a public issue and mark each question purpose.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence tags in inquiry notes
topics.japaneseName: 探究ノートの根拠タグ
topics.description: Tag inquiry notes by claim, source type, stakeholder, evidence kind, uncertainty, and possible use in writing.
evidence: Apply consistent tags to notes from at least two sources.
Use tags to find a gap or overrepresented viewpoint.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give mixed inquiry notes and ask the student to tag, sort, and identify one evidence gap.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Public issue explainer paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 公共的課題の解説段落
topics.description: Write a compact explanatory paragraph that defines the issue, names stakeholders, and distinguishes fact from stance.
evidence: Open with a neutral issue definition and stakeholder frame.
Separate factual background from one contested point.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a public-issue explainer paragraph from source notes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparative news summary paragraph
topics.japaneseName: ニュース比較要約段落
topics.description: Summarize how two news or commentary texts differ in topic focus, source voice, and implied audience.
evidence: State the common issue and each source focus in one paragraph.
Use comparison language without overstating bias or intent.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a comparative summary of two reports and underline comparison language.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence slide oral briefing
topics.japaneseName: 根拠スライドによる口頭ブリーフィング
topics.description: Present a short oral briefing that uses one slide or handout to show claim, evidence, source, and limitation.
evidence: Use a visual display that makes the claim-evidence relationship visible.
State the source and limitation aloud during the briefing.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to deliver a two-minute briefing from one evidence slide and answer a source question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Listening for interview stance shifts
topics.japaneseName: インタビューにおける立場の変化を聞き取る
topics.description: Track when a speaker shifts from experience, to opinion, to uncertainty, to recommendation during an interview.
evidence: Mark stance shifts in an interview transcript or audio notes.
Explain how a follow-up question prompted or clarified the shift.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play an interview excerpt and ask the student to map stance shifts with evidence from phrasing.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Literary setting as social lens
topics.japaneseName: 社会を見るレンズとしての場面設定
topics.description: Interpret how setting, time period, local language, and social situation shape character choices in modern literature.
evidence: Identify setting details that carry social or historical meaning.
Connect one setting detail to a character choice or conflict.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a passage for setting details and explain their interpretive effect.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical phrase in present-day quotation
topics.japaneseName: 現代文に引用された古典表現
topics.description: Explain how a classical phrase quoted in a contemporary text adds authority, irony, shared memory, or cultural resonance.
evidence: Identify the quoted or alluded classical phrase and its immediate context.
Explain its present-day rhetorical effect using evidence from surrounding sentences.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to analyze a contemporary passage that quotes or echoes a classical expression.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Source trail bias reflection note
topics.japaneseName: 情報探索経路の偏りを振り返るノート
topics.description: Reflect on how search terms, platform ranking, source genre, and personal interest shaped the sources found during inquiry.
evidence: Describe the path used to find sources, including search terms or entry points.
Identify one likely bias in the source trail and one corrective search action.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to submit a source-trail reflection after an inquiry search cycle.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Joyo kanji in specialized public terms
topics.japaneseName: 専門的公共語彙の常用漢字確認
topics.description: Check specialized public terms for standard kanji form, kana use, readability, and audience fit in high-school inquiry writing.
evidence: Verify kanji form or kana spelling of specialized terms with a reference.
Revise one term choice for readability without losing precision.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to proofread a public-issue paragraph for specialized terms and justify one notation choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Causal claim evidence chain
topics.japaneseName: 因果主張の根拠連鎖
topics.description: Trace how a logical text moves from observed evidence to causal explanation and where the chain may be incomplete.
evidence: Separate correlation, sequence, mechanism, and causal conclusion in a passage.
Identify a missing link or alternative cause that would need evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map a causal argument and mark one unsupported link.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Analogy argument test
topics.japaneseName: 類推論証の検証
topics.description: Evaluate an analogy by identifying shared structure, relevant difference, hidden assumption, and limits of transfer.
evidence: State what two cases are being compared and why.
Name one relevant difference that weakens or limits the analogy.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to evaluate an analogy in critical prose and write a limitation note.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Statistical language caveats
topics.japaneseName: 統計表現の留保
topics.description: Use caveat expressions to avoid overstating statistical tendencies, survey results, averages, or correlations.
evidence: Identify claim words that overgeneralize from statistical evidence.
Revise the claim with an appropriate caveat or scope marker.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise statistical claims using scope and caveat expressions.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Secondary source credibility triage
topics.japaneseName: 二次資料の信頼性トリアージ
topics.description: Quickly triage secondary interpretations by author expertise, evidence visibility, citation trail, publication context, and acknowledged limits.
evidence: Score or rank secondary sources using explicit credibility criteria.
Select one source to use and one to reject or reserve, with reasons.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to triage three secondary sources before literature-review writing.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Literature review gap statement
topics.japaneseName: 先行研究レビューの課題文
topics.description: Write a gap statement that explains what prior sources cover, what remains unresolved, and why the new inquiry matters.
evidence: Group prior sources by position or method rather than by list order.
Write a gap sentence that follows from the grouped source comparison.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to turn source notes into a literature-review paragraph ending with a gap statement.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Critical lens limitation note
topics.japaneseName: 批評観点の限界メモ
topics.description: Apply a literary-critical lens while also naming what the lens highlights, ignores, or may distort.
evidence: State the interpretive lens and the evidence it foregrounds.
Write a limitation note naming what the lens leaves underexplained.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to apply one critical lens to a passage and add a limitation note.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Narrator reliability evidence test
topics.japaneseName: 語り手の信頼性を根拠で検証する
topics.description: Evaluate narrator reliability using contradiction, distance, omission, diction, focalization, and response from other voices.
evidence: Identify textual evidence that supports or weakens narrator reliability.
Explain how reliability affects interpretation of an event or symbol.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to make and test a narrator-reliability claim with passage evidence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Poetic diction pattern analysis
topics.japaneseName: 詩的語彙パターンの分析
topics.description: Analyze repeated diction, sound, image fields, and line breaks as patterns that develop tone or theme.
evidence: Mark a repeated diction or image pattern across a poem or lyrical prose passage.
Explain how the pattern changes tone, theme, or speaker position.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate a poem for diction patterns and write a short interpretive claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical auxiliary meaning disambiguation
topics.japaneseName: 古典助動詞の意味識別
topics.description: Use form, connection, context, and modern paraphrase to distinguish likely meanings of common classical auxiliary expressions.
evidence: Identify the form and connection of a classical auxiliary in context.
Choose a meaning and justify it with context and paraphrase.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to annotate several auxiliary examples and justify one disambiguation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Tracing kanbun allusion
topics.japaneseName: 漢文的典故をたどる
topics.description: Trace a kanbun-derived allusion or compressed expression from source meaning to its function in a Japanese text.
evidence: Identify the allusive expression and its likely source context or meaning.
Explain how the allusion compresses evaluation, authority, or irony.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to explain one kanbun-derived allusion in a modern or classical-linked text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Seminar response synthesis
topics.japaneseName: ゼミ型討議の応答統合
topics.description: Synthesize several peer comments into a response that advances the shared interpretive or logical question.
evidence: Group peer comments by claim, evidence, or unresolved question.
Respond by connecting at least two comments to a new synthesis or next question.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During seminar discussion, ask the student to synthesize prior comments before adding a position.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Peer critique evidence standard
topics.japaneseName: 相互批評における根拠基準
topics.description: Evaluate peer presentations by whether evidence is relevant, sufficient, cited, interpreted, and connected to the claim.
evidence: Use a critique note to separate evidence presence from evidence quality.
Give a question or suggestion tied to a specific evidence standard.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to critique a peer presentation using explicit evidence standards.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Academic quotation signal phrases
topics.japaneseName: 学術的引用の導入表現
topics.description: Use signal phrases, punctuation, and citation formatting to integrate quotation without blurring source voice and writer voice.
evidence: Choose a signal phrase that matches the source role in the paragraph.
Format a quotation with correct punctuation, attribution, and follow-up interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a paragraph so quotations are introduced, cited, and interpreted clearly.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Methodology register choice
topics.japaneseName: 方法説明のレジスター選択
topics.description: Choose method and limitation vocabulary that fits academic explanation, public explanation, or literary-critical discussion.
evidence: Identify method terms whose register fits or misfits the intended audience.
Rewrite a method sentence for a different audience while preserving precision.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to adapt method-language sentences for academic and public readers.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Revision from counterargument
topics.japaneseName: 反論を受けた論旨修正
topics.description: Revise a claim after considering counterargument by narrowing scope, adding evidence, or changing the warrant.
evidence: Identify whether the counterargument challenges scope, evidence, or reasoning.
Revise the thesis or warrant in response without abandoning the main inquiry.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a draft thesis and counterargument, then ask the student to revise the claim and explain the revision.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Interpretation confidence rubric
topics.japaneseName: 解釈の確信度ルーブリック
topics.description: Rate interpretive confidence by evidence density, alternative explanations, source support, and remaining uncertainty.
evidence: Assign a confidence level to an interpretation and cite criteria.
Name what evidence would increase or reduce confidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to reflect on an interpretation using a confidence rubric after peer discussion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Capstone question narrowing criteria
topics.japaneseName: 卒業探究問いの焦点化基準
topics.description: Narrow a capstone research question by scope, evidence access, audience value, conceptual clarity, and feasible method.
evidence: Apply criteria to reject or revise overbroad research questions.
State the narrowed question and what evidence it requires.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise three candidate capstone questions and justify the strongest one.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Section transition logic control
topics.japaneseName: 章節間の論理接続制御
topics.description: Control transitions between sections by naming how the next section extends, limits, contrasts, or applies the previous claim.
evidence: Classify section transitions by logical function.
Rewrite a weak transition so the reader can predict the next section purpose.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise section-opening transitions in a capstone draft.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Synthesis matrix to thesis conversion
topics.japaneseName: 統合表から論旨へ変換する
topics.description: Convert a source synthesis matrix into a defensible thesis by identifying convergence, conflict, gap, and usable evidence.
evidence: Mark convergence, conflict, and gap columns in a synthesis matrix.
Write a thesis that reflects the strongest pattern in the matrix.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to turn a source matrix into a thesis and explain which pattern drove the claim.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Policy recommendation feasibility language
topics.japaneseName: 政策提言の実現可能性表現
topics.description: Write recommendations with feasibility, trade-off, stakeholder, timeline, and limitation language rather than ideal claims alone.
evidence: Identify feasibility and trade-off language in a recommendation.
Revise a recommendation so it states constraint and stakeholder impact.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a policy recommendation paragraph for feasibility and limitation language.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Public abstract for a research argument
topics.japaneseName: 研究論旨の公共向け要旨
topics.description: Write a public-facing abstract that states issue, method, finding, implication, and limitation in accessible language.
evidence: Condense a research argument into issue-method-finding-implication sequence.
Revise specialized terms for accessibility while retaining accuracy.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a public abstract from a capstone argument and annotate adaptation choices.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Slide evidence density control
topics.japaneseName: スライドの根拠密度調整
topics.description: Design presentation slides that balance readable density, evidence transparency, source labeling, and oral explanation.
evidence: Reduce overpacked evidence into visible claim, data, source, and takeaway.
Explain what is shown on the slide and what is reserved for speech.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise an overfull research slide and present the revised evidence orally.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Symposium equity moderation tracker
topics.japaneseName: シンポジウム発言配分トラッカー
topics.description: Track participation, question types, stakeholder perspectives, and unresolved issues while moderating a symposium.
evidence: Use a tracker to monitor speaker balance and perspective coverage.
Intervene with a question or summary that improves equity or focus.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student moderator to submit tracking notes and explain one moderation decision.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Q&A evidence retrieval notes
topics.japaneseName: 質疑応答の根拠検索ノート
topics.description: Prepare notes that allow quick retrieval of evidence, source, caveat, and follow-up offer during public Q&A.
evidence: Organize notes by likely question type and evidence location.
Answer a question with evidence and a clear caveat or follow-up boundary.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to answer Q&A using retrieval notes after a research presentation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Comparative literary-cultural introduction
topics.japaneseName: 比較文学・言語文化論の導入
topics.description: Frame a comparative literary-cultural interpretation by naming texts, context, comparison axis, and interpretive stakes.
evidence: Write an introduction that names the comparison axis and why it matters.
Avoid plot summary as the main organizing principle.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to revise a comparative essay introduction for axis and stakes.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Reception-history caveat paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 受容史の留保段落
topics.description: Explain how a classical or canonical expression has been received differently while noting limits in available evidence.
evidence: Describe at least two reception contexts or interpretations.
Add a caveat about source range, period, or interpretive uncertainty.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to write a reception-history paragraph with one explicit caveat.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Classical quotation translation note
topics.japaneseName: 古典引用の現代語訳注
topics.description: Attach a concise translation note to a classical quotation that preserves key wording, cultural force, and interpretive uncertainty.
evidence: Translate or paraphrase the quotation with a note on a key expression.
Explain why the quotation matters to the modern argument or interpretation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to add translation notes to quoted classical evidence in a comparative essay.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Language identity case comparison
topics.japaneseName: 言語アイデンティティ事例比較
topics.description: Compare cases where register, dialect, classical reference, or public language choice shapes identity and social positioning.
evidence: Identify comparable language-identity choices in two cases.
Explain how context changes the meaning or value of the language choice.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to compare two language-identity cases using evidence from texts or discourse.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Accessible copyedit checklist
topics.japaneseName: アクセシブルな校閲チェックリスト
topics.description: Copyedit public-facing work for readability, notation consistency, source visibility, captions, headings, and accessibility.
evidence: Use a checklist to find issues in notation, captioning, heading, or source labels.
Revise at least two issues and explain the reader need behind each fix.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to copyedit a publication-ready page with an accessibility checklist.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Bibliography annotation quality audit
topics.japaneseName: 参考文献注記の質点検
topics.description: Audit bibliography annotations for source role, credibility reason, evidence contribution, and limits in the final argument.
evidence: Check whether each annotation states source role and evidence contribution.
Revise weak annotations to include credibility and limitation language.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to audit final bibliography annotations and revise two weak entries.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Portfolio evidence transfer map
topics.japaneseName: ポートフォリオ根拠の転移マップ
topics.description: Map portfolio artifacts to transferable language abilities such as synthesis, interpretation, source ethics, and public presentation.
evidence: Select artifacts and label the transferable ability each demonstrates.
Write a transfer claim that names a future reading, writing, or speaking context.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build a portfolio transfer map with evidence from multiple genres.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Publication transparency note
topics.japaneseName: 発信の透明性注記
topics.description: Write a transparency note that discloses source basis, quotation boundaries, interview consent, editing role, and remaining limitations.
evidence: State source basis and quotation or interview boundaries clearly.
Name one limitation or editorial choice that readers should know.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to add a transparency note to a publication-ready inquiry product.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Asking who and why questions about a story
topics.japaneseName: 物語に「だれ」「どうして」を問う
topics.description: Ask simple who, what, and why questions about a story using picture, action, and sentence clues.
evidence: Ask a who or why question that fits the story events.
Point to a picture or sentence clue that motivated the question.
topics.assessmentPrompt: After shared reading, ask the student to write or say one who/why question with a clue.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Echo reading in short phrase groups
topics.japaneseName: 短いまとまりでまねて読む
topics.description: Echo-read short phrase groups with attention to sound, pause, and sentence ending.
evidence: Repeat a phrase group with similar rhythm and pause after a model.
Keep the phrase together instead of reading each kana separately.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Model a short sentence and ask the student to echo-read it in phrase groups.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Speech bubble and character voice
topics.japaneseName: ふきだしと登場人物の声
topics.description: Connect direct speech or speech bubbles to the character who is speaking and the feeling in the voice.
evidence: Identify who is speaking in a speech bubble or quoted line.
Read the line with a voice that matches a likely feeling.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show a story page with speech and ask the student to identify the speaker and read the line.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Sorting feeling words by intensity
topics.japaneseName: 気持ちを表す言葉の強さを分ける
topics.description: Sort simple feeling words by type and strength to describe story characters or personal experiences more precisely.
evidence: Group feeling words that are similar in meaning.
Choose a stronger or weaker feeling word for a story moment.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give feeling-word cards and ask the student to sort them, then choose one for a character.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Adding where or when to a sentence
topics.japaneseName: 文に「どこで」「いつ」を足す
topics.description: Expand a simple sentence by adding where or when information while keeping the sentence readable.
evidence: Add one where or when phrase to a simple sentence.
Read the revised sentence aloud and confirm it still makes sense.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a short sentence and ask the student to add where or when information.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Question from a picture sequence
topics.japaneseName: 絵の順序から問いを作る
topics.description: Use a sequence of pictures to ask what happened first, next, or last in an informational or procedural text.
evidence: Ask a sequence question based on pictures.
Answer by pointing to the picture that supplies the clue.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Show a picture sequence and ask the student to make and answer one order question.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing a book for a purpose
topics.japaneseName: 目的に合う本を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose a story, picture book, or information book based on whether the purpose is enjoyment, answer-finding, or sharing.
evidence: State a simple purpose for choosing a book.
Explain one feature that makes the book fit that purpose.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose from several books and explain the purpose for the choice.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Pair response sentence stems
topics.japaneseName: ペアで返す文の型
topics.description: Use simple response stems such as agreeing, asking one question, or adding one remembered detail in pair talk.
evidence: Respond to a partner with a complete simple sentence.
Use one stem for agreeing, asking, or adding a detail.
topics.assessmentPrompt: During pair talk, ask the student to respond using one practiced response stem.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Turning a caption detail into a sentence
topics.japaneseName: キャプションの細部を文にする
topics.description: Turn one observed picture or caption detail into a sentence with a clear who or what.
evidence: Choose one visible or captioned detail.
Write a sentence that names who or what the detail is about.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to choose a detail from a picture caption and write one sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Punctuation as voice marks
topics.japaneseName: 声を表す句読点
topics.description: Notice that punctuation can signal stopping, asking, surprise, or quotation in early reading and copying.
evidence: Identify a punctuation mark and the reading voice it suggests.
Read or copy a sentence with a pause or intonation matching the mark.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to read sentences with different punctuation and explain the voice change.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Noticing a repeated kana error pattern
topics.japaneseName: かなのまちがいのくせに気づく
topics.description: Notice a repeated kana copying or spelling error and name a concrete check to use next time.
evidence: Find one repeated kana error in recent work.
Name a check such as shape, mora count, small kana, or sound mark.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to review two short copied words or sentences and identify one repeated kana check.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Oral reason for story enjoyment
topics.japaneseName: 好きなところの理由を話す
topics.description: Give a simple oral reason for liking a story part, character, word, or picture.
evidence: Name a favorite part of a story or poem.
Give one reason using a story detail, picture, or feeling word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: After reading, ask the student to say a favorite part and one reason.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Dialogue speaker and action map
topics.japaneseName: 会話の話し手と行動マップ
topics.description: Map who speaks, what they do, and how the speech changes the event in a story scene.
evidence: Match each quoted line to the speaker and action.
Explain how one line changes the scene or relationship.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to map speakers and actions in a short dialogue scene.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Oral rehearsal before a paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 段落を書く前に口でたしかめる
topics.description: Orally rehearse a short paragraph by naming the topic, two details, and ending before writing.
evidence: Say the topic and two details before drafting.
Use the oral plan to write an ordered short paragraph.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to orally rehearse a paragraph plan, then write from it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Character feeling and evidence pair
topics.japaneseName: 人物の気持ちと根拠を組にする
topics.description: Pair a character feeling word with a sentence, action, or dialogue clue from the story.
evidence: Choose a feeling word for a character at one moment.
Point to an action or line that supports the feeling word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to complete feeling-evidence pairs for a story scene.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Simple survey graph sentence
topics.japaneseName: かんたんなアンケート表を文にする
topics.description: Read a simple class survey table or graph and turn one result into a factual sentence.
evidence: Identify the largest, smallest, or selected result in a simple table.
Write or say one sentence that reports the result accurately.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a class survey chart and ask the student to write one factual result sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Using a dictionary example sentence
topics.japaneseName: 辞書の例文を使う
topics.description: Use a dictionary or class word list example sentence to check how an unfamiliar word can fit in a sentence.
evidence: Find an example sentence for a word.
Explain how the example helps choose or use the word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to find an example sentence and use it to write a new sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Polite request role-play
topics.japaneseName: ていねいなお願いのロールプレイ
topics.description: Use polite classroom request expressions and adjust wording for teacher, friend, or group work.
evidence: Make a request using a polite expression suited to the listener.
Explain how the wording would change for a close classmate.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to role-play requesting help from a teacher and a classmate.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Feeling-word choice in revision
topics.japaneseName: 気持ちの言葉を選び直す
topics.description: Revise a sentence by replacing a broad feeling word with a more fitting feeling or intensity word.
evidence: Identify a broad or vague feeling word in writing.
Replace it with a word that better matches the event.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a short recount sentence and ask the student to revise the feeling word.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Instruction listening checkbox
topics.japaneseName: 指示を聞くチェックボックス
topics.description: Use a simple checkbox or mental checklist to track order, materials, place, and action in spoken instructions.
evidence: Record or repeat the key parts of a multi-step instruction.
Ask a clarifying question when one part is missing.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a three-part classroom instruction and ask the student to check off the needed parts.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Signal words for explanation steps
topics.japaneseName: 説明の順序を表す言葉
topics.description: Notice and use words that signal first, next, finally, reason, or result in explanations.
evidence: Circle step or reason signal words in an explanation.
Use two signal words to explain a simple process.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to explain a familiar process using order or reason signal words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Clean copy reader check
topics.japaneseName: 清書を読み手のために確認する
topics.description: Check a clean copy for readable spacing, line breaks, corrections, and punctuation before sharing.
evidence: Use a checklist to inspect a clean copy for reader readability.
Fix at least one spacing, punctuation, or line-break issue.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to clean-copy a paragraph and complete a reader-check checklist.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Retelling with time connectors
topics.japaneseName: 時間のつなぎ言葉で話を伝える
topics.description: Retell a story or experience using time connectors to preserve event order.
evidence: Use at least two time-order words in a retell.
Keep events in story or experience order.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to retell a short event using time-order words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kind partner feedback after reading
topics.japaneseName: 音読後のやさしい相互助言
topics.description: Give a partner one kind, specific comment about reading fluency, voice, or meaning after oral reading.
evidence: Name one thing a partner did clearly while reading.
Offer one specific next try without personal criticism.
topics.assessmentPrompt: After partner reading, ask the student to give one praise and one specific suggestion.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji word web for person, place, and action
topics.japaneseName: 人・場所・動きの漢字言葉ウェブ
topics.description: Build a small word web around familiar kanji by grouping words for people, places, actions, or nature.
evidence: Place kanji words into a semantic web with at least two branches.
Add one new related word and explain the connection.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to build a kanji word web from familiar grade 1-2 kanji words.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Diagram label as claim support
topics.japaneseName: 図のラベルを説明の根拠にする
topics.description: Use diagram labels and arrows as evidence that supports a sentence-level explanation in an informational text.
evidence: Match a diagram label or arrow to a sentence in the text.
Explain how the visual detail supports the written explanation.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to connect a diagram label to a sentence and explain the support relation.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Splitting copied words and own words in notes
topics.japaneseName: 写した言葉と自分の言葉を分ける
topics.description: Separate copied source wording from own-word notes when collecting information from one source.
evidence: Mark copied words and own wording differently in a note.
Explain why the two kinds of note should not be mixed.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a short source and ask the student to make notes with copied words clearly marked.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing connectors inside a paragraph
topics.japaneseName: 段落の中のつなぎ言葉を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose connectors that show addition, example, reason, or result inside an explanatory paragraph.
evidence: Classify the relationship between two sentences in a paragraph.
Choose a connector that fits the relationship.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to insert connectors into a paragraph and explain the relationship each shows.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Evidence role in small-group discussion
topics.japaneseName: 話し合いで根拠を出す役割
topics.description: Use a discussion role to contribute one text clue, note, example, or question that supports group thinking.
evidence: Contribute one evidence-based comment while in a group role.
Connect the comment to the group question or task.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Assign group roles and ask the student to contribute one evidence-based turn.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Kanji compound field note in science or social studies
topics.japaneseName: 理科・社会の熟語フィールドメモ
topics.description: Record kanji compounds from school subjects by field, meaning clue, reading, and example sentence.
evidence: Record a subject-area compound with reading and meaning clue.
Use the compound in a sentence or explanation from the subject field.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to collect three subject-area compounds and write one example sentence.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Interview note main point
topics.japaneseName: インタビューの要点メモ
topics.description: Listen to a short interview or explanation and write a note that captures the main point and one detail.
evidence: Write a main-point note from spoken information.
Add one supporting detail without trying to copy every word.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Play or conduct a short interview and ask the student to record main point plus detail.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Opinion, reason, example chain
topics.japaneseName: 意見・理由・例のつながり
topics.description: Write a short opinion paragraph that links opinion, reason, and example in a clear chain.
evidence: Identify opinion, reason, and example in a paragraph plan.
Write the chain without leaving the example disconnected.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to draft an opinion paragraph and label opinion, reason, and example.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Poem image line sketch
topics.japaneseName: 詩の言葉から情景を描く
topics.description: Use words from a poem to sketch or describe an image, then explain which words created the image.
evidence: Choose poem words that create a clear image or sound.
Explain the sketch or description using those words as evidence.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to sketch an image from a poem and cite the words that guided it.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Choosing a dictionary sense from context
topics.japaneseName: 文脈に合う辞書の意味を選ぶ
topics.description: Choose the dictionary meaning that fits the sentence context when a word has more than one meaning.
evidence: Compare at least two dictionary senses for one word.
Choose the sense that fits the sentence and explain why.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Give a common polysemous word in context and ask the student to choose the fitting dictionary sense.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: Presentation board and speech balance
topics.japaneseName: 発表資料と話す内容のバランス
topics.description: Balance short visible words or pictures on a presentation board with fuller oral explanation.
evidence: Choose short labels or key words for a visual aid.
Explain orally without reading every word from the board.
topics.assessmentPrompt: Ask the student to present a short report using a visual aid with limited text.

topics.subject: Japanese
topics.name: One-target paragraph revision
topics.japaneseName: 段落を一つの目標で推敲する
